options: Inspect the graph with CLI tools like `ros2 node info`, `ros2 topic echo`, and `ros2 doctor`, since it's almost always faster than debugging blind
Immediately attach a debugger directly to the node's process
Rewrite the suspected node from scratch
Restart the entire workspace build before investigating further
explanation: Unit 9 lists as a habit worth keeping: 'Inspect the graph with CLI tools (ros2 node info, ros2 topic echo, ros2 doctor) before reading code, when something misbehaves — it's almost always faster than debugging blind.'

question: According to Unit 1, FlexBE (Flexible Behavior Engine) is built on top of which existing ROS library?
options: MoveIt
SMACH
Nav2
rosbridge
explanation: The unit states: "FlexBE (Flexible Behavior Engine) is a behavior engine built on top of SMACH, ROS's state-machine library."

question: Per the unit's description of the FlexBE package layout, which package contains the runtime that actually executes behaviors on the robot itself?
options: flexbe_core
flexbe_onboard
flexbe_app
flexbe_states
explanation: The unit lists "flexbe_onboard (the runtime that executes behaviors on the robot)", distinct from flexbe_core (base classes) and flexbe_app (editor/OCS). "flexbe_states" is not one of the three named packages.

question: In the "Setting expectations" section, when does the unit say plain code is often simpler than using FlexBE?
options: When the robot has more than two or three states worth chaining together
When a human supervisor needs to be in the loop
For a single, straightforward action
When using the Operator Control Station
explanation: The text says FlexBE is most valuable with more states or a human supervisor in the loop, but "For a single 'go pick this up' action, plain code is often simpler."

question: What is the purpose of the "--" and "<=" annotations inside a FlexBE state's docstring, as explained in Unit 2?
options: They are purely human-readable comments with no functional effect
The FlexBE editor parses them to auto-generate the parameter and outcome sockets shown in the GUI
They define the ROS topic names the state will subscribe to
They specify which unit test assertions must pass before the state compiles
explanation: The unit states: "The docstring's -- and <= annotations aren't decoration — the FlexBE editor parses them to auto-generate the parameter and outcome sockets you'll see in the GUI."

question: In the WaitForSecondsState example, what does it mean when execute() returns nothing (None)?
options: The state throws a runtime exception
The behavior immediately transitions to its 'finished' outcome
The state stays active and execute() will be called again on the next cycle
The state machine aborts and requires operator restart
explanation: The code comment explicitly notes: "Returning nothing keeps the state active for another cycle," meaning execute() will be invoked again.

question: How does Unit 2 say states should share data with each other, such as one state's computed target pose being consumed by the next state?
options: Through global Python variables shared across state instances
Via a userdata object with explicitly declared input_keys/output_keys
Only by publishing and subscribing to ROS topics
By writing to a shared class-level static dictionary
explanation: The unit explains FlexBE (via SMACH) shares data "with a userdata object and explicit input_keys/output_keys on each state, so data flow is declared, not implicit global state."

question: According to Unit 3, why are actions preferred over services for most FlexBE state work like navigation or manipulation?
options: Actions are simpler to implement in Python than services
Services are request/response and block until done with no way to cancel, while actions provide goal/feedback/result plus cancellation
Services are deprecated in modern ROS distributions
Actions don't require running an action server on the robot
explanation: The unit states: "Services are request/response and block until done — fine for quick queries, bad for anything that takes seconds or needs cancelling. Actions give you goal, feedback, and result, plus the ability to cancel a running goal."

question: What role does ProxyActionClient play in a FlexBE Actionlib-based state?
options: It replaces the need for a separate ROS action server entirely
It wraps the action client lifecycle (connecting, sending goals, checking status) so the state code can focus on what goal to send and how to interpret the result
It is the visual component the operator uses in the OCS to pause and resume behaviors
It automatically converts any ROS service call into an action call
explanation: The unit describes ProxyActionClient as "a wrapper that manages the action client lifecycle for you (connecting, sending goals, checking status) so your state code stays focused on what goal to send and how to interpret the result."

question: Per Unit 3's description of autonomy levels, at which level must the operator manually approve every single state transition from the OCS before it fires?
options: Full autonomy
High autonomy
Low autonomy
None (autonomy disabled)
explanation: The unit states: "At low autonomy, the operator must manually approve every state transition from the OCS; at full autonomy, the behavior runs end to end without intervention."

question: Why does Unit 4 argue for unit testing FlexBE states in isolation rather than only testing whole behaviors on the robot?
options: ROS requires every state class to have an associated unit test file before it can be launched
Because if the only test is running the whole behavior on the robot, a bug in state logic and a flaky action server look identical from the outside, and every debug cycle costs a robot/simulator boot
Because states cannot be instantiated outside of a running ROS master
Because SMACH state machines cannot be paused for manual inspection
explanation: The unit explains: "If your only test is 'run the whole behavior on the robot,' a bug in state logic and a flaky action server look identical from the outside, and every debug cycle costs you a robot (or simulator) boot."

question: How does Unit 4 recommend testing an Actionlib-backed state such as SendTrajectoryState?
options: Launch a real action server in a dedicated test rig for every test run
Use unittest.mock to patch/mock ProxyActionClient so you can assert on the state's decision logic without any ROS infrastructure running
Skip unit tests for Actionlib states and rely solely on integration testing on the robot
Replace ProxyActionClient with a plain ROS service call during tests
explanation: The unit shows mocking ProxyActionClient with unittest.mock's patch/MagicMock, noting this "pins down... your state's job is to translate action results into outcomes" independent of a real action server.

question: In the "Where this fits in the workflow" section, what kind of outcome paths does the unit warn are easy to forget when writing a unit test for each outcome a state can produce?
options: Success paths
Failure/timeout paths
userdata read paths
on_enter paths
explanation: The unit recommends writing "a unit test for each outcome it can produce (including failure/timeout paths, which are easy to forget)."

question: In the TakeoffAndLandBehavior state machine built in Unit 5, where does the TAKEOFF state transition on a 'failed' outcome?
options: LAND
mission_complete
mission_failed
It retries TAKEOFF automatically
explanation: The wiring code shows TAKEOFF's transitions as {'done': 'LAND', 'failed': 'mission_failed'}, and the diagram confirms TAKEOFF --failed--> mission_failed.

question: Why does Unit 5 suggest requiring manual operator confirmation for the TAKEOFF → LAND transition instead of letting it run fully autonomously?
options: Because TakeoffState is described as inherently unreliable in the unit
Because a human should be able to visually confirm the drone is stably hovering before FlexBE proceeds to land
Because LandState has not yet been unit tested at that point
Because ROS requires manual confirmation for any action with 'failed' as a possible outcome
explanation: The unit states: "Takeoff succeeding safely is exactly the kind of moment you might want a human to visually confirm ('yes, it's stably hovering, go ahead and land') before FlexBE proceeds — set that transition to require manual confirmation."

question: Which real-world drone software stack does Unit 5 mention as an example that exposes takeoff/landing functionality similar to the project's generic action interface?
options: PX4 native SDK
MAVROS-based stacks (CommandTOL-style)
Nav2
MoveIt
explanation: The unit notes: "This mirrors real drone packages (e.g. MAVROS-based stacks expose CommandTOL-style services/actions for takeoff and landing), but the state logic below is written against a generic action interface."

question: According to the unit, why is the Parrot AR Drone specifically called out as a good teaching platform?
options: It has the highest payload capacity of any consumer quadrotor
It's controllable entirely over Wi-Fi via a lightweight UDP/AT-command protocol, with no flight controller SDK lock-in
It ships pre-installed with RTABMap and MoveIt configured
It requires a proprietary flight controller SDK that simplifies driver development
explanation: The unit states the AR Drone is 'controllable entirely over Wi-Fi using a lightweight UDP/AT-command protocol, with no flight controller SDK lock-in,' which lets a ROS driver node expose takeoff/land/velocity control as ordinary topics/services instead of a proprietary API.

question: Per the unit, what is the AR Drone's downward-facing camera specifically used for?
options: Loop closure detection in the SLAM pipeline
Optical-flow-based velocity estimation
Building the 2D occupancy grid for navigation
Detecting collisions for MoveIt's planning scene
explanation: The unit explicitly says the airframe carries 'a downward-facing camera (used for optical-flow-based velocity estimation)', distinct from the front camera used for mapping.

question: What is the correct order of the four software-stack layers the course builds toward, as described in the unit?
options: MoveIt planning, RTABMap mapping, simulator, driver/bridge node
Driver/bridge node, simulator, RTABMap SLAM/mapping package, MoveIt motion planning framework
Simulator, MoveIt, RTABMap, driver/bridge node
RTABMap, driver/bridge node, MoveIt, simulator
explanation: The unit lists the layers in this exact order: (1) driver/bridge node, (2) simulator, (3) RTABMap SLAM/mapping package, (4) MoveIt motion planning framework, mirroring the course's unit progression.

question: Why does the unit say a velocity command sent while the drone is still reported as 'landed' typically has no visible effect?
options: The cmd_vel topic doesn't exist until after takeoff
Such a command is usually just ignored by the driver in that state, a common source of beginner confusion
The simulator automatically restarts on any command received while landed
The Twist message type changes format after takeoff
explanation: The unit warns: 'a command sent while the drone is still "landed" is usually just ignored, which is a common source of "my code doesn't work" confusion for beginners.'

question: Per the unit, what happens when you set the angular.x or angular.y fields of a Twist command sent to a stabilized quadrotor?
options: They directly command precise roll and pitch angles
They are usually ignored; the flight controller handles roll/pitch internally to achieve the requested linear velocity
They control the downward camera's tilt
They set the drone's yaw rotation rate
explanation: The unit states angular.x/angular.y are 'usually ignored on a stabilized drone; the flight controller handles roll/pitch internally to achieve the requested linear velocity' — yaw is angular.z instead.

question: Why does the unit recommend always publishing an explicit zero-Twist command to stop the drone, instead of relying solely on auto-hover behavior?
options: Auto-hover is not implemented in ROS 2 drivers at all
A zero-Twist message is required to trigger the landing sequence
Auto-hover shouldn't be your only safety net; an explicit stop plus a watchdog timer guards against a stalled control loop
The driver otherwise ignores all Twist messages except zero-valued ones
explanation: The unit says a drone with no incoming commands 'should be programmed... to auto-hover, not drift — but don't rely on that as your only safety net. Always publish an explicit zero-Twist... and consider a watchdog timer.'

question: What does a 'loop closure' specifically refer to in RTABMap's mapping process, as described in the unit?
options: Terminating the ROS launch file once mapping finishes
Recognizing that the drone has returned to a previously visited place, which triggers correction of accumulated drift
Closing the feedback loop between odometry and cmd_vel during navigation
The occupancy grid reaching full resolution
explanation: The unit defines loop closure as comparing new frames 'against previously visited places to detect... "I've been here before,"' which then 'corrects accumulated drift across the whole graph.'

question: According to the unit, what is the single most common practical failure mode in drone SLAM with RTABMap?
options: The onboard simulator running out of memory during long flights
Flying too fast, in textureless environments, or with motion blur, producing few or no loop-closure matches so the map drifts uncorrected
The 2D occupancy grid exceeding the memory nav2 can handle
angular.z yaw commands interfering with visual odometry calculations
explanation: The unit states: 'Fly too fast, in too textureless an environment..., or with too much motion blur, and you'll get few or no candidate matches, and the map will drift uncorrected. This is the single most common practical failure mode in drone SLAM.'

question: Why, according to the unit, can RTABMap's 3D map be flattened into a 2D occupancy grid for navigation?
options: Because nav2/move_base cannot process any 3D data
Because most indoor navigation tasks only need to avoid obstacles at flight altitude, so the 3D point cloud can be projected into a height band
Because RTABMap only ever builds 2D maps in the first place
Because the AR Drone's front camera cannot capture depth information
explanation: The unit explains: 'for most indoor navigation tasks you only need to avoid obstacles at flight altitude — so RTABMap can project its 3D point cloud into a 2D occupancy grid at a given height band.'

question: How does MoveIt model a drone's motion, given that a drone has no joints like a robot arm's kinematic chain?
options: As a fixed end-effector with zero degrees of freedom
As a virtual floating joint: a single 6-DOF joint (3 translation + 3 rotation) connecting the world frame to the drone's body frame
As a chain of revolute joints, one per rotor
As a prismatic joint permitting translation only, with rotation handled separately
explanation: The unit states MoveIt models the drone's pose 'as a virtual floating joint: a 6-degree-of-freedom joint (3 translation + 3 rotation) connecting a fixed "world" frame to the drone's body frame,' with the planning group being just that single joint.

question: Why can't MoveIt's execute() call be used directly to fly the drone along a planned trajectory, per the unit?
options: execute() is only supported on ROS 1, and this course targets ROS 2
execute() assumes a joint trajectory controller, which a drone doesn't have, so waypoints must instead be converted into cmd_vel velocity commands
OMPL never produces a time-parameterized trajectory for execute() to use
The planning scene lacks the obstacle data execute() requires to run
explanation: The unit explains: "MoveIt's execute() call assumes a joint trajectory controller — which a drone doesn't have. Instead, you convert the planned sequence of poses into the velocity commands from Unit 2."

question: What typically serves as the obstacle representation in MoveIt's planning scene when planning a drone's 3D path, according to the unit?
options: A flat 2D occupancy grid identical to the one built in Unit 3
A manually authored list of GPS waypoint coordinates
Typically the same map (or an octomap derived from it) already built with RTABMap in Unit 3, loaded as collision geometry
A live, unmapped feed straight from the drone's front camera with no prior SLAM step
explanation: The unit states the planning scene 'is typically the same map (or an octomap derived from it) you already built with RTABMap in Unit 3, loaded as collision geometry instead of a flat occupancy grid.'

question: According to Unit 1, why is this course specifically focused on C++ rather than Python?
options: Because ROS 1 only supports C++
Because most performance-sensitive robotics code (drivers, control loops, perception pipelines) is written in C++, while Python is more common for glue code
Because C++ source compiles faster than Python scripts run
Because ROS 2 deprecated Python client library support
explanation: The unit states you're learning C++ ROS specifically because performance-sensitive code is written in C++, while Python is used more for glue code and quick scripts.

question: In the unit's grouping of the course by ROS communication pattern, what do Units 6-7 (Services) represent?
options: The fire-and-forget, many-to-many streaming pattern
The request/response pattern used for one-off, synchronous calls
The pattern for long-running, cancellable, feedback-producing goals
The class-based approach to structuring nodes
explanation: The unit explicitly labels Services (Units 6-7) as "the request/response pattern used for one-off, synchronous calls," distinct from Topics' streaming pattern and Actions' long-running goal pattern.

question: Per Unit 1, ROS bugs are overwhelmingly caused by what, rather than algorithmic mistakes?
options: Memory leaks in the C++ code
"Wiring" issues — wrong topic name, message type mismatch, or a node never spinning
Compiler version incompatibilities
Race conditions across multiple threads
explanation: The unit says ROS bugs are overwhelmingly about wiring (wrong topic name, message type mismatch, node never spinning) rather than algorithmic mistakes, which is why you build the instinct by running and watching code fail.

question: In Unit 2's four-layer breakdown of ROS, what is Layer 1, the computation graph?
options: Packages and the workspace on disk
The live, running system of nodes exchanging data over topics, services, actions, plus parameters
The CLI and GUI tools used to inspect the system
The docs.ros.org reference pages describing each package's interface
explanation: The unit defines Layer 1 as the live, running graph of nodes exchanging data via topics, services, and actions, plus parameters — the layer you reason about when asking what the robot is doing right now.

question: According to Unit 2, what is true about the relationship between source code and the resulting computation graph?
options: They are identical — the graph is just a visual representation of the source files
Two different pieces of source code can produce the same graph shape, and the same source code can produce different graphs depending on remapping and launch configuration
The graph is fixed at compile time and cannot vary based on launch configuration
The filesystem layer directly produces the graph without any compilation or execution step
explanation: The unit explicitly states this layer is separate from the code on disk: two different source codebases can produce the same graph shape, and the same source can produce different graphs depending on remapping and launch configuration.

question: In the package structure Unit 2 describes, what does package.xml declare?
options: The node's runtime topic names
Dependencies for the package
The generated C++ header locations
The RViz display configuration
explanation: The unit describes package.xml as the manifest file declaring dependencies, while CMakeLists.txt is the build description for C++.

question: Per Unit 3, what role does roscore (the ROS 1 master) play, and what happens without it?
options: It renders 3D sensor data; without it RViz cannot start
It's a registry every node talks to first to find out who else exists; forgetting to start it is the most common "why isn't anything connecting" beginner bug
It compiles the workspace source packages; without it colcon build fails
It stores custom message definitions; without it .msg files can't be generated
explanation: The unit states the master is the registry nodes talk to first, and forgetting to start it is the single most common connectivity bug for beginners. ROS 2 has no equivalent process since discovery happens via DDS.

question: According to Unit 3, what happens if a node calls rclcpp::init() and creates a node, but the code never calls rclcpp::spin()?
options: It processes exactly one callback and then exits cleanly
It returns immediately after init and never actually does any work, since spin() is what dispatches timers, subscriptions, and service calls
It blocks indefinitely waiting for a parameter to be declared
It automatically spins on a background thread created by rclcpp::init()
explanation: The unit states plainly: "a node that returns immediately after init never actually does any work" — spin() is what keeps the process alive and dispatching callbacks.

question: Per Unit 3, what does the ROS_DOMAIN_ID environment variable do in ROS 2?
options: It sets the default logging verbosity for all nodes
It isolates separate ROS networks running on the same physical network so your graph doesn't accidentally talk to a neighbor's
It defines which build tool (colcon vs catkin) is used
It specifies the workspace overlay search path for source packages
explanation: The unit describes ROS_DOMAIN_ID as a domain ID that isolates separate ROS networks on the same physical network, preventing accidental cross-talk between graphs.

question: In Unit 4, what does it mean for a topic to be "typed"?
options: Each publisher must declare a fixed publish rate in advance
Every message sent on the topic must match a single declared message type
Each subscriber must specify its callback's return type
The topic's string name must encode a type suffix
explanation: The unit defines "typed" as meaning every message sent on a topic must match a single declared message type — you can't publish a string on a topic declared to carry numbers.

question: In the publisher example, what does the `10` argument in `node->create_publisher<std_msgs::msg::String>("/greeting", 10)` control?
options: The timer period in milliseconds
The maximum number of subscribers allowed to connect
The queue size — how many outgoing messages can buffer before old ones are dropped if a subscriber is slow
The QoS reliability level (best-effort vs reliable)
explanation: The unit explicitly states "the 10 in create_publisher is the queue size — how many outgoing messages can buffer before old ones are dropped if a subscriber is slow."

question: According to Unit 4, what is a common silent-failure mode involving QoS settings in ROS 2?
options: Mismatched QoS between a publisher and subscriber means they simply never connect, with no error
A QoS mismatch causes the publisher node to crash immediately
QoS mismatches are always caught at compile time by the message generator
Mismatched QoS silently converts messages to the wrong type
explanation: The unit warns that mismatched QoS between a publisher and subscriber is a common silent-failure mode — they simply never connect, with no error.

question: Per Unit 5, why should subscriber callback code be fast?
options: ROS enforces a hard timeout that kills the node if a callback runs too long
Expensive work in a subscriber callback delays processing of every other callback running on that node's spin thread
Slow callbacks cause the message generator to fail at build time
Fast callbacks are a requirement for defining custom .msg files
explanation: The unit states callback code should be fast because expensive work in a subscriber callback delays processing of every other callback on that node, since callbacks run wherever spin() is running.

question: According to Unit 5, what happens when a publisher and subscriber declare mismatched topic names or message types?
options: ROS logs a fatal error and both nodes crash immediately
The message is automatically converted to match the subscriber's expected type
The two nodes simply never connect, silently, with no crash on either side
The subscriber blocks until an operator manually resolves the mismatch via the CLI
explanation: The unit calls this out as the single most common bug in a beginner's first pub/sub pair: a mismatch on name or type means the nodes never connect, silently, with no crash on either side.

question: Per Unit 5, what is required to make a custom message type usable in code after defining it?
options: Only a package.xml entry is needed; CMakeLists.txt is untouched
A .msg file inside a msg/ directory, plus generation wiring in CMakeLists.txt and message-generation dependencies declared in package.xml
Defining the fields as a plain C++ struct directly inside the node's .cpp file
Adding the fields to the node's launch file
explanation: The unit describes defining a .msg file in a msg/ directory, then wiring code generation into CMakeLists.txt (e.g. rosidl_generate_interfaces) and declaring generation dependencies in package.xml before the type becomes usable.

question: According to Unit 6, when should you use a service rather than a topic?
options: When data needs to stream continuously at high frequency to many listeners
When the interaction is "ask once, get one answer" and the work is short
When you need many nodes to broadcast to many other nodes
When the task takes tens of seconds and needs progress updates
explanation: The unit states: use a service when the interaction is "ask once, get one answer" and the work is short (milliseconds, not seconds); long or cancellable work should use an action instead.

question: Per Unit 6, what does `spin_until_future_complete` actually do?
options: It makes the underlying call genuinely synchronous, identical to ROS 1's blocking call
It blocks the calling thread until the future resolves (or the node stops spinning), making an inherently asynchronous call feel synchronous
It cancels the pending service request if it exceeds a timeout
It registers a brand-new service server on the node
explanation: The unit explains async_send_request always returns a future immediately, and spin_until_future_complete is what makes the call *feel* synchronous by blocking until the future resolves or spinning stops.

question: Unit 6 says a service client must always handle two failure modes. Which is one of them?
options: The message generator failing to compile the .srv file
The service not existing yet when called, and the call itself simply failing or timing out
A QoS mismatch preventing the client from ever connecting
The topic's queue size being exceeded
explanation: The unit lists the two failure modes to always handle: the service may not exist yet (hence looping on wait_for_service), and the call can simply fail or time out, so you must check the return code before trusting future.get().

question: Per Unit 7, how does a service callback's job differ from a subscriber callback's?
options: A service callback runs on a dedicated thread automatically, unlike a subscriber callback
A service callback's job is specifically to produce the response, since the caller is blocked or waiting until it does — a subscriber callback returns nothing
A service callback cannot access the node's member variables
A service callback must complete within a hard-coded millisecond limit or the node aborts
explanation: The unit states: "Unlike a subscriber callback (which returns nothing), a service callback's job is specifically to produce the response — the caller is blocked (or waiting on a future) until you do."

question: What is the three-step design loop Unit 7 describes for building a new service?
options: Write the .msg file, generate code, then echo the topic to confirm it works
Write the .srv file, wire generation into CMakeLists.txt/package.xml, then implement a server and throwaway test client to confirm it round-trips
Declare parameters, write a launch file, then run colcon build
Design an action first, then simplify it down into a service
explanation: The unit describes exactly this three-step loop, repeated until the request/response round-trips correctly, before building real logic on top.

question: Per Unit 7, since service callbacks by default share an executor thread with a node's other callbacks, what happens if a service handler does slow work?
options: The other callbacks run in parallel automatically thanks to multithreading
It delays everything else that node does — the same rule as subscriber callbacks
The node automatically spins up a new thread for every subsequent callback
The slow handler is silently dropped after a timeout
explanation: The unit states a slow service handler delays everything else that node does, the same rule as subscriber callbacks, and suggests offloading slow work to a background thread or reconsidering using an action.

question: In the class-based example in Unit 8, what mechanism connects a member function as a subscription callback, and why is it needed?
options: A raw function pointer, because subscriptions cannot accept lambdas
std::bind(&GreetingRelay::on_greeting, this, std::placeholders::_1), because member functions carry an implicit `this` argument that free functions don't
A virtual override of a base rclcpp::Node callback method
A global function pointer table registered at node startup
explanation: The unit explains std::bind (or an equivalent lambda capturing this) is required to hook a member function up as a callback, since member functions carry an implicit this argument.

question: According to Unit 8, what problem does wrapping a node in a class solve that plain free-function callbacks run into?
options: It makes callback dispatch faster by avoiding virtual function calls
Free-function callbacks needing to share state (a counter, a cached message) otherwise end up reaching for global variables; a class turns that shared state into member variables
It removes the need to ever call rclcpp::spin()
It lets multiple independent nodes run automatically inside one process
explanation: The unit states that free-function callbacks needing shared state end up reaching for globals, and wrapping the node in a class fixes this by making shared state member variables and callbacks member functions.

question: What does Unit 8 recommend under "composition over one giant node"?
options: Put your whole robot's logic into a single class for simplicity
Use a class per responsibility, one node per class (usually), letting the ROS graph — not internal function calls — be the seam between responsibilities
Avoid classes entirely and prefer free functions for maintainability
Share one global class instance across all nodes in the system
explanation: The unit explicitly advises resisting the urge to put a whole robot's logic in one class, favoring a class per responsibility with the ROS graph as the seam between them.

question: Per Unit 9, what two things does an action give you that implementing a long task as a service (and just waiting) would not?
options: Higher message throughput and lower network latency
Periodic feedback while the goal executes and the ability to cleanly cancel mid-goal
Automatic message compression and retry logic
Guaranteed synchronous blocking and stronger type safety
explanation: The unit states you'd lose feedback (periodic progress messages) and cancellation (the client asking the server to abort cleanly mid-goal) if you just used a service and waited.

question: According to the .action file format shown in Unit 9, what are the three parts of an action definition, in order?
options: Request, response, cancel
Goal, result, feedback, separated by `---`
Feedback, goal, result
Goal, feedback, cancel
explanation: The unit shows the Fibonacci.action file with goal fields, then `---`, result fields, then `---`, feedback fields — goal, result, feedback in that order.

question: Per Unit 9, what actually happens when a client calls async_cancel_goal?
options: The goal is guaranteed to stop within one feedback cycle
It requests cancellation, but the server decides how to honor it — a poorly written server may ignore the request entirely
The action server process is immediately terminated
The client blocks until the server sends an explicit cancellation acknowledgment before continuing
explanation: The unit states cancellation is only a request: the server decides how to honor it, and a poorly written server may ignore it entirely — which is why Unit 10 emphasizes checking for cancellation in the execution loop.

question: Per Unit 10, what three callbacks does an action server register?
options: One to accept/reject a goal, one to accept/reject a cancel request, and one to execute the goal
Publish, subscribe, and service callbacks
Init, spin, and shutdown callbacks
Goal, feedback, and result callbacks
explanation: The unit states an action server registers three callbacks: one to accept/reject an incoming goal, one to accept a cancel request, and one that executes the goal once accepted.

question: Why does Unit 10 say the execution callback typically runs on its own thread?
options: Because rclcpp requires every callback type to run on a separate thread by default
Because it may run for seconds or longer and must not block the node's other callbacks
Because publish_feedback() can only be called from a background thread by API design
Because handle_goal and handle_cancel need exclusive access to the main thread while executing
explanation: The unit explains the execution callback typically runs on its own thread since it may run for seconds or longer and must not block the node's other callbacks.

question: According to Unit 10, what happens if the execution loop never checks goal_handle->is_canceling()?
options: The server automatically times out and cancels after 30 seconds
Calling cancel from the client does nothing but flip an internal flag the server never looks at, and the goal runs to completion regardless
The action server throws an unhandled exception and crashes
The client's future resolves as canceled even though the server keeps executing
explanation: The unit states plainly that without the is_canceling() check, cancel calls do nothing but flip a flag the server never inspects, so the goal runs to completion regardless.

question: What does Unit 11 call "the single highest-leverage debugging habit in robotics"?
options: Permanently setting the log level to DEBUG
Recording bag data broadly and often, especially before you know you'll need it
Always testing spatial data in RViz before deployment
Writing a unit test for every callback function
explanation: The unit states recording (rosbag/ros2 bag) broadly and often, especially before you know you'll need it, is the single highest-leverage debugging habit in robotics.

question: Per Unit 11's guidance on log levels, when should WARN be used?
options: Only for things that actually broke behavior
For recoverable-but-suspicious conditions, e.g. a parameter defaulted because it was unset
For verbose values you want to inspect only during development
For any message that will be viewed by an operator
explanation: The unit's suggested habit is DEBUG for values you'd want to inspect, WARN for recoverable-but-suspicious conditions like a parameter defaulting because it was unset, and ERROR only for things that actually broke behavior.

question: According to Unit 11, what does the node graph visualizer reveal quickly that text-based introspection makes you hunt for?
options: The exact numeric battery percentage being published
A subscriber that never actually connected to its intended publisher, shown as no arrow between them
The CPU usage of each running node
The full contents of a recorded bag file
explanation: The unit states the graph tool immediately reveals a subscriber that never connected to its intended publisher — visible as a missing arrow — which text-based tools make you hunt for.

question: Per Unit 12, what is the general shape of installing ROS on a machine?
options: Download a single self-contained binary and run it; no package manager is involved
Add the ROS package repository and signing key, install the distro's meta-package, then install the build tool (colcon or catkin)
Build ROS entirely from source every time, since prebuilt binaries aren't distributed
Install only via a container, since native installs are unsupported for beginners
explanation: The unit describes the general installation shape as: add the ROS repo & signing key, install the distro meta-package, then install the build tool (python3-catkin-tools or python3-colcon-common-extensions).

question: According to Unit 12, what should you only ever put under a workspace's src/ directory?
options: Source packages — the build tool generates build/, install (or devel)/, and log/ directories that shouldn't be hand-edited or usually committed
Only compiled binaries produced after a build
The base ROS distro's system installation files
Launch files exclusively, with source code kept elsewhere
explanation: The unit states you only ever put source packages under src/; the build tool generates build/, install/ or devel/, and log/, which should not be hand-edited or usually committed to version control.

question: What does Unit 12 identify as the most common overlay bug when working with multiple workspaces?
options: Installing colcon before catkin instead of after
Sourcing workspaces out of order, or forgetting to re-source after a rebuild, so a node runs old code
Setting a different ROS_DOMAIN_ID in each workspace
Placing generated build directories inside src/
explanation: The unit states the most common overlay bug is sourcing workspaces out of order or forgetting to re-source after a rebuild — check which workspace's setup script is active if a node seems to run old code.

question: According to Unit 0, why does ROS exist, and why does this matter for reuse across robots?
options: It provides a shared set of conventions for how processes talk, so a driver written by one team can be reused by another building a different robot
It provides a faster runtime than any custom C++ stack, making individual programs faster
It eliminates the need to test code in simulation before deploying to hardware
It provides a single certified hardware platform that all robots must use
explanation: Unit 0 states that bespoke, one-off robotics software doesn't scale, and ROS gives the community shared conventions (how processes talk, how data is represented, how packages are built) so code composes and reuses across different robots.

question: Per Unit 0's 'How the five days fit together', which pair of units is described as teaching one communication mechanism via 'call then serve'?
options: Units 3-4
Units 5-6
Units 8-9
Units 1-2
explanation: Unit 0 explicitly says Units 3–4 and 5–6 each teach one mechanism from both directions: 'publish then subscribe, call then serve' — the 'call then serve' pair is Units 5–6 (services).

question: What does Unit 0's 'Try it yourself' section ask you to do before starting Unit 1?
options: Install ROS 2 and source the workspace overlay
Build a package with colcon and run a node
Write one sentence each guessing what problems topics, services, and actions solve, based only on the words themselves
Record a rosbag of the simulated robot moving
explanation: Unit 0 asks you to write down, before Unit 1 and with no ROS install needed, one-sentence guesses for what problems topics, services, and actions solve based only on their names, to revisit later.

question: In Unit 1's theoretical-pass teaching approach, a topic is compared to what?
options: A function call
A radio broadcast
A service that streams progress updates
A remote procedure call
explanation: Unit 1 says each concept gets a comparison to something familiar: 'a topic is like a radio broadcast; a service is like a function call; an action is like a service that streams progress updates.'

question: Per Unit 1's list of the 'five things you need to learn', what specifically sets actions apart from topics and services?
options: Actions are for long-running, goal-oriented tasks that need progress feedback and the ability to be cancelled mid-way
Actions are synchronous, blocking one-to-one calls
Actions have no message type at all
Actions are only usable from the command line, never from Python
explanation: Unit 1 defines actions as being for long-running, goal-oriented tasks needing progress feedback and cancellation — something neither topics nor services handle well alone.

question: According to Unit 1, the auto-corrected quizzes counted toward a course certificate appear in which units?
options: Units 1, 5, and 10
Units 3, 7, and 8
Units 4, 6, and 9
Units 2, 5, and 9
explanation: Unit 1 states a certificate requires completing every unit's exercises and passing 'the auto-corrected quizzes in Units 4, 6, and 9.'

question: Per Unit 2, why do generic CLI tools like ros2 topic pub and ros2 topic echo work with any topic without you writing custom code?
options: Because ROS standardizes how data is described (message types) independently of who produces or consumes it
Because every topic is secretly implemented as a service
Because these tools only work with std_msgs/String topics
Because colcon compiles a universal adapter for every topic
explanation: Unit 2 explains that ROS standardizes message types independent of producer/consumer, which is why generic CLI tools work with any topic and is 'the whole point of the framework.'

question: What did ROS 2 replace ROS 1's centralized roscore naming service with?
options: The colcon build tool
A decentralized discovery protocol (DDS)
The parameter server
rqt_graph
explanation: Unit 2 states ROS 2 removed the single point of failure of ROS 1's roscore in favor of a decentralized discovery protocol (DDS), so nodes find each other automatically without a roscore step.

question: Per Unit 2, what are the two common first-run issues that produce a 'package not found' or 'executable not found' error?
options: A missing ROS_DOMAIN_ID and a missing roscore process
Forgetting to source the workspace overlay after building, and forgetting to make the file executable/register it correctly in setup.py
A missing package.xml and an outdated colcon version
A missing launch file and an unset ROS_DISTRO
explanation: Unit 2 names exactly these two common first-run issues: not sourcing install/setup.bash after building, and not properly registering/making executable the node's Python file in setup.py.

question: In `self.create_publisher(String, 'chatter', 10)` from Unit 3, what does the 10 represent?
options: The number of subscribers permitted
The publish rate in Hz
The queue size (QoS depth) for buffering outgoing messages
A timeout in seconds before republishing
explanation: Unit 3 explains the 10 is 'the queue size (QoS depth) — how many outgoing messages to buffer if a subscriber can't keep up.'

question: Per Unit 3, why is publish/subscribe communication described as 'decoupled'?
options: Because a publisher requires the subscriber's acknowledgment before sending the next message
Because a second subscriber can be added to a topic without changing a single line of the publisher's code
Because only one subscriber can exist per topic at a time
Because publishers and subscribers must live in the same package
explanation: Unit 3 gives exactly this example: you can add a second subscriber to /cmd_vel 'without changing a single line of the publisher's code,' calling this the defining trait of decoupled pub/sub.

question: Per Unit 3, what is the simplest standard message type discussed, and what field does it contain?
options: geometry_msgs/msg/Twist, with linear and angular fields
std_msgs/msg/String, with a single field string data
sensor_msgs/msg/LaserScan, with a ranges array
std_msgs/msg/Int64, with a single field int64 data
explanation: Unit 3 states 'std_msgs/msg/String is the simplest message you'll use, with a single field: string data.'

question: Per Unit 4, what role does rclpy.spin() play for a subscriber node?
options: It is the loop that notices new messages and invokes your callback; you never call the callback yourself
It directly calls your listener_callback once and then exits
It builds the custom message class from a .msg file
It publishes a message onto the topic being subscribed to
explanation: Unit 4 says you 'hand control to rclpy.spin() and let the ROS executor drive your node's callbacks as messages arrive' — you never invoke the callback yourself.

question: Per Unit 4, what happens if a subscriber declares a different message type than the publisher on the same topic name?
options: ROS automatically converts between the two types
The subscriber node crashes on startup with a type error
They simply won't see each other; ROS will not connect them
The publisher throws an exception the next time it publishes
explanation: Unit 4 states 'ROS will not connect a String subscriber to a Twist publisher on the same topic name; they simply won't see each other,' calling this a common source of 'why isn't my callback firing' bugs.

question: Per Unit 4, where does a custom .msg file belong, and how is it registered so the interface generator picks it up?
options: In the launch/ folder, referenced from the launch file
In a msg/ folder inside a package, declared in CMakeLists.txt/package.xml
Directly appended to the bottom of hello_node.py
In install/setup.bash after the workspace is built
explanation: Unit 4 instructs putting the .msg file in a msg/ folder inside a package (conventionally a dedicated _msgs package) and declaring it in CMakeLists.txt/package.xml.

question: Per Unit 5's comparison table, what is the key blocking difference between a topic publish and a service call?
options: A topic publish blocks until acknowledged; a service call does not
Neither a topic publish nor a service call ever blocks
A service call blocks (waits for a reply); a topic publish does not
Both block for the same fixed duration
explanation: Unit 5's table marks the Service row as 'Blocking?: Yes' and the Topic row as 'Blocking?: No' — a service call waits for a reply, a topic publish is fire-and-forget.

question: Per Unit 5, how many servers can own a given service name at one time, and how does that compare to topics?
options: Unlimited servers, exactly like topics allow many publishers
Only one server, unlike topics where many publishers can coexist
Exactly two, for failover redundancy
Zero — services have no concept of ownership
explanation: Unit 5 states 'Only one server can own a given service name at a time, which is different from topics where many publishers can coexist.'

question: Per Unit 5, why does the example Python client call rclpy.spin_until_future_complete rather than simply blocking on the result directly?
options: Because call_async doesn't block the executor thread by itself, and you must drive execution until the future resolves so other node callbacks aren't frozen
Because services in ROS 2 don't support any synchronous behavior at all
Because the request must be resent repeatedly until the server accepts it
Because it converts the service request into an action goal internally
explanation: Unit 5 explains 'call_async never blocks the executor thread by itself — you're responsible for driving execution until the future resolves,' which matters once a node has other callbacks that also need to run.

question: Per Unit 6, what happens if a service callback returns None or the wrong response type?
options: The node automatically restarts itself
ROS silently retries the request against a different server
The client's call will hang or error out
The server logs a warning but the call otherwise completes normally
explanation: Unit 6 warns: 'get this wrong (return None, or the wrong type) and the client's call will hang or error out,' since the callback's return value is the actual reply sent back.

question: Per Unit 6, why should service callbacks be kept fast?
options: Because ROS enforces a hard 10ms timeout on every service
Because a client blocks (or waits on a future) for the response, so a slow callback makes every caller of that service wait too
Because slow callbacks corrupt the per-node parameter server
Because faster callbacks consume less network bandwidth
explanation: Unit 6 states that because clients wait for a response, 'a service callback that does anything slow ... makes every caller of that service wait too.'

question: Per Unit 6, if a task genuinely takes a while and callers need progress updates, what should you use instead of a service?
options: A topic
A launch file
A parameter
An action
explanation: Unit 6 concludes that a task needing time and progress updates 'is a strong signal you actually want an action ... not a service.'

question: Per Unit 7, why is rclpy built around subclassing Node rather than writing plain functions?
options: A node needs to hold state across many independent callback invocations, and a class instance holds that state via self
Functions are incapable of being registered as ROS entry points under any circumstances
Subclassing is a hard requirement of Python's syntax for defining any callback
Only classes can be compiled by colcon
explanation: Unit 7 explains that a node needs to hold state across callbacks, and 'a plain function has no natural place to keep that state between calls; a class instance does, via self.'

question: In the PatrolNode example from Unit 7, how do odom_callback and stop_callback coordinate with each other?
options: odom_callback calls stop_callback directly as a plain method call
They coordinate through shared instance state (self.patrolling, self.last_position), not by talking to each other directly
They exchange a dedicated ROS service defined just for that purpose
They publish to a shared topic that both subscribe to
explanation: Unit 7's diagram and text show both callbacks reading and writing the same shared instance state (self.patrolling, self.last_position) rather than talking to each other directly.

question: Per Unit 7, what does the call super().__init__('minimal_publisher') actually accomplish?
options: It only sets a cosmetic log prefix with no other functional effect
It starts the node's timer immediately
It registers the node with the ROS graph under that name, giving access to Node's pre-wired methods like create_publisher
It triggers a colcon build of the enclosing package
explanation: Unit 7 states this call 'registers the node with the ROS graph under that name — this is inheritance doing real work,' providing create_publisher, create_subscription, etc., pre-wired to the node's identity.

question: Per Unit 8, in what order do the three sections of a .action file appear?
options: Feedback, goal, result
Goal, feedback, result
Goal, result, feedback
Result, feedback, goal
explanation: Unit 8 states explicitly: 'The three sections (separated by ---) are goal, result, and feedback, in that order,' matching the Takeoff.action example.

question: Per Unit 8, what is the key advantage of send_goal_async over a blocking service call for a long-running task?
options: It guarantees the task will finish faster
The node's executor stays free to keep processing other callbacks while the goal runs in the background
It removes the need to define a result message
It automatically retries the goal if it is rejected
explanation: Unit 8 says that because send_goal_async returns immediately, 'the node's executor is free to keep processing other callbacks ... This is the key advantage over a blocking service call for long tasks.'

question: Per Unit 8, what actually happens when a client calls cancel_goal_async?
options: The action server process is immediately terminated
The client's node shuts down to free resources
The client only requests preemption; the server decides how to actually respond to the cancel request
The goal is guaranteed to stop within one control cycle
explanation: Unit 8 states 'The server decides how to actually respond to a cancel request ... the client only requests preemption, it can't force an immediate stop.'

question: Per Unit 9, what must an action server's execute_callback do before returning, no matter the outcome?
options: Call rclpy.spin() one more time
Call exactly one of goal_handle.succeed(), abort(), or canceled()
Publish at least ten feedback messages
Re-register the action server under a new name
explanation: Unit 9 states 'exactly one of succeed(), abort(), or canceled() must be called before returning, or the client's result future never resolves cleanly.'

question: Per Unit 9, what is described as the most common bug in a first action server implementation?
options: Forgetting to call rclpy.init() before creating the node
Publishing feedback on the wrong topic name
A loop that does a large chunk of work (or sleeps a long time) between checks of is_cancel_requested
Defining the .action file with only two sections instead of three
explanation: Unit 9 states: 'The most common bug in a first action server is a loop that does a large chunk of work (or sleeps a long time) between checks of is_cancel_requested.'

question: Per Unit 9, how does the number of sections in a .action file compare to a .srv file?
options: A .action file has three sections (goal, result, feedback); a .srv file has two
Both have exactly two sections
A .action file has one section; a .srv file has two
A .action file has four sections; a .srv file has three
explanation: Unit 9 notes .action files have three sections separated by --- (goal, result, feedback), 'easy to mix up with services, which are only two sections.'

question: Per Unit 10, which log severity levels are shown by default when using self.get_logger()?
options: Only fatal
debug and above
info and above
Only error and fatal
explanation: Unit 10 states 'By default info and above are shown' among the debug/info/warn/error/fatal severity levels.

question: Per Unit 10, what specific problem does rqt_graph help you spot?
options: A wiring mistake, such as two nodes you expected to be connected showing up as isolated boxes due to a topic name/type mismatch
The exact battery percentage of the simulated robot
Which severity level a particular log message was sent at
The CPU load of each running node
explanation: Unit 10 says rqt_graph is 'the fastest way to confirm a wiring mistake: if you expected [two nodes] to be connected and the graph shows them as isolated boxes, you have a topic name or type mismatch.'

question: Per Unit 10, why is a recorded rosbag useful for regression testing?
options: It automatically patches bugs discovered during the recorded session
It converts every recorded topic into a service for easier replay control
It plays messages back on the same topic names at the same relative timing, so a subscriber can't tell live data from a replay
It only works when RViz is open and displaying the robot model
explanation: Unit 10 states a bag 'plays messages back on the same topic names at the same relative timing they were recorded — any subscriber node can't tell the difference between live data and a bag replay, which is what makes this useful for regression testing.'

question: In the Simple Commander API example, what does calling nav.waitUntilNav2Active() actually do, and why does the unit call out skipping it as a common mistake?
options: It blocks until AMCL and the BT navigator lifecycle node report active; skipping it is why a first goal sent right after startup can be silently dropped
It blocks until the costmap_filter_info_server publishes its first CostmapFilterInfo message; skipping it means keepout masks are ignored
It waits for the global costmap to finish its first full inflation pass; skipping it just makes the first path slightly suboptimal
It triggers a lifecycle 'configure' transition on controller_server; skipping it prevents the action server from ever appearing
explanation: The unit states waitUntilNav2Active() 'blocks until AMCL + the BT navigator report active' and is 'the piece people skip and then wonder why their first goal is silently dropped.'

question: Per the unit, how does followWaypoints differ from goThroughPoses when driving the same list of poses?
options: goThroughPoses stops at each pose to run a waypoint task, while followWaypoints passes through them as via-points for a single continuous path
goThroughPoses treats the poses as via-points for one continuous path without necessarily stopping at each, while followWaypoints visits each pose as a discrete stop, optionally running a task there
followWaypoints can only be triggered from RViz's 'Nav2 Goal' button, while goThroughPoses is Python-API-only
goThroughPoses requires a costmap filter mask to be loaded first, while followWaypoints does not
explanation: The unit says goThroughPoses treats intermediate poses as via-points for a continuous path (e.g. patrol a hallway), while followWaypoints stops at each pose and can run a task via waypoint task executors (e.g. inspection points).

question: According to the unit, what is the functional difference between a Keepout Filter and a Speed Filter?
options: A Keepout Filter only affects the local costmap, while a Speed Filter only affects the global costmap
A Keepout Filter requires the Simple Commander API to function, while a Speed Filter works only with RViz-issued goals
A Keepout Filter marks painted regions as unenterable via high cost, while a Speed Filter scales the robot's max speed within painted regions instead of blocking it
A Keepout Filter runs inside the BT navigator, while a Speed Filter runs inside controller_server directly with no costmap involvement
explanation: The unit describes the Keepout Filter as marking regions the robot must never enter via high cost, while the Speed Filter 'scales the robot's max speed within painted regions instead of blocking them entirely.'

question: Based on the unit's explanation of Nav2's two workhorse composite node types, how do Sequence and Fallback differ?
options: Sequence stops at the first child that succeeds, while Fallback requires every child to succeed before returning SUCCESS
Sequence ticks all children simultaneously, while Fallback ticks them strictly one at a time with a delay between each
Sequence is used only for recovery actions, while Fallback is used only for the main planning/following pipeline

Sequence ticks children in order and stops (fails) at the first failing child, succeeding only if all succeed; Fallback ticks children in order and stops (succeeds) at the first succeeding child, failing only if all fail
explanation: The unit defines Sequence as 'do A, then B, then C' logic (stops at first failure) and Fallback/Selector as 'try A, and if that doesn't work, try B' logic (stops at first success).

question: In the default recovery tree XML shown in the unit, what role does the blackboard (the {goal} and {path} syntax) play?
options: It's a shared key-value store that lets one node's output, such as the path produced by ComputePathToPose, become another node's input, like FollowPath, without either node referencing the other directly
It's a live network topic that streams tree state to Groot so the visual editor can render ticking nodes
It's the plugin description XML that pairs a BT node's class name with its pluginlib base class
It's a fixed configuration file loaded once at startup that stores the RecoveryNode's retry count
explanation: The unit explains the {goal}/{path} syntax is 'a blackboard entry — a shared key-value store all nodes in the tree can read and write, which is how a ComputePathToPose output becomes a FollowPath input without any node needing a direct reference to another.'

question: Why does the unit say Nav2's default trees treat recovery behaviors (Spin, Wait, BackUp) as a first line of defense rather than failing immediately?
options: Because pluginlib requires every behavior tree to contain at least one RoundRobin node to compile correctly
Because navigation failure is normal rather than exceptional — a transient obstacle or a momentarily infeasible plan is common, so cheap recoveries are tried before escalating to task failure
Because the controller plugin cannot compute any velocity command until the robot has physically spun in place once
Because planner_server refuses to accept a new ComputePathToPose request unless a prior recovery action has completed
explanation: The unit states recovery behaviors 'exist because navigation failure is normal, not exceptional' and that default trees 'treat these as a first line of defense, escalating to task failure only after several combined attempts.'

question: Per the unit's description of the pluginlib pattern, what two pieces must agree for a custom Nav2 plugin to be loaded correctly?
options: The node name given in the launch file and the class name used inside the URDF
The parameter YAML's controller_frequency value and the plugin's internal update-rate constant
The PLUGINLIB_EXPORT_CLASS macro in the .cpp naming the concrete class and its base class, and the plugin description XML mapping that same class name to the same base class
The build_type declared in package.xml and the C++ standard version set in CMakeLists.txt
explanation: The unit says registration 'has two halves that must agree: a PLUGINLIB_EXPORT_CLASS macro in your .cpp naming the concrete class and the base class it implements, and a plugin description XML that maps a human-readable plugin name to that same class.'

question: According to the unit's comparison of the three plugin types, why is a controller plugin singled out as having 'hard real-time expectations' compared to a planner plugin?
options: computeVelocityCommands() runs once per replanning cycle, while createPlan() runs continuously at 20 Hz+ to keep the costmap updated
Both run at the same rate, but only the controller plugin is allowed to modify the shared costmap directly
The controller plugin is the only one of the three that is not loaded through pluginlib, so it runs as a separate real-time process
createPlan() runs once per replanning cycle, while computeVelocityCommands() runs every tick at the full control-loop rate (commonly 20 Hz+), so a slow controller directly stalls the robot
explanation: The unit states the planner plugin 'runs once per replanning cycle, not continuously,' while the controller plugin 'runs at the control loop's full rate (commonly 20 Hz+)... it's the only one of the three with hard real-time expectations, since a slow controller directly stalls the robot.'

question: Among the default costmap layers listed in the unit, which one is described as spreading cost outward from obstacles so the planner keeps clearance?
options: InflationLayer
StaticLayer
ObstacleLayer
VoxelLayer
explanation: The unit lists 'InflationLayer (spreads cost outward from obstacles so the planner keeps clearance)' as distinct from StaticLayer (loads the map) and ObstacleLayer/VoxelLayer (mark live sensor obstacles).

question: According to the unit, what does the progress checker plugin actually monitor, and why does the unit flag it as important?
options: Whether the robot's pose and velocity are both within tolerance of the goal; it's often mistaken for the controller plugin's job
Whether the robot's pose has moved more than a threshold distance within a time window; it's often the real cause of a triggered recovery, not the controller plugin itself
Whether controller_frequency is being met on every tick; it forces the control loop back to 20 Hz if it drifts
Whether the plugin description XML matches the PLUGINLIB_EXPORT_CLASS macro at load time
explanation: The unit says the progress checker 'answers is the robot actually making headway, or stuck? by watching whether the robot's pose has moved more than a threshold distance within a time window... this is often what's really behind a robot triggering a recovery, not the controller plugin itself.'

question: Per the unit, what does the controller_server parameter failure_tolerance control?
options: The maximum lateral distance the robot may deviate from the planned path before the controller plugin is swapped for a fallback
The positional tolerance in meters used by the goal checker to decide the robot has arrived
How many seconds the controller is allowed to produce no valid velocity command before the server reports failure to the behavior tree, triggering a recovery instead of an immediate abort
The number of RecoveryNode retries attempted before controller_server itself shuts down
explanation: The unit defines failure_tolerance as 'how long (seconds) the controller is allowed to produce no valid command before the server reports failure back up to the behavior tree, which is what triggers a recovery... rather than an immediate abort.'

question: The unit's final exercise compares StoppedGoalChecker against SimpleGoalChecker at a high desired_linear_vel. What causes the robot to travel further past the nominal goal point before success is reported?
options: StoppedGoalChecker uses a wider xy_goal_tolerance by default than SimpleGoalChecker, so it takes longer to register arrival
StoppedGoalChecker replaces the controller plugin near the goal and decelerates the robot more slowly than RegulatedPurePursuitController would
StoppedGoalChecker is only compatible with DWBLocalPlanner, which brakes later than RegulatedPurePursuitController
StoppedGoalChecker additionally requires the robot's velocity to be near zero before declaring the goal reached, unlike SimpleGoalChecker, which checks position only
explanation: The unit notes StoppedGoalChecker 'additionally requires the robot's velocity to be near zero before declaring the goal reached,' and frames the extra travel distance at high speed as 'entirely attributable to which goal checker plugin you loaded, not to the controller plugin driving the robot.'

question: According to Unit 1, why do Finite State Machines (FSMs) tend to become unmaintainable as more edge cases and recoveries are added?
options: The number of transitions grows roughly with the square of the number of states, so a single new rule can mean touching transitions all over the graph
FSMs cannot return a RUNNING status, so every action must block the whole program
FSMs require a shared blackboard that becomes a bottleneck as more states are added
ROS2's executor does not support FSM-based nodes natively
explanation: Unit 1 states that FSM transitions grow roughly with the square of the number of states, so adding one new 'abort and retry' rule can mean touching transitions all over the graph — this is the specific problem BTs are introduced to solve.

question: In Unit 1's sample XML tree (Fallback containing a Sequence of CheckBattery and MoveToGoal, followed by GoToChargingStation), what happens if CheckBattery fails?
options: The Sequence fails immediately and the outer Fallback moves on to GoToChargingStation
MoveToGoal still runs because Fallback ignores failures from earlier siblings
The tree returns RUNNING until the battery is manually recharged
CheckBattery is retried automatically before the Sequence can fail
explanation: The unit explains that the root Fallback tries the Sequence first; if CheckBattery fails, the whole Sequence fails and control falls through to GoToChargingStation.

question: Per the environment setup instructions in Unit 1, which package should you install to follow along with Nav2-flavored examples and inspect real-world reference BT nodes?
options: ros-$ROS_DISTRO-nav2-behavior-tree
ros-$ROS_DISTRO-groot2
ros-$ROS_DISTRO-behaviortree-msgs
ros-$ROS_DISTRO-nav2-bringup
explanation: Unit 1 says to also install ros-$ROS_DISTRO-nav2-behavior-tree, which ships a library of navigation-specific BT nodes usable as real-world reference material.

question: Per Unit 2, what specifically separates Behavior Trees from a plain decision tree?
options: The RUNNING status, which lets long-running actions be re-ticked over multiple cycles instead of blocking the tree
The presence of a shared blackboard for passing data between nodes
The fact that BTs are always defined in XML rather than code
The requirement that every leaf node be a Condition node
explanation: Unit 2 states that RUNNING is what separates BTs from a plain decision tree: long-running actions report RUNNING and get re-ticked, keeping the tree responsive rather than blocking.

question: How does Unit 2 distinguish the Sequence control-flow node from the Fallback (Selector) node?
options: Sequence succeeds only if all children succeed (AND, in order); Fallback succeeds as soon as one child succeeds (OR, in priority order)
Sequence ticks all children simultaneously every cycle; Fallback ticks only one child at a time chosen at random
Sequence is reactive by default and re-checks earlier children; Fallback is not
Fallback stops ticking further children as soon as one fails; Sequence keeps ticking children after a failure
explanation: Unit 2 describes Sequence as 'AND, in order' (succeeds only if all children succeed, fails on first failure) and Fallback as 'OR, in priority order' (succeeds as soon as one child succeeds, fails only if all fail).

question: In Unit 2's end-to-end example tree (Fallback over a Sequence of BatteryOK, a Retry-wrapped MoveToGoal, and PickUpObject, with ReturnToBase as the other Fallback branch), what happens if the battery is fine but navigation fails three times in a row?
options: The Retry decorator reports FAILURE, which fails the Sequence, and the outer Fallback falls back to ReturnToBase
The tree returns RUNNING indefinitely since Retry never produces a final result
The Sequence skips MoveToGoal and proceeds directly to PickUpObject
BatteryOK is re-ticked and the entire tree restarts from the beginning
explanation: Unit 2 traces this exact case: after three failed attempts, Retry finally reports FAILURE, which fails the Sequence, and the outer Fallback falls back to returning to base.

question: In BehaviorTree.CPP XML as described in Unit 3, what does writing object_pose="{target}" (with curly braces) signify, as opposed to a plain string value?
options: It marks the value as a blackboard reference rather than a literal string
It marks the port as optional, so the node can run without it being set
It declares the port as an OutputPort rather than an InputPort
It tells the BehaviorTreeFactory to treat "target" as a registered node ID
explanation: Unit 3 explicitly states the {target} syntax marks a blackboard reference, as opposed to a literal string, and that this explicitness lets you read data flow directly from the XML.

question: Why does Unit 3 recommend using a ReactiveSequence instead of a plain Sequence for a 'battery check + navigate' combination?
options: A plain Sequence won't re-check BatteryOK once MoveToGoal is RUNNING, so a battery failure mid-navigation would go unnoticed until ReactiveSequence re-evaluates it
ReactiveSequence executes its children in parallel, completing the combined task faster
A plain Sequence cannot contain any Action node capable of returning RUNNING
ReactiveSequence automatically retries BatteryOK up to three times before reporting failure
explanation: Unit 3 explains that a plain Sequence won't re-check earlier children like BatteryOK while a later child is RUNNING, so ReactiveSequence is needed to re-evaluate BatteryOK every tick and interrupt navigation if it fails.

question: What trade-off does Unit 3 identify for using ReactiveSequence over a plain Sequence?
options: Reactive nodes re-tick their condition children every cycle at extra cost, so they should be reserved for genuinely safety- or priority-relevant checks
ReactiveSequence cannot be combined with Decorator nodes such as Retry
ReactiveSequence requires each child to maintain its own separate blackboard
ReactiveSequence can only be used as the root of a tree, never nested inside a SubTree
explanation: Unit 3 states the trade-off is cost: reactive nodes re-tick their condition children every cycle, so they should be used for safety- or priority-relevant checks, not one-time setup steps.

question: When subclassing BT::RosActionNode as shown in Unit 4, which parts do you actually need to implement yourself?
options: setGoal (to build the goal) and onResultReceived (to interpret the result) — the base class already handles sending the goal and reporting RUNNING
tick() and halt(), since RosActionNode provides no default tick behavior
Only providedPorts(); setGoal and onResultReceived are optional overrides
A custom feedback callback that manually sets the node's status to RUNNING on every feedback message
explanation: Unit 4 states the base class handles sending the goal and reporting RUNNING while the action server works; you only implement the hooks specific to your action, namely building the goal (setGoal) and interpreting the result (onResultReceived).

question: Why does Unit 4's example tick the tree at 10 Hz (a 100 ms wall timer) rather than much faster or much slower?
options: It is fast enough to feel responsive but slow enough not to burn CPU on trees with expensive condition checks
100 ms is the minimum interval BT::Tree::tickOnce() enforces internally
ROS2 action servers are only able to publish feedback at 10 Hz
Groot2 cannot render live tree state for ticks faster than 10 Hz
explanation: Unit 4 explicitly describes 10 Hz (100 ms) as 'a common default — fast enough to feel responsive, slow enough not to burn CPU on trees with expensive condition checks.'

question: Per Unit 4, what must happen before the XML tags referencing a custom node class (e.g., NavigateToPose) have any meaning to the tree?
options: The node class must be registered with a BT::BehaviorTreeFactory (e.g., via registerNodeType)
The node must be compiled directly into the nav2_behavior_tree package
Every port on the node must be pre-populated with a default blackboard value
Groot2 must be connected to the running tree to assign the node a unique ID
explanation: Unit 4 states that custom node classes need to be registered with a BT::BehaviorTreeFactory before the XML tags that reference them mean anything.

question: According to Unit 5, what is the problem with wrapping every action in an unconditional Retry 'just in case'?
options: It masks genuine failures — a truly unreachable goal will still retry N times before eventually failing anyway, wasting time and making debugging harder
It breaks the three-status contract because Retry decorators can only return SUCCESS
It causes the tree to return RUNNING forever and never reach a final status
It prevents the blackboard from being updated between successive attempts
explanation: Unit 5 calls unconditional Retry a common anti-pattern, explaining it hides genuine failures and wastes time since an unreachable goal will retry N times before failing anyway.

question: In the 'handling flaky actions' pattern from Unit 5, what actually determines whether a failed action gets retried versus reported as a failure right away?
options: Whether IsFailureTransient judges the failure reason on the blackboard to be transient rather than persistent
Whether the confidence_threshold input port is set above 0.8
Whether the failing action is wrapped in a ReactiveSequence instead of a plain Sequence
Whether an automated planner had marked that action as high priority
explanation: Unit 5's pattern uses an IsFailureTransient condition that inspects the failure reason on the blackboard, retrying only the transient category and reporting persistent failures immediately.

question: How does Unit 5 describe the division of responsibility between an automated planner and the Behavior Tree it produces?
options: The planner is the deliberative component deciding what to do and the tree's shape, while the BT remains the reactive executor handling tick-by-tick execution and interruption
The planner replaces the blackboard as the mechanism for passing data between BT nodes
The BT decides what actions to take, while the planner only logs the resulting statuses after execution
The planner ticks each leaf node directly, bypassing the Sequence/Fallback control structure entirely
explanation: Unit 5 states the BT is the reactive executor and the planner is the deliberative component that decides the tree's shape, keeping execution (reactivity, interruption handling) separate from deliberation (what to do).

question: Per the project brief in Unit 6, what makes a fallback 'genuine' rather than just a disguised retry?
options: It is a materially different strategy when the primary approach fails, such as asking for help or trying an alternate object, rather than simply repeating the same action
It must be implemented using a Parallel node instead of a Fallback node
It is any Decorator node that flips a SUCCESS result into a FAILURE result
It must include a confidence-gated Condition node backed by a probabilistic estimate
explanation: The project brief requires 'at least one genuine fallback, not just a retry — a materially different strategy when the primary approach fails (e.g., "ask for help" or "try an alternate object" rather than just repeating the same action).'

question: Unit 6's self-review asks whether adding a brand-new failure mode (e.g., 'gripper jams') would require inserting one new branch or rewiring transitions all over the tree. What is this test checking, and where is it introduced?
options: It is the FSM-vs-BT test from Unit 1, checking whether the tree is structured well enough that new behavior can be added locally instead of requiring rewiring
It is a check of whether Groot2 can render the live tree without visualization errors
It is testing whether every blackboard key is strongly typed at compile time
It is checking whether the tree can still be ticked at the standard 10 Hz rate
explanation: Unit 6 explicitly labels this 'the FSM-vs-BT test from Unit 1 — if the answer is "rewiring," the tree isn't structured well yet,' tying the self-review directly back to Unit 1's core argument for BTs over FSMs.

question: In the suggested 'tidy the table' project scope from Unit 6, what specifically triggers the 'call for help' fallback?
options: Grasping repeatedly fails on the same object
The robot's battery level drops below a set threshold during navigation
The table is detected as already empty of objects
The maximum number of navigation attempts (not grasping attempts) is reached
explanation: Unit 6 describes the scope as repeating pick-up-and-deliver 'with a battery-check interrupt and a "call for help" fallback if grasping repeatedly fails on the same object' — the help fallback is tied to repeated grasp failure, not battery or navigation.

question: According to the unit, what is a snap?
options: A compressed, read-only squashfs filesystem image containing the app plus all its library and runtime dependencies, mounted read-only and run under confinement
A tarball of compiled binaries that gets extracted directly into /usr/local on the target machine
A Docker container image built from a Dockerfile and run by the Docker daemon
A .deb package that relies on apt to resolve and install the right ros-humble-* dependencies
