explanation: The unit says a single centroid "may average between two paths and steer into neither," and the fix is to find all contours and "choose the one whose centroid is closest to the robot's current heading (or closest to the previous frame's chosen centroid)."

question: What is the role of RANSAC in the surface-detection pipeline described in Unit 4?
options: Repeatedly sample a random set of points, fit a plane, count inliers close to it, and keep the best-scoring plane after many trials — separating the surface (inliers) from candidate objects (outliers)
It clusters the outlier points into discrete object groups based on distance thresholds
It converts a PointCloud2 message into an OpenCV-compatible NumPy array
It trains a deep-learning model end-to-end to segment the point cloud
explanation: Unit 4 defines RANSAC as repeatedly picking random points, fitting a plane, counting inliers, and keeping the best-scoring plane — "points that fit the plane are the surface; everything else is a candidate object."

question: In the DBSCAN clustering code shown in Unit 4, what does a label of -1 mean, and how is it handled?
options: -1 marks DBSCAN's "noise" points, and the code excludes label -1 when building the per-cluster point groups
-1 marks the detected surface plane itself, which is merged into the largest object cluster
-1 indicates a sensor malfunction and causes the entire cloud message to be discarded
-1 marks the object closest to the sensor's origin and is treated as the primary target
explanation: The code comment explicitly notes "-1 is DBSCAN's 'noise' label" and the dict comprehension only keeps clusters where `label != -1`.

question: How does Unit 4 describe the relationship between pcl_conversions and cv_bridge?
options: pcl_conversions bridges PointCloud2 messages to PCL's native cloud types, mirroring how cv_bridge bridges Image messages to OpenCV
pcl_conversions replaces cv_bridge entirely once 3D point cloud data is involved
cv_bridge itself handles both Image and PointCloud2 conversions, making pcl_conversions redundant
pcl_conversions is described as a Python-only wrapper around cv_bridge's C++ internals
explanation: The unit states a PointCloud2 message "is converted to and from PCL's native cloud types using pcl_conversions, mirroring how cv_bridge bridges Image messages to OpenCV."

question: Why does Unit 5 run eye detection only inside a previously detected face region rather than across the whole frame?
options: It both speeds things up and avoids false eye-like detections elsewhere in the image
OpenCV's eye cascade cannot technically process an image region larger than a detected face crop
Haar cascades can only ever detect one object class per node, requiring a nested detection call
It compensates for the face cascade missing faces turned past 45 degrees
explanation: The unit states running eye detection inside each detected face region "both speeds things up and avoids false eye-like detections elsewhere in the image."

question: What practical limitation of Haar cascade face detection does the Unit 5 hands-on exercise call out?
options: Detection degrades once a person's head turns roughly past 30-45 degrees, since cascades are sensitive to pose and scale
It requires a GPU and cannot run in real time on modest hardware
It cannot distinguish a face from an eye without a separate recognition model
It only works if the detected face fills the entire camera frame
explanation: The unit says to expect detection to "degrade past roughly a 30-45 degree turn," since "Haar cascades are sensitive to head pose and scale."

question: According to Unit 5, how does tracking (e.g., with TrackerCSRT) differ from running full detection fresh on every frame?
options: Tracking maintains identity and position across frames using appearance and motion cues, avoiding the slowness and jitter of re-detecting from scratch each frame
Tracking is used only to determine identity, not position, so it cannot be used to steer the robot
Tracking makes detection unnecessary altogether once a person's identity is known
Tracking still runs full detection every frame, just caching the previous frame's result
explanation: The unit explains tracking "maintains identity and position across frames, so the robot can follow a specific person rather than re-detecting from scratch every frame (which is both slower and prone to jitter if detection briefly fails)."

question: What is the fundamental architectural difference between YOLO and the classical "propose regions, then classify" detection pipeline, per Unit 6?
options: YOLO passes the whole image through one convolutional network exactly once, directly outputting boxes, classes, and confidence scores in a single forward pass
YOLO still proposes candidate regions first, but classifies all of them in parallel on a GPU
YOLO requires a separate segmentation pass before any bounding box can be produced
YOLO uses Haar cascades internally in place of convolutional layers to find candidate regions
explanation: The unit states YOLO "reframes object detection as a single regression problem... the whole image passes through one convolutional network exactly once, and the network directly outputs a set of bounding boxes with class labels and confidence scores in one forward pass."

question: According to Unit 6, when should you fine-tune a pretrained YOLO model rather than just using pretrained COCO weights directly?
options: When your robot needs to recognize an object class that genuinely isn't in any general-purpose dataset
Always — the unit states pretrained weights are never usable for real robotics tasks
Only when running exclusively on CPU-only hardware with no GPU available
Never — the unit states fine-tuning is always more expensive than training from scratch
explanation: The unit says pretrained weights are the right default "unless your robot needs to recognize an object class that genuinely isn't in any general-purpose dataset — in which case fine-tuning... is far cheaper than training from scratch."

question: How does Unit 6 distinguish instance segmentation from pose estimation within YOLO's task family?
options: Instance segmentation produces a pixel-precise mask per object (useful when exact shape matters, e.g. a grasp point), while pose estimation produces a skeleton of keypoints per detected person
Pose estimation produces pixel-precise masks, while instance segmentation only produces bounding boxes
Instance segmentation only runs on the nano model variant, while pose estimation runs on all variants
Pose estimation eliminates the need for object detection entirely, while instance segmentation still requires it
explanation: The unit defines instance segmentation as producing "a pixel-precise mask per detected object... useful whenever the exact shape matters," versus pose estimation's "skeleton of keypoints... per detected person."

question: According to Unit 7, what is the actual skill being tested by this capstone project?
options: Wiring already-learned techniques into one state machine that hands control between "drive" and "inspect" modes
Designing a brand-new deep-learning architecture not covered in any earlier unit
Calibrating a physical robot's wheel encoders for precise odometry
Writing a custom PCL algorithm from scratch to replace RANSAC
explanation: The unit states "almost none of the individual techniques are new, but wiring them into one state machine that hands control between 'drive' and 'inspect' modes is the actual skill being tested."

question: Why does the capstone's color-encoding step use a lookup table (COLOR_RANGES) of multiple named ranges instead of the single hardcoded HSV threshold from Unit 3?
options: Because the robot now needs to classify which of several colors a detected box is, not just detect the presence of one
Because HSV thresholding no longer functions once RANSAC plane fitting has been applied to the scene
Because Python dictionaries are required to store any HSV threshold values
Because the stop-marker detector needs more color ranges than the object-counting step does
explanation: The unit explains this is needed "since you now need to classify *which* of several colors a detected box is, not just detect the presence of one."

question: How does the robot in Unit 7's capstone know when to switch from FOLLOWING_LINE to INSPECTING state?
options: It detects a distinct stop marker (e.g., a perpendicular stripe or fiducial) crossing the line, checked on every frame during line-following
It counts elapsed time since the last shelf and switches automatically after a fixed duration
It switches only when the YOLO detector reports zero detections for three consecutive frames
A human operator sends a manual service call to trigger the state transition
explanation: The unit describes checking `detect_stop_marker(frame)` on every frame, using "a distinct marker at each stop (a perpendicular stripe crossing the line, or a fiducial marker)" to trigger the switch to INSPECTING.

question: According to the unit, what is the role of cv_bridge in a ROS perception node?
options: It converts sensor_msgs/Image messages to and from OpenCV's numpy arrays
It converts PointCloud2 messages into voxel-downsampled clouds
It publishes the camera intrinsics needed for pixel-to-3D conversion
It performs HSV color thresholding on incoming frames
explanation: The unit calls cv_bridge 'the seam between ROS and OpenCV,' converting sensor_msgs/Image messages to and from OpenCV numpy arrays via imgmsg_to_cv2 — thresholding, intrinsics, and cloud downsampling are separate topics covered in other units.

question: What is the common publish-subscribe shape the unit says every perception node in the course follows?
options: A sensor driver node publishes raw data; a perception node converts and processes it; a downstream consumer node acts on the result
A single monolithic node handles sensing, processing, and actuation together for efficiency
The consumer node publishes commands telling the perception node what to look for
Perception nodes bypass ROS topics and access sensor drivers through shared memory
explanation: The unit's opening diagram and text describe the pattern explicitly: a sensor driver publishes raw data, a perception node processes it, and a consumer (navigation, arm control, pan-tilt) uses the higher-level result — the same shape every later unit reuses.

question: Which sensor_msgs topic type does the unit say becomes relevant 'from Unit 4 onward' for surface/object recognition?
options: sensor_msgs/PointCloud2
sensor_msgs/LaserScan
sensor_msgs/CameraInfo
sensor_msgs/Image
explanation: The unit explicitly states that sensor_msgs/PointCloud2 on topics like /camera/depth/points carries 3D points and is 'used from Unit 4 onward for surface/object recognition.'

question: Why does the unit recommend converting to HSV instead of working directly in RGB for color thresholding?
options: HSV separates color (Hue) from brightness (Value), making thresholding more robust to lighting changes
HSV images use fewer bits per pixel, making cv2.inRange run faster
cv2.inRange can only operate on HSV images, never on BGR images
HSV encodes hue from 0-359 degrees, matching a standard color wheel exactly
explanation: The unit states RGB mixes color and brightness together while HSV separates Hue from Value, making it more robust to lighting changes. It also notes OpenCV's hue actually runs 0-179, not 0-359, making option D a common misconception the text corrects.

question: What is the purpose of the erode/dilate ('opening') step applied to the color mask before finding contours?
options: To remove small noise specks from the mask, since skipping it is the most common cause of jittery tracking
To smooth the raw BGR image before it is converted to HSV
To increase color contrast between the blob and the background
To merge several separate blobs into a single contour for centroid calculation
explanation: The unit states the erode/dilate pair removes small noise specks before contour-finding, and that skipping this step is 'the most common reason blob tracking looks jittery.'

question: In the proportional controller `twist.angular.z = -0.003 * error`, what does the unit say the negative sign accomplishes?
options: It ensures the robot turns toward the blob rather than away from it
It reduces angular velocity as the blob approaches the frame center
It compensates for the image's inverted vertical (y) axis
It converts the pixel error into an estimated distance to the blob
explanation: The code comment in the unit explicitly says 'negative: turn toward the blob' — the sign flips the direction of rotation relative to the sign of the pixel error, not its magnitude or axis orientation.

question: Why does the line-following pipeline crop a region of interest (ROI) near the bottom of the frame before processing?
options: It is faster and less prone to false positives from background clutter that will never contain the line
It removes the need for any HSV-based color conversion
It corrects for lens distortion that is worse near the frame edges
It guarantees the centroid aligns with the robot's physical center of mass
explanation: The unit states that processing the whole frame 'wastes cycles on sky, walls, and clutter,' and cropping a bottom strip makes subsequent steps 'both faster and less prone to false positives from background objects.'

question: What does cv2.THRESH_BINARY_INV do in this unit's line-detection example, and when should its logic be flipped?
options: It turns dark line pixels white in the mask for a dark line on a light floor; invert it if the line is light-on-dark
It converts the grayscale ROI back to color so contours can be drawn on it
It automatically distinguishes a straight line from a curved one
It filters out small noise contours below 300 pixels in area
explanation: The unit says THRESH_BINARY_INV 'makes dark line pixels become white in the mask (assuming a dark line on a light floor) — invert the logic if your line is light-on-dark.'

question: What does the unit say should happen when no contour (line) is found in a frame?
options: An explicit behavior must be defined, such as stopping or a search routine, rather than leaving the case unhandled
The robot should continue driving with its last computed angular velocity indefinitely
The ROI crop height should automatically expand to search more of the frame
The node should switch immediately to full-frame HSV color thresholding as a fallback
explanation: The unit stresses defining explicit 'line lost' behavior — stopping is safest for learning — and notes this 'what happens when perception has no answer' question recurs in later units and should be decided deliberately rather than left undefined.

question: What makes RANSAC plane segmentation robust to noisy point-cloud data, according to the unit?
options: Outlier points simply fail to vote for the winning plane, rather than dragging a least-squares fit off target
It automatically removes all points more than 1cm from the camera
It requires no distance threshold, so noise cannot affect the result
It clusters points by proximity before fitting any plane
explanation: The unit explains RANSAC repeatedly fits planes to random point triples and keeps the one with the most inliers, which is robust to noise because outliers 'simply fail to vote for the winning plane, rather than dragging a least-squares fit off target.'

question: What happens if set_distance_threshold in RANSAC plane segmentation is set too large?
options: Short objects sitting on the surface can get absorbed into the table plane itself
The table gets split into many small, separate clusters
The voxel grid filter leaf size is forced to shrink correspondingly
The Euclidean clustering step is skipped entirely
explanation: The unit states this parameter controls how far a point can be from the fitted plane and still count as part of it: 'too small splits a real table into many small clusters ... too large absorbs short objects into the table itself.'

question: Why does the Euclidean clustering step use a KD-tree, according to the unit?
options: So the algorithm doesn't need to check every point against every other point when determining what's nearby
To transform points from the camera's optical frame into base_link
To fit the dominant plane before clustering the remaining points
To compress the cloud for faster network transmission over ROS topics
explanation: The unit explains: 'The KD-tree exists so the algorithm doesn't check every point against every other point when deciding what's "nearby."'

question: According to the unit, why is a detector like YOLO used instead of the color/shape thresholding from Units 2-3?
options: Thresholding breaks down when recognizing dozens of categories under varying lighting/backgrounds, while YOLO predicts boxes and classes for a whole image in one pass
YOLO needs no camera calibration data at all, unlike thresholding
Thresholding cannot run in real time on any hardware, while YOLO always exceeds 60 fps
YOLO removes the need for the cv_bridge conversion step used in earlier units
explanation: The unit states thresholding 'breaks down the moment you need to recognize dozens of object categories under varying lighting and backgrounds,' while YOLO is 'a single-pass convolutional network that predicts bounding boxes and class labels for an entire image at once.'

question: How does this unit compute the 3D position of a YOLO-detected object from a single RGB+depth frame?
options: By looking up the depth at the bounding-box-center pixel and running the pinhole camera model backward using the camera's intrinsics
By triangulating the detection between two separate YOLO passes on the same image
By applying Euclidean clustering directly to the 2D bounding box pixels
By fitting a RANSAC plane to the bounding box region
explanation: The unit describes looking up depth at (cx, cy) and using CameraInfo intrinsics (fx, fy, principal point) to reconstruct x, y, z — 'the pinhole camera model run backward: instead of projecting a 3D point onto the image plane, you're reconstructing the 3D ray.'

question: What does the unit say must be done to the computed (x, y, z) point before it is useful to navigation or manipulation stacks?
options: Transform it from the camera's optical frame into a robot-centric frame such as base_link using tf/tf2
Convert it back into a 2D bounding box using the forward pinhole projection
Run it through RANSAC segmentation to confirm it lies on a plane
Downsample it with a voxel grid filter to reduce point density
explanation: The unit states the (x, y, z) point 'is in the camera's optical frame — use tf/tf2 to transform it into a robot-centric frame (e.g. base_link) before acting on it.'

question: What do the scaleFactor and minNeighbors parameters control in the Haar cascade detector used in this unit?
options: scaleFactor controls how thoroughly the image is scanned at different scales (a trade-off with speed); minNeighbors filters false positives by requiring multiple overlapping detections
scaleFactor sets the minimum face size in pixels; minNeighbors caps the total number of faces returned per frame
scaleFactor sets the pan/tilt controller gain; minNeighbors sets the HSV threshold range
scaleFactor converts the frame to grayscale; minNeighbors converts the frame back to color for display
explanation: The unit states 'scaleFactor controls how much the image is shrunk at each scale step (smaller = more thorough but slower); minNeighbors filters out false positives by requiring multiple overlapping detections before accepting a face.'

question: How does this unit's face-tracking approach relate to the blob tracking from Unit 2?
options: It reuses the same proportional-controller idea, reducing a detected face to a bounding-box-center point and steering error, now potentially across two axes
It replaces the proportional controller entirely with a Kalman filter for smoother motion
It tracks by matching full bounding-box overlap between frames instead of a centroid
It requires no error signal since Haar cascades output servo angles directly
explanation: The unit says tracking is done 'exactly as with the color blob in Unit 2,' reducing the face to its bounding-box center and reusing the proportional controller pattern, now with error_x/error_y driving pan and tilt.

question: What does the unit recommend when zero faces are detected for several consecutive frames?
options: Define explicit lost-detection behavior — hold last position briefly, then re-center or scan — reusing the discipline from Unit 3
Immediately switch to the Unit 7 face-recognition pipeline to reacquire identity
Increase scaleFactor sharply to force a detection on the very next frame
Stop publishing entirely until the node is manually restarted
explanation: The unit says to 'reuse the "line lost" discipline from Unit 3: define what the robot does when len(faces) == 0 for several consecutive frames (hold last position briefly, then re-center or scan).'

question: What is a face 'encoding' as described in this unit, and why is it used instead of comparing raw pixels?
options: A fixed-length numeric vector (commonly 128-d) where encodings of the same person cluster together, making it robust to changes in lighting, angle, and expression
A compressed JPEG thumbnail of the face used to save storage in the database
A bounding box plus confidence score output directly by the Haar cascade classifier
A hash of the source image file used to detect duplicate photos
explanation: The unit states pixel-by-pixel comparison is fragile because lighting, angle, and expression change raw pixels, so instead a neural network produces a fixed-length (commonly 128-dimensional) encoding where same-person faces sit close together in vector space.

question: In the face-matching logic shown in this unit, what trade-off does the 0.6 distance threshold control?
options: False accepts (recognizing the wrong person) against false rejects (failing to recognize a known person)
Detection speed against the number of faces processed per frame
The number of reference photos needed against overall accuracy
Camera frame rate against the dimensionality of the encoding vector
explanation: The unit states the 0.6 threshold 'trades off false accepts (recognizing the wrong person) against false rejects (failing to recognize a known person) — tune it against your own test set.'

question: According to the unit, how is the known-faces database typically built for a robot deployed in a fixed environment like a home or lab?
options: Built once offline from reference photos and loaded at startup, rather than learned dynamically during operation
Continuously retrained every frame from the live camera stream
Downloaded from a cloud API each time a new face is detected
Generated automatically from the COCO dataset used by YOLO in Unit 5
explanation: The unit states that for a robot in a fixed environment, 'this database is typically built once offline,' contrasting it with a more dynamic system that would add people on the fly.

question: Why does this unit pair a laser-based leg detector with a vision-based person detector rather than relying on vision alone?
options: Laser gives reliable range/bearing and works even with the person's back turned, while vision adds confirmation useful in ambiguous or crowded scenes but degrades with lighting/occlusion
Vision detectors cannot run in real time at all, so laser is required for any live tracking
The leg detector supplies identity information that vision alone cannot produce
Laser scans are required to compute the face encodings used for recognition
explanation: The unit explains leg_detector is 'robust to lighting, works even with the person's back turned,' while vision 'works well when the laser is ambiguous (e.g. crowded scenes)' — the two are fused rather than either being used alone.

question: What problem does the predict-associate-correct Kalman loop solve that pure per-frame detection cannot?
options: It maintains a tracked person's identity/continuity across frames, surviving a single missed detection instead of losing the target entirely
It removes the need for nearest-neighbor data association between detections
It converts 2D image detections directly into 3D point-cloud clusters
It eliminates the need to fuse laser and vision estimates
explanation: The unit states this loop 'is what lets tracking survive a single missed detection (occlusion, motion blur) without losing the person entirely — a strict "redetect every frame with no memory" approach loses the target the moment one frame fails.'

question: How does the unit say a 'follow me' behavior should be produced once a stable tracked (x, y) position exists?
options: Treat it as a navigation problem — publish an offset goal position or drive velocity commands proportional to distance and bearing error
Continuously re-run Haar cascade face detection to confirm identity before every movement
Recompute a RANSAC plane fit at each step of the pursuit
Discard the Kalman filter and re-detect the target from scratch every cycle
explanation: The unit states that once a tracked position exists, 'following is a navigation problem, not a perception one: publish a goal position offset from the person ... or drive velocity commands proportional to distance and bearing error.'

question: What role does the 'Behavior/coordinator node' play in the hexapod project's architecture, as distinct from the other nodes?
options: It runs a state machine (SEARCHING -> APPROACHING -> ARRIVED) that turns the tracked target position into motion commands and decides what to do if the target is lost
It performs the object detection/segmentation directly on raw point clouds
It implements the hexapod's low-level leg-gait walking controller
It smooths perception output over time using a Kalman filter before publishing PoseStamped
explanation: The unit assigns detection to the perception node, smoothing to the tracking/state node, and walking control to the gait node — the coordinator node specifically runs the SEARCHING/APPROACHING/ARRIVED state machine and handles lost-target decisions.

question: According to the unit, why does a walking gait pose a distinct perception challenge compared to a wheeled base?
options: Camera shake from the gait (yaw, sway) can make frame-to-frame jitter look like the target is moving when really the camera is moving
Legged robots cannot physically mount a depth camera due to weight limits
The hexapod's 18 degrees of freedom exceed the bandwidth of a PointCloud2 topic
Walking gaits prevent YOLO from running due to insufficient onboard compute
explanation: The unit states 'a walking gait introduces camera shake that a wheeled base doesn't have, which can make frame-to-frame jitter look like the target moving when it's really the camera moving,' recommending smoothing (moving average or Kalman filter) as mitigation.

question: What testing approach does the unit recommend before letting the coordinator drive the real gait node?
options: Validate each node in isolation, including replaying recorded perception data with rosbag to confirm the state machine transitions correctly, before wiring the full live pipeline
Deploy the complete six-node pipeline directly on the walking hexapod and debug live
Skip the tracking/state node entirely and connect perception directly to the gait node
Test only in simulation, since real hexapod hardware cannot be debugged incrementally
explanation: The unit says to validate the perception node stationary, confirm the coordinator's state machine on rosbag-replayed data, 'and only then let the coordinator drive the real gait node' — calling live full-pipeline debugging 'the slowest possible way to find a bug.'

question: According to Unit 1, what does 'manipulation' require in ROS terms?
options: A kinematic chain (arm), an end effector, a planner that computes a collision-free path, and a controller that executes it
Only a gripper and a depth camera to detect graspable objects
A SLAM stack combined with a global costmap for obstacle avoidance
A set of ROS topics and services with no additional planning or collision-checking layer
explanation: The unit defines manipulation as needing a kinematic chain, an end effector, a planner, and a controller — adding motion planning and collision checking on top of nodes/topics/TF.

question: Why does Unit 1 have you run a demo launch file before covering any concepts?
options: To confirm your ROS + MoveIt install works, using a fake ('dummy') controller so no real hardware is needed
To auto-generate a URDF for whatever robot you plan to use later in the course
To calibrate the depth sensor that later units rely on for octomap generation
To benchmark OMPL against alternative planning libraries before choosing one
explanation: The 'Confirming your environment' section says the demo launch spins up RViz with a dummy controller precisely so you can verify your setup works without needing real hardware.

question: Per Unit 1, why does this course teach MoveIt specifically rather than another framework?
options: Because it's the framework you'll meet in almost every ROS manipulation job, paper, or tutorial
Because it is the only motion planning framework that supports ROS 2
Because it eliminates the need to understand URDF or TF
Because it was designed specifically for this course's pick-and-place project
explanation: The unit explicitly says MoveIt is taught 'not because it's the only option, but because it's the framework you'll meet in almost every ROS manipulation job, paper, or tutorial.'

question: How does Unit 2 define a 'planning group'?
options: A named subset of joints that MoveIt plans for together, defined in the SRDF
A cluster of TF frames that update at the same publishing rate
A group of URDF links that share an identical collision mesh
The set of ROS nodes launched together by a single launch file
explanation: Unit 2 states a planning group is 'a named subset of joints that MoveIt plans for together, defined in the SRDF,' with typical groups being 'arm' and 'gripper'.

question: Why does Unit 2 recommend getting comfortable with tf2_echo and the frame tree viewer early on?
options: Because nearly every manipulation bug ('the arm moved to the wrong place') turns out to be a frame mix-up, not a planner bug
Because the MoveIt Setup Assistant requires tf2_echo output as an input file
Because URDF files cannot be parsed without first validating them with tf2_echo
Because controllers refuse to execute trajectories unless frames are echoed first
explanation: The unit explicitly says most manipulation bugs are frame mix-ups rather than planner bugs, which is why it stresses getting comfortable with tf2_echo and the frame viewer early.

question: According to Unit 2, what is Xacro used for?
options: Macros that expand into URDF, so you don't repeat boilerplate for every joint/link pair
A binary-compiled version of URDF that loads faster into MoveIt
A GUI tool for visually assembling planning groups without writing XML
A ros2_control plugin that commands hardware joints directly
explanation: Unit 2 states Xacro consists of 'macros that expand into URDF so you don't repeat boilerplate for every joint/link pair.'

question: What does the self-collision matrix generated by the MoveIt Setup Assistant determine?
options: Which link pairs can never collide and so should be ignored for speed
Which joints require torque limiting during trajectory execution
Which planning groups are permitted to share the same end effector
Which TF frames must be recalculated after every planning cycle
explanation: Unit 3 describes the self-collision matrix step as identifying 'which link pairs can never collide and so should be ignored for speed.'

question: Per Unit 3, why might you prefer a vendor's analytic IK plugin over KDL when configuring a planning group's kinematics solver?
options: KDL's numerical IK works for any chain but can be slow or get stuck near singularities
KDL only supports named/stored poses, not pose goals computed from perception
Analytic solvers are required for OMPL to function at all
KDL cannot be paired with a gripper end-effector group
explanation: The unit notes that KDL's numerical IK 'works for any chain but can be slow or get stuck near singularities,' so an analytic vendor plugin is preferable when available.

question: In the Setup Assistant, what does defining a 'virtual joint' configure?
options: How the robot's base attaches to the world — fixed for a bolted-down arm, floating for a mobile base
The excluded link pairs used to build the self-collision matrix
The mapping between a named pose and its stored joint values
The connection between the gripper's fingers and the wrist link
explanation: Unit 3 explains virtual joints define 'how the robot's base attaches to the world — fixed for a bolted-down arm, floating for a mobile base.'

question: According to Unit 4, why does feeding depth-sensor data into the planning scene matter?
options: It lets MoveIt build a live occupancy representation so a plan can be rejected or replanned if something now blocks it, not just what was anticipated in advance
It replaces the URDF-defined kinematic chain with a sensor-derived one
It lets the planner skip self-collision checking entirely to save compute
It automatically converts all pose goals into Cartesian path goals
explanation: Unit 4 states this is 'the difference between a robot that only avoids obstacles a programmer anticipated and one that avoids obstacles it's currently looking at,' via a live occupancy model.

question: Per Unit 4, what is the trade-off of choosing a finer octomap_resolution (smaller voxel size)?
options: It catches smaller obstacles but costs more compute to update and plan against
It removes the need to configure a self-collision matrix
It automatically extends the depth sensor's max_range
It disables PointCloudOctomapUpdater in favor of DepthImageOctomapUpdater
explanation: The unit states finer resolution 'catches smaller obstacles but costs more compute,' while coarser resolution is faster to update and plan against.

question: Per Unit 4, if planned trajectories keep passing straight through a known obstacle, what's the first thing to check?
options: Whether the topic name in the sensor's publisher matches the topic named in sensors_3d.yaml
Whether a virtual joint was defined for the sensor's mounting link
Whether the gripper's self-collision pairs were excluded correctly
Whether the named 'home' pose was saved in the SRDF
explanation: The unit calls topic name mismatch between the sensor's publisher and sensors_3d.yaml 'the most common wiring bug' to check first.

question: In the Python planning sequence described in Unit 5, what happens when plan() fails?
options: The node logs the failure and skips the execute call
The controller automatically retries with a different planner
The last successful trajectory is executed instead
The pose goal is silently converted into a joint goal and re-planned
explanation: The sequence diagram and text show that on plan failure the node logs it and skips execute, rather than retrying or falling back automatically.

question: According to Unit 5, when is a pose goal the more natural choice than a joint goal?
options: When the target comes from perception, e.g. a detected object's pose, since MoveIt runs IK internally
When you need a deterministic result that avoids IK ambiguity entirely
When the goal is a fixed waypoint you reuse constantly across the whole project
When you want to skip the plan() call and execute directly
explanation: Unit 5 says pose goals are 'most natural when the goal comes from perception,' while joint goals are the deterministic, IK-ambiguity-avoiding choice for known configurations.

question: Why does Unit 5 recommend Cartesian path planning for a task like sliding along a table surface?
options: Free planning finds a collision-free path but doesn't guarantee a straight line, while Cartesian planning interpolates waypoints and follows them directly via IK
Cartesian path planning is the only mode that supports named/stored goals
Free planning cannot use OMPL and requires a separate planner library
Cartesian path planning skips the plan-success check entirely before executing
explanation: The unit explains free planning finds 'a' collision-free path without guaranteeing straightness, whereas Cartesian planning interpolates waypoints and asks IK to follow them directly.

question: What does the moveit_msgs/Grasp message bundle together, per Unit 6?
options: The grasp pose plus pre-grasp/grasp posture (gripper joint states) and approach/retreat directions
Only the final joint angles of the arm at the moment of grasping
The planning scene's octomap together with the target CollisionObject
The named SRDF pose and the controller name used to execute it
explanation: Unit 6 describes the Grasp message as bundling 'the grasp pose plus pre-grasp/grasp posture (gripper joint states) and approach/retreat directions' for pick() to consume directly.

question: Why does Unit 6 have the retreat pose move straight back along the approach axis after grasping?
options: To avoid dragging the object through other geometry
Because the Setup Assistant only permits linear retreat vectors
Because IK cannot be solved for poses off the approach axis
To automatically trigger detach_object once the retreat completes
explanation: The unit states the retreat pose is 'usually straight back along the approach axis, to avoid dragging the object through other geometry.'

question: According to Unit 6, what happens when attach_object() is called on the planning scene interface?
options: The object's collision geometry is added to the robot's own collision model, excluded from self-collision checks against the fingers holding it
The object is removed from the planning scene entirely until detach_object() is called
The controller switches from position control to effort control for the carry
The object's grasp pose is recomputed using a learned grasp generator
explanation: Unit 6 explains attaching 'adds the object's collision geometry to the robot's own collision model (excluded from self-collision checks against the fingers holding it) for the duration of the carry.'

question: Per Unit 7, at which milestone does an under-sized collision margin around the carried object tend to surface as near-misses?
options: Transit to place pose (milestone 4)
Scene setup (milestone 1)
Grasp + attach (milestone 3)
Return home (milestone 6)
explanation: The unit specifically flags milestone 4, transit to the place pose with the object attached, as where an under-sized collision margin tends to surface as near-misses.

question: Why does Unit 7 recommend building and verifying each milestone independently rather than writing one monolithic script?
options: It's good engineering practice and the fastest way to debug when something goes wrong later
MoveIt's API only permits a single plan/execute call per node lifetime
Each milestone requires its own separate MoveIt configuration package
The Setup Assistant cannot generate more than one named pose at a time
explanation: The unit states verifying each stage independently 'is both good engineering practice and the fastest way to debug when something goes wrong later.'

question: What simplification does the Unit 7 project brief explicitly allow regarding the object's location?
options: Treating 'known location' as acceptable, rather than requiring a full object detector built from scratch
Skipping collision checking during transit as long as the object is lightweight
Assuming the gripper never needs to retreat after grasping
Allowing the arm to skip the return-home stage if placement succeeds
explanation: The brief states to 'treat "known location" as an acceptable simplification for this project — the goal is to prove out the manipulation pipeline, not to also build an object detector from scratch.'

question: According to the unit, which parts of the manipulation problem does MoveIt2 actually own, versus merely integrate with?
options: MoveIt2 owns kinematics and motion planning; it integrates with (but does not replace) perception and gripper control
MoveIt2 owns perception and gripper control; it integrates with kinematics and motion planning
MoveIt2 owns all four problems, including perception and gripper drivers
MoveIt2 owns only motion planning; kinematics is handled by a separate ROS 2 framework
explanation: The unit states MoveIt2 'owns the first two' (kinematics and motion planning) — 'It does not replace perception or gripper drivers — it integrates with them.'

question: How does the unit define "manipulation" as distinct from navigation?
options: Manipulation is changing the state of the environment through contact (grasping, moving, placing, releasing objects), while navigation is just moving the robot itself
Manipulation only applies to mobile robots, while navigation only applies to fixed manipulator arms
Manipulation is any task that uses inverse kinematics, while navigation only uses forward kinematics
Manipulation always requires a depth camera, while navigation never involves perception
explanation: The unit defines manipulation as 'a robot changing the state of its environment through contact... as opposed to just moving itself around (that's navigation).'

question: Per the unit, what three things does launching a demo configuration package's `demo.launch.py` bring up at once?
options: The move_group node, RViz2 with the MoveIt2 Motion Planning plugin, and (in simulation) a virtual robot to move
The move_group node, a Gazebo physics world, and a gripper action server
RViz2, rqt_joint_trajectory_controller, and the MoveIt2 Setup Assistant
The Setup Assistant, a URDF/XACRO loader, and the colcon build system
explanation: The unit says demo.launch.py 'brings up three things at once: the move_group node..., RViz2 with the MoveIt2 Motion Planning plugin loaded, and (in simulation) a virtual robot to move.'

question: What is the SRDF, and why does MoveIt2 need one in addition to the robot's URDF?
options: A Semantic Robot Description Format file that encodes planning groups, disabled collision pairs, the end effector link, and named poses that have no natural home in a URDF
A binary mesh format that replaces the URDF entirely once the Setup Assistant finishes
A ros2_control hardware interface file listing each joint's velocity and effort limits
An alternate XACRO file used only for Gazebo simulation, not for planning
explanation: The unit says MoveIt2 needs semantic information 'that has no natural home in a URDF... That information lives in an SRDF (Semantic Robot Description Format) file.'

question: Why does the Setup Assistant generate a self-collision matrix by sampling random joint configurations?
options: To disable collision checking between link pairs that can never physically collide or that always collide (adjacent overlapping meshes), avoiding wasted computation during planning
To automatically determine which joints belong to the arm versus the gripper planning group
To calculate the maximum reachable workspace so pose goals outside it are rejected early
To generate the virtual joint that attaches the robot's root link to the world frame
explanation: The unit explains the matrix 'disables collision checking between link pairs that either can never physically collide... or always collide... This matters for performance — checking every link pair... is wasted work.'

question: In the Setup Assistant's controller step, what does mapping a planning group to a ros2_control controller accomplish, and what action interface is typically used?
options: It bridges MoveIt2's planned trajectories to the robot's real or simulated actuators, typically via a FollowJointTrajectory action interface
It bridges MoveIt2's planned trajectories to actuators via a GripperCommand service call
It selects which kinematics solver plugin (e.g., KDL or IKFast) will be used for that group
It defines the named robot poses, such as "home" and "ready", for that group
explanation: The unit states you map each planning group to 'the ros2_control controller (typically a FollowJointTrajectory action interface) that will actually execute its trajectories — this is the bridge between MoveIt2's planned trajectories and your robot's real or simulated actuators.'

question: Why does the unit say `plan()` and `execute()` are deliberately kept as two separate calls on `MoveGroupInterface`?
options: So you can inspect or reject a plan (e.g., check its length or sanity-check the path) before committing the robot to actually move
Because the move_group action server technically cannot combine planning and execution into one request
So the gripper can be commanded independently while the arm trajectory executes
Because the kinematics plugin has to be reloaded between the planning and execution phases
explanation: The unit says: 'Splitting plan from execute is deliberate: it lets you inspect or reject a plan... before committing the robot to actually moving.'

question: What is the essential difference between a joint-space goal and a pose goal when using `MoveGroupInterface`?
options: A joint-space goal specifies a target angle for every joint directly, while a pose goal specifies a target end-effector position/orientation and lets the IK solver determine the joint angles
A joint-space goal only applies to the gripper planning group, while pose goals only apply to the arm group
A pose goal guarantees a straight-line Cartesian path is taken, while a joint-space goal does not
A joint-space goal requires calling computeCartesianPath(), while a pose goal only requires setPoseTarget()
explanation: The unit defines joint-space goals as specifying 'a target angle for every joint directly' and pose goals as specifying 'a target position and orientation for the end effector, and let the inverse kinematics solver figure out the joint angles.' Neither guarantees a straight-line path — that's the subject of Unit 4.

question: What tool does the unit recommend for manually testing that your joints move correctly through `ros2_control`, without writing any planning code?
options: rqt_joint_trajectory_controller, which gives sliders to directly command each joint
The MoveIt2 Setup Assistant's built-in test panel
RViz2's Motion Planning plugin interactive markers
A GripperCommand action client called from the command line
explanation: The unit says: 'rqt_joint_trajectory_controller gives you sliders to directly command each joint through your ros2_control controllers — useful for confirming your hardware/simulation moves correctly before you start planning against it programmatically.'

question: What does the `fraction` value returned by `computeCartesianPath()` mean, and what should you do if it comes back below 1.0 near the edge of the workspace?
options: It's the portion of the requested path actually achieved; a value below 1.0 near workspace edges is normal — shorten the waypoint list or move the start pose
It's the percentage of joints that reached their commanded target; retry the same waypoints with a different kinematics plugin
It's the IK solver's confidence score; a value below 1.0 means the plugin must be replaced with IKFast
It's the fraction of the path that is collision-free; a value below 1.0 means an obstacle must be removed from the planning scene
explanation: The unit states the fraction 'tells you how much of the requested path was actually achievable' and 'it's normal for fraction to come back less than 1.0 near the edges of the workspace — treat that as a signal to shorten the waypoint list or move the start pose, not as a bug.'

question: Why does the unit describe Cartesian path planning as more restrictive than pose-goal planning?
options: Because the path is fixed, leaving the IK solver less freedom to avoid joint limits or singularities along the way
Because Cartesian planning cannot use the default kdl_kinematics_plugin and always requires IKFast
Because Cartesian planning only works within the gripper planning group, never the arm group
Because Cartesian planning requires regenerating the SRDF through the Setup Assistant each time
explanation: The unit states: 'Cartesian planning is more restrictive than pose-goal planning: because the path is fixed, there's less freedom for the IK solver to avoid joint limits or singularities along the way.'

question: How does the unit compare the default `kdl_kinematics_plugin` to an analytic solver like IKFast?
options: KDL is a general-purpose numerical solver that works with any kinematic chain but can be slow and occasionally fail to converge near singularities, whereas IKFast is generated specifically for a robot's own kinematic chain
KDL only supports Cartesian path planning, while IKFast only supports joint-space planning
KDL requires hand-tuning kinematics.yaml on every planning call, while IKFast configures itself automatically
KDL cannot be swapped for another plugin, while IKFast can be selected per planning group
explanation: The unit describes kdl_kinematics_plugin as 'a general-purpose numerical solver that works with any kinematic chain but can be slow and occasionally fails to converge near singularities,' contrasted with 'analytic solvers like IKFast (generated specifically for your robot's kinematic chain).'

question: Why does `simple_grasping` expose object detection as a ROS 2 action rather than a plain service call?
options: Because detection can take a noticeable, variable amount of time, and an action provides feedback and cancellation that a blocking service call wouldn't
Because actions are required whenever a node later calls tf2 to transform a pose
Because MoveGroupInterface only accepts goals delivered through actions, never through services
Because services are incapable of returning a list containing more than one detected object
explanation: The unit says detection is exposed as an action 'appropriate because detection can take a noticeable, variable amount of time, and an action gives you feedback and cancellation instead of a blocking service call.'

question: Before a detected object's pose can be handed to `MoveGroupInterface`, what must be done to it and why?
options: It must be transformed with tf2 from the camera's optical frame into the robot's base/world frame, since MoveIt2 plans in the robot's base frame, not the camera frame
It must be converted from a PoseStamped into a joint-space target using the kinematics plugin
It must first be cropped to a region of interest and clustered before any planning can occur
It must be rescaled using the eef_step resolution parameter from computeCartesianPath()
explanation: The unit states: 'The detection is in the camera's optical frame; MoveIt2 plans in the robot's base or world frame. Use tf2 to transform the pose before handing it to MoveGroupInterface.'

question: According to the unit, what preprocessing steps does `simple_grasping` apply to the raw point cloud before returning detected objects?
options: Cropping to a region of interest, plane segmentation to remove the table surface, and clustering the remaining points
Generating a self-collision matrix followed by attaching a virtual joint to the world frame
Transforming the cloud into the base_link frame, then running inverse kinematics on each cluster
Downsampling the cloud to match the eef_step resolution, then interpolating a Cartesian path through it
explanation: The unit says reading the detection code 'clarifies exactly what preprocessing happens to the raw point cloud (cropping to a region of interest, plane segmentation to remove the table surface, clustering the remaining points) before objects come out the other end.'

question: What kind of robot does this course build across all its units?
options: A differential-drive mobile robot with two independently driven wheels plus a caster, an SBC running ROS 2, a LiDAR, and a camera
A four-wheel skid-steer rover with mecanum wheels
A stationary robotic arm mounted on a workbench
A legged hexapod robot with six independently actuated legs
explanation: Unit 1 states the course builds 'a small differential-drive mobile robot — two independently driven wheels plus a caster, a single-board computer running ROS 2, a LiDAR, and a camera.'

question: Why does the unit warn against skipping ahead to URDF (Unit 7) before finishing the motors and computer units?
options: Because each unit adds exactly one layer on top of the previous one, so you'd end up modeling a robot you can't yet actually drive
Because URDF files require a compiled ROS 2 workspace to even open in a text editor
Because the course's mermaid diagrams are rendered in strict alphabetical order
Because CAD software cannot import URDF without a working motor driver node running
explanation: The unit explicitly says 'skipping ahead to, say, URDF before the motors and computer are sorted will leave you modeling a robot you can't yet actually drive,' since the sequence is dependency-driven, not alphabetical.

question: Which debugging habit does the introduction recommend building early, given that robotics debugging is often version-specific?
options: Reading error messages fully before searching for them, and keeping a running notes file of part numbers and firmware versions
Always reflashing the onboard computer from scratch whenever an error appears
Skipping the failing unit and returning to it only after finishing the whole course
Relying exclusively on forum posts rather than official error text
explanation: The unit says the two most useful early habits are 'reading error messages fully before searching for them, and keeping a running notes file of every part number and firmware version you use.'

question: In the motor driver hardware described, what specifically controls the motor's speed?
options: PWM duty cycle, which controls the average voltage the motor sees
Which pair of H-bridge switches is enabled
The encoder's counts-per-revolution resolution
The gauge of wire used between the battery and the driver
explanation: The unit states speed is set by PWM, where 'the duty cycle (fraction of time on) controls the average voltage the motor sees, and therefore its speed,' while direction (not speed) is set by which H-bridge switch pair is enabled.

question: Why can't a microcontroller's GPIO pin drive a DC motor directly, according to the unit?
options: It can't supply enough current, and it has no way to reverse the motor's direction
GPIO pins output an AC signal that DC motors cannot use
GPIO pins are incompatible with quadrature encoder feedback
It would immediately overheat the microcontroller's voltage regulator
explanation: The unit says a motor driver is needed because a GPIO pin 'cannot drive a DC motor directly — it can't supply enough current, and it has no way to reverse the motor's direction.'

question: What happens if you accidentally swap a motor's two power leads during wiring, per the unit?
options: The robot spins the wrong way, which can be fixed in software by flipping a sign or by physically swapping the leads
The encoder immediately stops producing A/B tick signals
The motor driver's H-bridge is permanently damaged
The robot's top-speed calculation from the datasheet becomes invalid
explanation: The unit lists swapped motor power leads as one of the two most common wiring mistakes, causing the robot to 'spin the wrong way,' fixable by flipping a sign in software or swapping the leads physically.

question: Why does the unit say battery capacity and discharge rating must be matched to peak current draw, not just typical draw?
options: A battery with too low a C-rating sags under load exactly when the motors need the most current
Typical current draw is always numerically higher than peak draw for mobile robots
Peak draw only affects long-term battery lifespan, not immediate performance
Regulators cannot handle typical draw unless peak draw is specified first
explanation: The unit warns to 'match capacity (mAh) and discharge rating (C-rating) to your peak current draw, not just typical draw — a battery with too low a C-rating sags under load exactly when the motors need the most current.'

question: In the recommended power distribution path, what is the correct order of components starting from the raw battery?
options: Power switch, then fuse, then voltage regulator(s), then the motor driver/computer/sensors
Fuse, then power switch, then voltage regulator, then the motor driver/computer/sensors
Voltage regulator, then fuse, then power switch, then the motor driver/computer/sensors
Power switch, then voltage regulator, then fuse, then the motor driver/computer/sensors
explanation: The unit's diagram and wiring description both show: Battery → Power switch → Fuse → Voltage regulator(s) → motor driver / onboard computer / sensors.

question: What is the specific purpose of the inline fuse placed between the battery and the rest of the circuit?
options: So a wiring fault trips the fuse instead of stressing the battery, with the fuse sized just above peak current draw
To convert battery voltage down to the 5V logic level needed by the computer
To measure amp-hours consumed so runtime can be tracked in software
To prevent LiPo cells from being discharged below their minimum safe cell voltage
explanation: The unit recommends 'an inline fuse (or resettable polyfuse) between the battery and the rest of the circuit sized just above your peak current, so a wiring fault trips the fuse instead of the battery.'

question: Why does the unit recommend installing ROS 2 from the official APT repositories rather than building it from source?
options: It's faster and matches what most tutorials and packages expect
Ubuntu Server does not support compiling any software from source
APT installation is the only method that includes ros-base
Source builds are incompatible with headless (no desktop) images
explanation: The unit states to 'Install ROS 2 from the official APT repositories for your Ubuntu release rather than building from source — it's faster and matches what most tutorials and packages expect.'

question: Why is an underpowered power module for the compute board a particularly tricky problem to diagnose?
options: It causes random reboots under load, which looks like a software bug but isn't
It silently corrupts the flashed Ubuntu Server image on the SD card
It prevents SSH access while leaving the board otherwise fully functional
It causes ros2 doctor --report to hang indefinitely
explanation: The unit warns that 'an underpowered supply causes random reboots under load, which looks like a software bug but isn't,' making it important to verify the power module's specs match the board.

question: What is the stated purpose of running `ros2 doctor --report` immediately after installing ROS 2?
options: It flags common environment problems, like missing environment variables or network configuration issues, before they cause confusing bugs later
It benchmarks CPU performance to predict navigation stack frame rates
It automatically resolves and installs any missing ROS 2 dependencies
It verifies that the motor driver and battery wiring are correctly connected
explanation: The unit says `ros2 doctor` 'flags common environment problems (missing environment variables, network configuration issues) before they surface confusingly three units from now.'

question: In the twist_to_wheel_speeds kinematics function, what happens to v_left and v_right as angular velocity omega increases while linear velocity v stays constant?
options: v_right increases and v_left decreases, producing a turn
Both v_left and v_right increase by the same amount
Both v_left and v_right decrease by the same amount
Neither wheel speed changes, since omega only affects heading, not wheel speed
explanation: The code computes v_left = v - (omega * wheel_separation / 2.0) and v_right = v + (omega * wheel_separation / 2.0), so increasing omega raises v_right and lowers v_left.

question: Why does the motor_driver_node use declare_parameter for serial_port and wheel_separation instead of hardcoding those values?
options: Hardcoding them would make the package unreusable the moment the wiring or chassis changes; parameters let you override them at launch time
ROS 2 node classes are not permitted to contain hardcoded string or float literals
colcon build --symlink-install fails to build packages containing hardcoded values
Parameters allow the values to be changed live while the node is spinning, which literals cannot do
explanation: The unit says hardcoding 'makes the package unreusable the moment your wiring or chassis changes; parameters let you override them at launch time instead' — the point is launch-time flexibility, not live runtime editing.

question: Why is the bringup package kept separate from motor_driver rather than folding the launch file into the motor_driver package?
options: Later units will add more nodes to the same launch file without touching driver code at all
ament_python build type does not support packages that contain launch files
ROS 2 requires launch files to always live in a package literally named 'bringup'
motor_driver already has too many dependencies to also hold a launch file
explanation: The unit explains that keeping bringup separate matters 'because later units will add more nodes to this same launch file without touching driver code at all — bringup is where the whole robot gets assembled.'

question: What bottom-up order does the unit recommend for building the CAD assembly?
options: Chassis base plate, then motors, then wheels, then battery, then compute board, then sensor mounts
Sensor mounts, then compute board, then battery, then wheels, then motors, then chassis base plate
Motors, then chassis base plate, then battery, then wheels, then compute board, then sensor mounts
Battery, then chassis base plate, then motors, then compute board, then wheels, then sensor mounts
explanation: The unit's diagram and text both specify building 'bottom-up... start from the chassis base plate as your fixed reference, then bring in and position one component at a time (motors, then wheels, then the battery, then the compute board, then sensor mounts).'

question: Why does the unit recommend mating wheels with a revolute joint rather than a fastened one?
options: It allows rotation about an axis so you can check ground clearance and rotation in CAD, and it's the same relationship you'll encode as a URDF joint in Unit 7
Revolute mates are the only mate type supported for manufacturer-provided 3D models
A fastened mate would hide the wheel from the assembly's visual rendering
Revolute mates are required before a part can be extruded from a 2D sketch
explanation: The unit says mating wheels with revolute joints 'is what lets you later drag them in the CAD tool to sanity-check ground clearance... the same revolute relationship is also what you'll encode in the URDF joint in Unit 7.'

question: Which CAD habit does the unit single out as key to avoiding parts that don't physically fit?
options: Modeling to real component dimensions pulled from datasheets rather than round numbers
Always designing every part directly inside the assembly context, never separately
Avoiding parametric dimensions since they complicate hole placement
Using only manufacturer-supplied 3D models and never hand-modeling any part
explanation: The unit states: 'Model to real component dimensions, not round numbers... "close enough" holes mean parts that don't fit.'

question: Why is a drive wheel's joint typically given type="continuous" instead of type="revolute"?
options: Continuous allows free rotation with no limits, appropriate for a drive wheel, whereas revolute is bounded rotation like an arm joint
Continuous joints are the only type that can have an <axis> element defined
Revolute joints cannot be visualized correctly in RViz
Continuous is simply the default type applied when a joint's type attribute is left blank
explanation: The unit explains that 'continuous... means it can rotate freely without limits — the natural choice for a drive wheel, as opposed to revolute (bounded rotation, like a robot arm joint) or fixed (no motion at all).'

question: What is the functional difference between a link's <visual> and <collision> elements in a URDF?
options: <visual> is what's rendered on screen, while <collision> is used by physics/planning for contact checks and is often deliberately simpler
<visual> defines mass and inertia, while <collision> defines the rendered mesh
<collision> is purely cosmetic and optional, while <visual> is mandatory for the URDF to parse
They must always describe identical geometry or robot_state_publisher will refuse to start
explanation: The unit states '<visual> is what's rendered on screen... <collision> is what physics/planning uses for contact checks (often deliberately simpler than <visual> for performance).'

question: Why does robot_state_publisher publish a fixed joint (like a chassis-mounted LiDAR) on /tf_static rather than /tf?
options: Because fixed joints never change, so they only need to be published once instead of continuously
Because /tf_static is reserved exclusively for the base_link frame itself
Because robot_state_publisher cannot track any joint that lacks /joint_states data at all
Because /tf only accepts transforms that originate from a continuous-type joint
explanation: The unit says 'Fixed joints (like a LiDAR bolted to the chassis) are published once on /tf_static since they never change; moving joints (wheels) are republished continuously on /tf.'

question: Why does the unit recommend creating a udev rule for the LiDAR's serial device?
options: Default /dev/ttyUSBx names can shift based on plug-in order or reboot, silently breaking launch files that hardcode a specific device path
udev rules are required before Linux will recognize any USB-to-serial device
It increases the LiDAR's effective angular resolution and scan rate
It lets two ROS 2 driver packages share the same physical serial port at once
explanation: The unit explains that without a udev rule, 'your LiDAR might jump to /dev/ttyUSB1, silently breaking any launch file that hardcodes /dev/ttyUSB0,' and the rule fixes this with a persistent symlink.

question: Why does the unit recommend using an existing manufacturer or community ROS 2 driver rather than writing a LiDAR driver from scratch?
options: Correctly parsing the LiDAR's raw serial protocol (checksums, partial-packet framing, intensity data) is fiddly work that's almost always already been done well
ROS 2 forbids custom nodes from publishing sensor_msgs/LaserScan directly
Writing a custom driver would require rewriting the udev rule for every reboot
Only the manufacturer's driver package can be added to a bringup launch file
explanation: The unit states that 'parsing a LiDAR's raw serial protocol correctly (handling checksums, partial-packet framing, intensity data) is fiddly work that's almost always already been done well.'

question: According to the unit, which LiDAR spec bounds how quickly the robot can react to newly appearing obstacles, especially at speed?
options: Scan rate (Hz) — how many full rotations per second
Angular resolution — samples per rotation
Minimum reliable range
Interface type (USB-to-serial vs. other)
explanation: The unit says scan rate 'bounds how quickly your robot can react to new obstacles, especially at speed,' distinguishing it from angular resolution (obstacle/wall detection fidelity) and range (detection distance).

question: Why does a CSI-connected raspicam typically have lower latency and CPU overhead than a comparable USB webcam?
options: CSI bypasses the USB stack entirely
CSI cameras include built-in hardware image compression that USB webcams lack
CSI operates at a higher voltage than USB, allowing faster data transfer
USB webcams require an extra ROS 2 node just to decode raw frames
explanation: The unit lists 'Low latency, dedicated interface — CSI bypasses the USB stack entirely, which matters once you're doing anything reactive with vision' as a key reason raspicam is a common first choice.

question: Why does the unit emphasize standardizing on sensor_msgs/Image and CameraInfo output from the camera driver?
options: Every downstream perception tool (OpenCV bridges, calibration tools, object detection packages) expects these types, avoiding custom glue code
It is the only message type combination that rqt_image_view is able to display
It is a hard requirement for a driver to compile against rclpy
It guarantees the camera reaches 30 frames per second regardless of hardware
explanation: The unit says these standard types matter 'because every downstream perception tool... expects them, so you never have to write custom glue code just to move pixels from the driver to the rest of your stack.'

question: What does the unit suggest doing if v4l2_camera doesn't cooperate with your specific raspicam/OS combination?
options: Try a libcamera-aware ROS 2 camera package instead, since the goal either way is the same standard Image/CameraInfo output
Reflash the SBC with a desktop Ubuntu image instead of Ubuntu Server
Physically replace the CSI ribbon connector with a USB adapter cable
Disable /camera_info publishing so the driver only needs to handle /image_raw
explanation: The unit states: 'If v4l2_camera doesn't cooperate with your specific camera/OS combination, a libcamera-aware ROS 2 camera package is the alternative path.'

question: What does the unit identify as a common cause of a simulated robot flying apart or falling through the floor as soon as physics starts?
options: A link with zero or missing mass/inertia values in its <inertial> block
The <visual> geometry being more detailed than the <collision> geometry
The diff_drive plugin being subscribed to the wrong topic name
The URDF using continuous joints instead of revolute joints for the wheels
explanation: The unit warns that 'a link with zero or missing mass/inertia is a common cause of a simulated robot that flies apart or falls through the floor the instant physics starts running.'

question: Why can the same navigation and teleop nodes drive both the real robot and the simulated one with zero code changes?
options: The Gazebo plugins publish and subscribe to the exact same ROS 2 topics (e.g. /cmd_vel, /scan) as the real hardware nodes
Gazebo automatically converts real hardware driver source code into simulation plugins
ROS 2 nodes detect at runtime whether they are connected to simulated or real hardware
The URDF format used for simulation is functionally different and handles translation automatically
explanation: The unit states this is 'the key idea behind simulating with ROS 2 specifically: because the plugin speaks the same topics as your real hardware nodes... everything you built... works against the simulated robot with zero code changes.'

question: Why does the unit recommend keeping sim.launch.py as a separate file from robot.launch.py rather than one file with conditionals?
options: It makes it obvious at a glance which one you're running and keeps hardware-specific parameters out of a file that never touches real hardware
ROS 2 launch files are technically incapable of containing conditional logic
Gazebo requires a proprietary launch file format that's incompatible with real-hardware launch files
Simulated sensors publish different message types than real ones, which would break a shared launch file
explanation: The unit says keeping the files separate 'makes it obvious at a glance which one you're running, and keeps hardware-specific parameters (serial ports, camera device paths) out of a file that never touches real hardware.'

question: Why does the unit recommend printing a small test coupon before committing to a full enclosure print?
options: A two-hour test print that reveals a bad tolerance is far cheaper than an eight-hour full print that doesn't fit
Test coupons are required to calibrate the 3D printer's bed leveling before any print
A full enclosure cannot include screw bosses unless a coupon is printed first
The coupon determines which material, PLA or PETG, the printer is physically capable of extruding
explanation: The unit concludes: 'a two-hour test print that reveals a bad tolerance is far cheaper than an eight-hour full enclosure print that doesn't fit.'

question: Why should a fully sealed enclosure with no ventilation be avoided, per the unit?
options: It can cause thermal throttling or shutdowns of the compute board and motor driver under sustained load
It prevents the LiDAR from reaching its rated angular resolution
It blocks the udev-assigned serial symlinks from being created correctly
It adds enough weight to noticeably reduce battery runtime
explanation: The unit warns to 'leave clearance around anything that generates heat or needs airflow, particularly the compute board and motor driver — a fully sealed enclosure with no ventilation can cause thermal throttling or shutdowns under sustained load.'

question: Why does the unit recommend orienting a part expected to bend or snap so the load runs along, not across, the print layers?
options: FDM parts are weakest across layer lines, which run perpendicular to the print bed
Overhangs beyond 45 degrees only ever occur across layer lines
PLA only bonds properly to itself along the direction of the layers
Loads applied across layers cause the print to require more support material
explanation: The unit states: 'parts are weakest across layer lines (perpendicular to the print bed), so orient any part that will experience bending or snapping force so the load runs along, not across, the layers.'

question: According to Unit 1, why does the course build a simulation (Unit 3) before connecting to the physical robot (Unit 4)?
options: Because the physical robot kit takes longer to ship than the software setup
Because a bug in simulation costs a restarted process instead of a robot driving off a table
Because ROS cannot compile motor driver nodes without a simulated model present
Because the physical robot's battery must be charged for several days before first use
explanation: Unit 1 explicitly says this ordering is deliberate: every piece of logic should be exercised in simulation first, 'where a bug costs you a restarted process instead of a robot driving off a table.'

question: Unit 1 explains why the motor driver is built as a ROS node instead of a one-off Python script. What reason does it give?
options: ROS nodes execute faster than plain Python scripts on the same hardware
A ROS node exposes a standard topic/service interface that later units can call without knowing about GPIO pins or PWM duty cycles
Plain scripts are incapable of reading camera data
ROS automatically prevents wiring mistakes that scripts cannot detect
explanation: The unit's stated reason is reuse and composability: wrapping the driver as a node gives every later unit a standard interface without needing to know GPIO/PWM details.

question: What classic-ROS tooling does this course use, as distinguished from ROS 2?
options: colcon workspaces with rclpy/rclcpp and ros2 launch
catkin workspaces with rospy/roscpp and roslaunch
ament workspaces with rclpy and ros2 run
bazel workspaces with rospy and roslaunch
explanation: Unit 1 states the course 'uses classic ROS (catkin workspaces, rospy/roscpp, roslaunch) as its plumbing,' predating ROS 2's dominance.

question: Per Unit 2, what is described as the single most common cause of 'the motors just don't respond' bugs?
options: A missing common ground between the SBC and the driver board
An undercharged battery pack
Over-tightened screws on the chassis mounts
Using the wrong PWM frequency in the driver code
explanation: The unit states that a missing shared ground between the SBC and driver board 'is the single most common cause of "the motors just don't respond" bugs.'

question: What is the first step in the wiring order Unit 2 recommends to minimize the risk of 'magic smoke'?
options: Connect the SBC's GPIO control pins to the driver board first to test logic signals
Connect the motors to the driver board's motor outputs first
Wire battery to voltage regulator to driver board power input, confirming voltage with a multimeter before connecting anything else
Establish the common ground connection before any power wiring
explanation: Unit 2 lists the wiring order as: battery to regulator to driver board power input (multimeter-checked) first, then motors, then SBC GPIO, then common ground.

question: When checking that both wheels spin freely with no motor power applied, what does binding at this stage actually indicate, according to Unit 2?
options: A weak motor that will need replacing
An alignment problem in the mechanical assembly, not a motor power issue
A firmware bug in the SBC's GPIO driver
Insufficient voltage from the regulator
explanation: The unit states: 'binding at this stage means an alignment problem now, not a "weak motor" problem later.'

question: Unit 3 lists three things a simulator gives you that the real robot can't. Which set matches the unit's text?
options: Infinite retries, perfect ground-truth pose, and the ability to develop with no robot present
Precise wheel odometry, unlimited battery life, and remote network access
Automatic sensor calibration, gravity compensation, and collision-free motion
Faster processing, built-in path planning, and wireless connectivity
explanation: The unit says a simulator gives 'infinite retries (no motors to burn out), a perfect ground-truth pose... and the ability to develop on a laptop with no robot present at all.'

question: According to Unit 3, what happens if a URDF robot model has only <visual> geometry and no <collision> geometry?
options: The robot renders correctly but falls through the simulated floor
The simulator refuses to spawn the model at all
The robot appears invisible in Gazebo but still has physics
The differential-drive plugin fails to load
explanation: The unit warns: 'a robot with only visual geometry will fall through the simulated floor,' which is why collision geometry must be added alongside visual geometry.

question: How does Unit 3 define URDF?
options: A ROS launch file format used to start simulation nodes
An XML dialect describing a robot's links (rigid bodies) and joints (how they connect and move)
A binary format for storing physics-engine collision meshes
A YAML file format for camera calibration parameters
explanation: Unit 3 defines URDF (Unified Robot Description Format) as 'an XML dialect' describing a robot's links and joints before a simulator can render or physically simulate it.

question: Why does Unit 4 recommend debugging the ROS network link over Ethernet before ever touching WiFi?
options: Ethernet is required to flash firmware onto the SBC
It removes variables like signal strength and DHCP lease timing from the problem
WiFi adapters cannot carry ROS message traffic
roscore will not start unless a wired connection is detected first
explanation: The unit states debugging at the Ethernet layer first 'removes an entire class of variables (signal strength, DHCP lease timing) from the problem.'

question: In the classic-ROS setup described in Unit 4, which machine should usually host the roscore master, and why?
options: The laptop, because it's easier to set ROS_MASTER_URI locally
The robot, so the ROS graph stays operational even if the laptop disconnects
Whichever machine has the faster CPU
Both machines simultaneously, using two separate masters
