question: Why does a convolutional layer typically have far fewer parameters than a Dense layer applied to the same-size input?
options: Convolutional layers store weights as integers instead of floats
Pooling layers remove most parameters before training starts
Convolutional layers only process half of the input pixels by default
The same filter weights are reused at every position across the image
explanation: The unit states: 'Because the same filter weights are reused at every position, a convolutional layer has vastly fewer parameters than a Dense layer would for the same input size.'

question: What problem does non-maximum suppression (NMS) solve in a YOLO-based detection pipeline?
options: It boosts every detection's confidence score before publishing
It discards overlapping, lower-confidence boxes so only the best box remains per detected object
It converts bounding boxes into human pose keypoints
It resizes the incoming camera frame to the network's expected input size
explanation: The unit explains that because many cells and candidate boxes fire near the same real object, NMS 'discards overlapping lower-confidence boxes, keeping only the best box per detected object.'

question: Per this unit, why is YOLO much faster than sliding a classifier over every window and scale of an image?
options: YOLO always processes images at a lower resolution than a classifier would
YOLO does not use any convolutional layers
YOLO runs a single CNN forward pass over the whole image instead of repeatedly scanning it
YOLO only processes every other frame from the camera
explanation: The unit contrasts sliding-window classification, which is 'far too slow for anything real-time,' with YOLO, which 'runs a single CNN forward pass over the whole image' — hence 'You Only Look Once.'

question: According to this unit's ROS integration guidance, what should a detection node do if inference can't keep up with the camera's frame rate?
options: Drop frames rather than let the subscriber backlog grow
Queue every incoming frame so that none are ever lost
Move inference onto the main sensor-processing thread
Instruct the camera driver to stop publishing until inference catches up
explanation: The unit states: 'Keep inference off the main sensor-processing thread if it can't keep up with the camera's frame rate — dropping frames is usually preferable to a growing subscriber backlog.'

question: Why does this unit warn against training on a dataset that is 90% 'straight' examples?
options: Keras refuses to train on class-imbalanced datasets
It would cause the camera driver node to crash during data collection
It makes the AlexNet-style kernels too large to process the images
The model would learn to mostly predict 'straight' regardless of input, since that alone minimizes training loss
explanation: The unit states: 'a dataset that's 90% "straight" will train a model that learns to mostly predict "straight" regardless of input, since that alone minimizes training loss.'

question: What key limitation of behavior cloning does this unit describe?
options: It cannot be implemented using convolutional neural networks
The model is only ever as good as the driving behavior it was trained to imitate
It only works in simulation and cannot be deployed on real hardware
It requires a LiDAR sensor and cannot use a camera
explanation: The unit states this is 'a classic instance of behavior cloning — the model is only ever as good as the driving behavior it was trained to imitate, so the quality and diversity of your collected data matters more than almost any architectural choice.'

question: Why does the unit's AlexNet-style architecture use a large 11x11 kernel with stride 4 in its first convolutional layer?
options: To detect small, fine-grained obstacles with maximum precision
Because Keras requires the first Conv2D layer to use the largest kernel in the network
To aggressively downsample early, trading fine spatial detail for the ability to process bigger images without an explosion in early-layer computation
To match the exact resolution of the LIMO's LiDAR sensor
explanation: The unit says this large early kernel and stride is 'AlexNet's signature — it aggressively downsamples first, trading fine spatial detail for the ability to process bigger images without an explosion in early-layer computation.'

question: During the MoveIt Setup Assistant workflow, why does the self-collision matrix generation step sample random joint configurations?
options: To calibrate the camera-to-arm-base static transform used for grasp poses
To find link pairs that can never collide, so planning doesn't waste time checking them
To determine velocity and acceleration scaling limits for each joint
To verify the URDF file has no XML syntax errors before import
explanation: The unit states the assistant 'samples random joint configurations to find link pairs that can never collide, so planning doesn't waste time checking them' — this is a performance optimization for the planner's collision checking, not a calibration, limits, or syntax step.

question: According to the unit, what is the tradeoff between the two kinematics solver options offered when defining a planning group in the Setup Assistant?
options: KDL is the safe default; IKFast is faster but needs per-robot generation
IKFast is the safe default; KDL needs per-robot code generation
KDL and IKFast are identical in speed, differing only in license terms
IKFast is used only for the gripper group, KDL only for the arm group
explanation: The unit explicitly describes 'a kinematics solver (KDL is the safe default; IKFast is faster but needs per-robot generation)' when defining a planning group.

question: In the generated limo_mycobot_moveit_config package described in the unit, which file maps planning groups to the ros2_control controllers?
options: kinematics.yaml
initial_positions.yaml
moveit_controllers.yaml
joint_limits.yaml
explanation: The package structure listing tags moveit_controllers.yaml specifically as the file that '# maps planning groups to ros2_control controllers', distinct from kinematics.yaml (IK solver) and joint_limits.yaml (velocity/accel scaling).

question: Per the unit's description, what does the MoveGroupInterface class actually do when you call plan()?
options: It performs the actual OMPL trajectory planning locally within the client node
It packages the request and sends it to the move_group node, which runs the planner
It directly dispatches trajectories to ros2_control controllers, bypassing move_group
It replaces the SRDF by generating planning groups dynamically at runtime
explanation: The unit states MoveGroupInterface 'doesn't do any planning itself, it packages up your request... and sends it to move_group, which runs the OMPL (or another) planner and returns a trajectory.'

question: Why does the unit recommend computeCartesianPath for the final approach into a grasp rather than ordinary joint-space planning?
options: Because joint-space planning can produce curved, unpredictable end-effector paths, while Cartesian interpolation gives a straight, predictable descent
Because joint-space planning ignores obstacles added via PlanningSceneInterface
Because named targets from the SRDF can only be reached via Cartesian paths
Because computeCartesianPath always plans faster than move_group's OMPL planner
explanation: The unit explains: 'Joint-space planning...can produce curved, unpredictable end-effector paths — fine for free-space moves, wrong for the final approach into a grasp, where you want a straight descent,' which is what computeCartesianPath's waypoint interpolation provides.

question: What does the 'fraction' value returned by computeCartesianPath tell you, and what should you do with it?
options: It reports how many named poses in the SRDF were reachable from the current state
It indicates how much of the requested path was actually achievable, so a low value signals an unreachable or obstructed waypoint
It reports the percentage of planning time consumed by the OMPL planner
It reflects the velocity scaling factor applied via setMaxVelocityScalingFactor
explanation: The unit says 'fraction tells you how much of the path was actually achievable — always check it before executing; a low fraction usually means a waypoint was unreachable or would hit an obstacle,' and shows only executing when fraction > 0.95.

question: Why does the ObjectDetector node in the unit subscribe to the camera topic using image_transport rather than a plain Image subscription?
options: So it transparently benefits from compressed transport when available
So it can decode depth images without needing camera_info intrinsics
Because raw sensor_msgs/Image topics cannot be parsed by cv_bridge
So it automatically time-synchronizes the RGB and depth streams
explanation: The unit instructs wiring the subscriber 'using image_transport (not a raw Image subscription) so it transparently benefits from compressed transport when available.'

question: How does the unit compare classical color/contour detection to a pretrained ML detector for the object-detection stage?
options: ML-based detection is required first since color/contour detection cannot output pixel coordinates

Color/contour detection is fast, dependency-light, and easy to debug for a known uniquely colored object; you swap in an ML detector only once scenes get cluttered or need multiple classes, keeping downstream code identical
Both methods require retraining the camera's depth intrinsics before deployment
Color/contour detection replaces tf2 transforms, while ML detection still requires them
explanation: The unit says color/contour detection 'is fast, dependency-light, and easy to debug' for a known object, and that once 'color segmentation stops being enough (cluttered scenes, multiple object classes)' you swap the detect() body for an ML model while everything downstream stays the same.

question: Why does the unit stress that an accurate camera-to-arm-base transform (defined back in Unit 1's URDF) matters for object detection?
options: It sets the HSV threshold bounds used during color segmentation
It determines the exposure and frame rate of the RGB camera driver
It is used to deproject and transform the detected 3D point from the camera optical frame into the arm's planning frame, so any error in it caps detection accuracy
It configures the self-collision matrix sampled by the MoveIt Setup Assistant
explanation: The unit states the deprojected point 'is in the camera's optical frame, not the arm's planning frame' and must be transformed via tf2, concluding 'this is why an accurate...camera-to-arm-base transform in your URDF from Unit 1 matters — every detection's accuracy is capped by how correct that static transform is.'

question: What does `colcon build --symlink-install` do for Python packages, and why is it recommended during development?
options: It symlinks source files into install/ instead of copying them, so edits to a Python node take effect without rebuilding
It compiles Python files to bytecode ahead of time for faster node startup
It creates one symbolic link for the whole workspace to save disk space
It skips dependency resolution so incremental builds finish faster
explanation: The unit states that for Python packages, --symlink-install symlinks source files into install/ rather than copying them, so edits take effect without a rebuild (C++ nodes still need rebuilding).

question: According to the unit, what replaces the centralized master process that ROS1 used?
options: Nodes discover each other automatically at runtime via DDS, with no central master process
A single roscore-equivalent node still coordinates discovery
The colcon build tool maintains a runtime discovery registry
Launch files maintain a static list of all node network addresses
explanation: The unit explicitly says nodes discover each other automatically via DDS, the underlying middleware, and there is no central master process like there was in ROS1.

question: In the HelloLimo example, what must happen before the node actually processes anything (like its logged startup message continuing to run)?
options: rclpy.spin(node) must be called, handing control to the executor that processes callbacks
package.xml must declare an executor dependency
colcon build must be re-run after the node object is created
The node begins processing automatically as soon as it is constructed
explanation: The unit notes the node 'does nothing on its own until you spin() it, which hands control to the executor that processes callbacks.'

question: Why does the unit emphasize that a topic is 'typed', using the LIMO lidar as an example?
options: Because every message must match one interface, you can swap the real lidar driver for a simulated one and downstream nodes keep working unchanged
Because typing guarantees a message will be delivered before the publisher's next call
Because the type field sets the network port used by the underlying DDS transport
Because all subscribers must be written in the same programming language as the publisher
explanation: The unit says publishers and subscribers only need to agree on topic name and type, which is why LIMO's real lidar driver can be swapped for a simulated one without changing downstream nodes.

question: What does the unit say happens when a publisher's and subscriber's QoS profiles are incompatible?
options: The connection silently never forms, with no error raised
ROS2 automatically negotiates a compromise profile between the two
The subscriber crashes with an explicit QoS mismatch exception
The message is queued indefinitely until the profiles converge
explanation: The unit states that if QoS profiles are incompatible, 'the connection silently never forms' — and recommends checking QoS mismatch first if ros2 topic echo shows nothing despite the publisher running.

question: Why does the unit warn against putting heavy processing directly inside a subscriber's callback?
options: It can starve the executor, causing other callbacks on the same node to stall
It would exceed the QoS history depth and get dropped by the middleware
It causes the publisher to automatically stop sending further messages
It silently changes the topic's reliability setting to best_effort
explanation: The unit says callbacks should be fast and non-blocking; heavy processing belongs elsewhere, 'otherwise you starve the executor and other callbacks on the same node stall.'

question: Why does the unit treat map-building and map-using as deliberately separate phases, even though the underlying algorithms overlap?
options: So the robot can reuse a world model captured once, offline, instead of rebuilding it from scratch every time it drives, only needing to figure out where it is within it
Because localization on LIMO requires a completely different sensor than mapping does
Because ROS2 cannot run SLAM and navigation packages in the same workspace
Because slam_toolbox is physically incapable of running in a localization-only mode
explanation: The unit explains that a map is knowledge captured once so the robot only needs to localize within it later, and calls this a deliberate separation despite both phases estimating pose from sensor data.

question: What visible symptom does the unit describe for a map built by driving in one long straight line with no loop closure?
options: The map visibly warps over distance
The .pgm file fails to save to disk entirely
The occupancy grid comes out completely blank
RViz refuses to display the /map topic at all
explanation: The unit states that 'a map built by driving in one long straight line with no loop closure will visibly warp over distance' due to uncorrected drift.

question: What two files does map_saver_cli produce when saving LIMO's occupancy grid, and what does each hold?
options: A .pgm image of the grid and a .yaml file with resolution, origin, and occupancy thresholds
A .db database file and a .json metadata file
A .bag recording of the whole mapping run and a .pgm image
A .yaml file holding the grid pixels and a .pgm file holding just the map origin
explanation: The unit shows map_saver_cli writing 'both a .pgm image (the grid) and a .yaml file (resolution, origin, occupied/free thresholds).'

question: How does localization fundamentally differ from SLAM regarding the map, per the unit?
options: Localization only updates the robot's belief about its own pose and never modifies the map, unlike SLAM which builds the map
Localization requires no map at all and works purely from raw odometry
Localization rebuilds the map every cycle to correct accumulated drift
Localization and SLAM both continuously update the map together in this course
explanation: The unit states: 'Unlike SLAM, the map is fixed here — localization only ever updates the robot's belief about its own pose, never the map itself.'

question: Per the troubleshooting guidance, what is one plausible cause if /amcl_pose keeps drifting from LIMO's real position?
options: update_min_d/update_min_a set so high that AMCL isn't updating often enough
The particle count exceeding max_particles, causing the filter to crash
The BT Navigator triggering unnecessary recovery behaviors
The local costmap's inflation_radius being set larger than LIMO's turning radius
explanation: The unit lists a stale/inaccurate map, bad odometry calibration, or update_min_d/update_min_a set too high as causes of drifting pose estimates.

question: In AMCL's 'adaptive' behavior, what condition causes the particle count to grow rather than shrink?
options: High uncertainty, such as right after a kidnapped-robot event or at startup with an unknown initial pose
The lidar's publish rate exceeding the update_min_d threshold
The robot successfully reaching its navigation goal
The static map file being reloaded from disk
explanation: The unit says AMCL grows the particle count 'when uncertainty is high (e.g. right after a kidnapped-robot event or at startup with an unknown initial pose)' and shrinks it once converged.

question: Per the unit, what is the key difference in timescale and information between Nav2's planner and controller?
options: The planner computes a full path infrequently against the static map, while the controller produces velocity commands tens of times a second reacting to local obstacles
The planner runs continuously in real time while the controller only runs once per goal
Both run at identical frequencies but differ only in message type
The controller computes the full global path while the planner only reacts to nearby obstacles
explanation: The unit says the planner runs 'relatively infrequently' against the static map, while the controller converts the path into velocity commands 'tens of times a second, reacting to obstacles the global path doesn't know about.'

question: How does RegulatedPurePursuit differ from DWB according to the unit?
options: RegulatedPurePursuit tracks a lookahead point on the path and is often smoother on differential-drive robots, while DWB samples and scores candidate velocity commands
RegulatedPurePursuit is a global planner plugin while DWB is a behavior-tree plugin
DWB only works in simulation while RegulatedPurePursuit only works on real hardware
RegulatedPurePursuit replaces the BT Navigator entirely
explanation: The unit contrasts the two: 'DWB samples a set of candidate velocity commands and scores them ... while RegulatedPurePursuit tracks a lookahead point on the path and is often smoother on differential-drive robots.'

question: In the 'try it yourself' scenario, what happens to the global plan when a temporary obstacle is placed in front of LIMO but doesn't block the planned corridor?
options: Only the local controller's behavior changes in the short term; the global plan is not recomputed
The BT Navigator immediately aborts the current navigation goal
The global planner recomputes an entirely new path on every costmap update
The static costmap layer permanently marks the obstacle as a wall
explanation: The unit notes 'only the local controller's behavior changes in the short term, not the whole global plan, unless the obstacle actually blocks the planned corridor.'

question: What does the 'clearing' operation in the obstacle layer do, per the unit?
options: It removes previously marked cells that the sensor can now see through, e.g. after a person walks away
It clears the entire costmap and rebuilds it fresh from the static map
It deletes the inflation layer's cost gradient around lethal obstacles
It resets the BT Navigator's recovery-behavior retry counter
explanation: The unit explains every scan both marks new obstacles and 'clears cells the sensor can now see through that were previously marked ... this is what lets a person who walked away stop blocking LIMO's path.'

question: Why does the unit say inflation_radius should be at least LIMO's turning radius plus a safety margin?
options: So the planner prefers routes keeping LIMO's centerline further from walls/objects rather than shaving them as closely as geometrically possible
So the local costmap updates at the same rate as the global costmap
So the static layer can be safely disabled without affecting navigation
So recovery behaviors like Spin are never triggered during navigation
explanation: The unit states the inflation layer's gradient makes the planner 'naturally prefer routes that keep LIMO's centerline further from walls and objects,' and that inflation_radius should cover the turning radius plus margin to achieve this.

question: Which recovery behavior does the unit describe as useful specifically when the obstruction is a person likely to move on their own?
options: Wait
Spin
BackUp
Reroute
explanation: The unit lists 'Wait (pause, useful if the obstruction is a person who will likely move)' among the recovery behaviors, distinct from Spin and BackUp.

question: What core challenge is RTAB-Map specifically engineered to solve at scale, per the unit?
options: Loop closure via appearance-based place recognition, without comparison time growing unmanageable as the map grows
Correcting wheel-slip drift from noisy encoder readings
Synchronizing wheel odometry readings with IMU data
Computing a 2D occupancy grid from a single 2D lidar scan
explanation: The unit says RTAB-Map is 'specifically engineered to solve loop closure at scale,' using appearance-based place recognition and a memory management scheme so comparison doesn't grow unmanageably slow as the map grows.

question: Why is approx_sync:=true usually needed when launching RTAB-Map with a consumer depth camera?
options: RGB and depth frames rarely arrive with identical timestamps, so RTAB-Map matches them within a small tolerance instead
It reduces point cloud resolution to conserve memory
It disables loop closure detection to speed up initial mapping
It forces the camera driver to publish at a fixed synchronized frame rate
explanation: The unit explains that 'RGB and depth frames from consumer depth cameras rarely arrive with identical timestamps — RTAB-Map matches them within a small tolerance instead of requiring an exact match.'

question: What does the unit identify as a common cause of a map that never starts building with RTAB-Map?
options: Unsynchronized or missing depth data
The lidar driver failing to publish to /scan
The BT Navigator failing to launch
An incorrectly configured inflation_radius parameter
explanation: The unit warns that 'a common source of a map that never starts building is unsynchronized or missing depth data,' recommending you verify those topics are publishing at a healthy rate.

question: Why does the unit describe localization mode as both cheaper and safer than mapping mode when redeploying LIMO in an already-mapped space?
options: It freezes the map and adds no new nodes, so it avoids the map silently drifting or growing on redeployment
It disables the camera entirely and relies only on wheel odometry
It turns off the Nav2 controller server to conserve CPU
It eliminates the need for any transform tree, reducing TF overhead
explanation: The unit states localization mode 'freezes the map — no new nodes are added ... which is both cheaper to run and safer (you don't want the map silently drifting or growing every time you redeploy LIMO in a space you've already mapped).'

question: What advantage does feeding /rtabmap/cloud_obstacles into the local costmap give over a lidar-only obstacle layer, per the unit?
options: It captures obstacles at multiple heights, like a table overhang or low shelving, that a single-plane lidar scan would miss
It removes the need for a global costmap entirely
It allows the static layer to update in real time instead of at map-build time
It removes the need for the inflation layer's cost gradient
explanation: The unit notes that with RTAB-Map you typically feed /rtabmap/cloud_obstacles into the costmap 'since it captures obstacles at multiple heights that a single-plane lidar scan would miss entirely (a table overhang, low shelving).'

question: Per the troubleshooting section, what is one mitigation for tracking loss caused by a visually featureless stretch of wall?
options: Driving slower through such stretches or supplementing with wheel odometry fusion
Increasing the inflation_radius parameter in the local costmap
Switching from localization mode back into mapping mode
Reducing the max_particles parameter used by AMCL
explanation: The unit states that for tracking loss from fast motion or featureless walls, 'driving slower through such stretches or supplementing with wheel odometry fusion mitigates this.'

question: What kinematic model does the unit say describes RB-CAR's motion, in contrast to the unicycle model used by differential-drive robots?
options: Bicycle model, since Ackermann steering follows car-like kinematics
Unicycle model, same as differential-drive robots
Omnidirectional/holonomic model
A hybrid model that switches between bicycle and unicycle depending on speed
explanation: The unit states that because RB-CAR uses Ackermann steering (it cannot rotate in place and has a minimum turning radius), its kinematics are those of a bicycle model rather than a unicycle model.

question: According to the unit, why does RB-CAR carry a GPS/GNSS receiver in addition to its 3D LIDAR?
options: Outdoor environments don't offer the wall geometry indoor SLAM relies on, so GPS supplies a global position reference
GPS is needed to compute the Ackermann steering angle in real time
GPS eliminates the need for wheel encoders on the platform
GPS gives finer-grained obstacle detection than the 3D LIDAR
explanation: The unit explains that outdoor environments lack the dense wall geometry indoor SLAM depends on, so GPS is included to provide a global position reference.

question: What role do robotnik_msgs and the platform-specific driver packages play in RB-CAR's software stack?
options: They implement the bicycle-model kinematics equations used by the local planner
They translate raw hardware (motor/steering controller, sensors) into standard ROS topics and services, hiding wire-protocol details
They run the Gazebo simulation used to test the robot before real-world deployment
They provide the SLAM and AMCL localization algorithms used in later units
explanation: The unit states these packages wrap raw hardware into standard ROS topics and services so the rest of the stack doesn't need to know about wire protocols.

question: In RB-CAR's TF frame hierarchy, which frame is described as locally continuous but drifting over time, never jumping?
options: base_link, since it's fixed to the vehicle's reference point
map, since it never changes
odom, a locally-continuous frame that drifts over time but never jumps
velodyne_link, since it's fixed to base_link
explanation: The unit explicitly defines odom as a locally-continuous frame that drifts over time but never jumps, distinct from the fixed map frame.

question: When a plain Twist command is sent to RB-CAR, how does the unit say it is internally handled?
options: It's passed unchanged to independent per-wheel velocity controllers
It's internally decomposed into a steering angle and drive velocity using the bicycle model
It's rejected outright since RB-CAR only accepts AckermannDriveStamped
It's converted into a rotation-in-place command like a differential-drive robot
explanation: The unit shows that linear.x and angular.z are decomposed into steering_angle and speed via the bicycle-model formula, even though Twist is accepted for convenience.

question: According to the unit, what is `ros2 topic hz` particularly useful for diagnosing?
options: Confirming the TF tree hierarchy is correctly published
Detecting a silently-dead sensor by checking whether a topic is publishing at its expected rate
Calculating the vehicle's real-time turning radius
Measuring the maximum steering angle the driver will accept
explanation: The unit says a silently-dead sensor is a common cause of navigation problems, and `ros2 topic hz` is worth running on every sensor topic to sanity-check its publish rate.

question: Why does the unit say outdoor LIDAR-based SLAM is harder than indoor SLAM?
options: Outdoor LIDAR units have inherently lower range than indoor ones
Open fields and long straight paths present sparse or repetitive geometry that looks the same everywhere to a scan-matcher
GPS signals interfere with LIDAR return signals outdoors
Ackermann-steered vehicles cannot run SLAM algorithms designed for differential-drive robots
explanation: The unit states that open fields and repetitive geometry look the same everywhere to a LIDAR scan-matcher, unlike the dense stable geometry of indoor corridors and rooms.

question: What two files does `nav2_map_server map_saver_cli` produce when saving a completed map?
options: A rosbag recording and a YAML sensor calibration file
A .pgm occupancy grid image and a .yaml metadata file
A URDF robot description and a Gazebo .world file
A .pgm image and a nav2 launch.py file
explanation: The unit states this command produces a .pgm occupancy grid plus a .yaml metadata file, the standard map format across the ROS navigation ecosystem.

question: If RB-CAR refuses to move toward a sent navigation goal, what does the unit say to check first?
options: Whether the AMCL particle cloud has converged
Whether the costmap has an obstacle inflated over the goal or start pose
Whether the TEB local planner's minimum turning radius is set correctly
Whether the GPS fix has sufficient satellite coverage
explanation: The unit says an obstacle inflated over the goal or start pose in the costmap is the most common cause of the robot refusing to move, and it's a mapping/costmap issue rather than a planner bug.

question: What does TEB stand for, and how does it represent a trajectory according to the unit?
options: Trajectory Estimation Bicycle model — waypoints fit to bicycle-model kinematics
Timed Elastic Band — a sequence of poses connected by time intervals, deformed by optimization to satisfy constraints
Turning Envelope Boundary — a polygon marking the maximum turning envelope
Terrain Elevation Band — a representation of ground height along the planned path
explanation: The unit defines TEB (Timed Elastic Band) as representing the trajectory as poses connected by time intervals, an 'elastic band' deformed by optimization to satisfy given constraints.

question: Why does the unit say a differential-drive-oriented local planner like classic DWA is a poor fit for RB-CAR?
options: It assumes the robot can rotate in place and turn on a dime, which is false for an Ackermann vehicle
It cannot read costmaps produced by Nav2
It requires a continuous GPS fix to compute any trajectory
It only supports circular footprint models, never rectangular ones
explanation: The unit explains that DWA/TrajectoryPlanner assumes the robot can rotate in place and turn on a dime, assumptions that are false for RB-CAR, leading to infeasible or jerky trajectories.

question: What mistake does the unit warn costs students the most debugging time when configuring TEB for RB-CAR?
options: Setting max_vel_x too high, causing oscillation near the goal
Leaving min_turning_radius at 0, which tells TEB the robot can spin in place
Forgetting cmd_angle_instead_rotvel, which disables the planner entirely
Using a rectangular instead of circular footprint model
explanation: The unit specifically calls out leaving min_turning_radius at 0 as one of the two costliest mistakes, since it tells TEB the robot can spin in place and produces unexecutable plans.

question: In the two-EKF fusion architecture described in the unit, what does the first EKF instance fuse?
options: GPS and IMU, fused directly into the map frame
Wheel odometry and IMU, fused into a continuous odom frame
Wheel odometry and GPS, fused into the map frame
LIDAR scan matching and IMU, fused into base_link
explanation: The unit describes running two EKF instances, with the first fusing only wheel odometry and IMU into a continuous, drift-prone-but-smooth odom frame.

question: What is the role of navsat_transform_node in the unit's GPS fusion pipeline?
options: It fuses wheel odometry with IMU data into a smoothed local estimate
It converts GPS lat/long fixes into a local UTM-based Cartesian frame anchored at the robot's start position
It runs AMCL-style particle filter localization against the saved map
It publishes the AckermannDriveStamped command directly from GPS waypoints
explanation: The unit states navsat_transform_node converts GPS lat/long fixes into a local UTM-based Cartesian frame anchored at the robot's starting position, since the nav stack expects Cartesian coordinates.

question: According to the unit, what is a common cause of the fused odometry estimate being worse than raw wheel odometry?
options: The wheelbase parameter being misconfigured in the TEB planner
A misconfigured *_config matrix where two sources fight over the same state, or an untrusted/misreported GPS covariance
Running the EKF nodes at too high an update frequency
A miscalibrated LIDAR extrinsic transform
explanation: The unit names a misconfigured *_config matrix (sources fighting over the same state variable) or improperly reported/trusted GPS covariance as the most common culprits.

question: Why does the unit recommend adding a separate color-classification step for detected traffic lights?
options: YOLO/SSD detectors cannot detect traffic lights at all, so color classification substitutes for detection
A generic detector usually only reports that a traffic light exists, not its current color/state, so a color-classification pass is added
Color classification replaces the need for a bounding-box detector entirely
Traffic lights move too quickly for a deep detector to track reliably
explanation: The unit explains that a generic detector usually only tells you 'there's a traffic light here,' not its current state, so a cheap HSV-based color-classification pass is added on the cropped bounding box.

question: In the lane detection pipeline, what is the purpose of the region-of-interest (ROI) mask applied after Canny edge detection?
options: To reduce image resolution before running the Hough transform
To prevent horizon clutter, sky, and background objects unrelated to the road from being picked up as edges
To correct for camera lens distortion before edge detection
To convert the image into HSV color space for the lane-color check
explanation: The unit states the ROI mask matters as much as the edge detector because without it, Canny picks up horizon clutter, sky, and background objects unrelated to the road surface.

question: Why does the unit keep the detection node and the lane-estimation node separate, even though both subscribe to the same camera topic?
options: ROS 2 topics cannot support more than one subscriber per publisher
It keeps each node simple to test/swap independently and lets them run at different rates, since lane-finding usually needs to run faster
The detection node requires a GPU while the lane-finding node cannot use one
Combining them would exceed the message size limit of sensor_msgs/Image
explanation: The unit explains keeping them separate keeps each simple to test and swap independently and lets them run at different rates, since lane-finding usually needs to run faster than object detection.

question: Which approach to halting the vehicle on a perception-triggered stop does the unit say to start with?
options: A zero-velocity override with a velocity-arbitration layer, since it's smoother
Canceling and resending the NavigateToPose goal, since it's simpler, more robust, and easier to debug
Disabling the perception node until the vehicle comes to a stop
Switching the vehicle to manual teleoperation
explanation: The unit recommends starting with the simpler cancel-and-resend approach, noting it's easier to debug and good enough to demonstrate the concept, versus the smoother but more complex zero-velocity override.

question: What order of milestones does the unit recommend for building toward the final integrated run?
options: Perception-triggered stop, navigation-only run, static integration, full run
Static integration, navigation-only run, perception-triggered stop, full run
Full run, static integration, navigation-only run, perception-triggered stop
Navigation-only run, full run, static integration, perception-triggered stop
explanation: The unit's suggested milestones are, in order: static integration, navigation-only run, perception-triggered stop, and then the full run.

question: Why does the unit recommend deliberately breaking one subsystem at a time (e.g., killing the GPS topic mid-run) before attempting the full integrated run?
options: To calibrate the EKF's covariance matrices before the full run
To observe whether the rest of the stack degrades gracefully instead of attempting an unsafe maneuver when a subsystem fails
To benchmark the maximum achievable speed of each subsystem
To satisfy a built-in Nav2 requirement for fault-injection testing
explanation: The unit states a robust final project should degrade gracefully — e.g., a lost GPS signal shouldn't make the vehicle attempt a maneuver it can't safely complete — and this exercise previews how a real autonomous system fails.

question: According to the unit, what is the defining trait of RB-Vogui+ that shapes the whole course?
options: Its high payload capacity for warehouse logistics work
Its ability to operate both indoors and outdoors, requiring a localization/navigation strategy that doesn't rely on a lidar bouncing off walls
Its factory-line deployment as a fully autonomous system
Its onboard PC being directly SSH-accessible for development
explanation: The unit states the dual indoor/outdoor design is the defining trait: it needs localization/navigation that works without walls to bounce lidar off, plus payload capacity and a mounting deck for a manipulator and perception sensors.

question: How does the unit describe RB-Vogui+'s locomotion being controlled?
options: Through raw motor commands sent directly to each wheel controller
Through velocity commands (e.g., Twist messages) rather than raw motor commands
Through joint trajectory messages sent via MoveIt
Through PWM signals sent over a serial connection
explanation: The unit explicitly says the four powered wheels are 'controlled through velocity commands rather than raw motor commands,' consistent with the cmd_vel/Twist interface shown later.

question: Per the unit, why should you check the TF tree (base_link -> odom -> map) early, before debugging anything more complex?
options: Because RViz will crash if the tree is missing
Because teleop_twist_keyboard cannot publish cmd_vel without it
Because navigation, costmaps, MoveIt, and GPS fusion computations all depend on that frame tree being correct
Because it is the only way to verify the physical emergency stop works
explanation: The unit says if the base_link -> odom -> map tree and sensor frames aren't there and sane, 'nothing downstream (costmaps, MoveIt, GPS fusion) will work correctly,' so it's worth confirming before debugging anything more complex.

question: How does the unit distinguish SLAM from AMCL for indoor localization?
options: SLAM only works outdoors while AMCL only works indoors
SLAM builds the map and localizes simultaneously, while AMCL localizes against an already-built, static map
AMCL builds the map while SLAM requires a pre-built map to function
They are two names for the exact same underlying localization algorithm
explanation: The unit defines SLAM (slam_toolbox) as building the map and localizing simultaneously, and AMCL as localizing against an already-built static map — cheaper and more predictable once a reliable map exists.

question: Per the unit, what happens if the costmap's inflation_radius is set too large?
options: The robot moves faster because it avoids planning overhead
The costmap stops incorporating live lidar data
The planner may refuse to pass through doorways it should actually fit through
The robot's configured footprint automatically shrinks
explanation: The unit warns that too large an inflation radius means 'the planner refuses to pass through doorways it should fit through,' while too small a radius lets it clip furniture.

question: According to the unit, if obstacles shown in the RViz costmap don't line up with what you can see in the room, what does that usually indicate?
options: A bug in the Nav2 planning algorithm itself
A TF or sensor topic misconfiguration, not a planning bug
That the inflation radius is set too small
That the map needs to be rebuilt from scratch with SLAM
explanation: The unit states directly: 'obstacles that don't line up with what you can see in the room usually mean a TF or sensor topic misconfiguration, not a planning bug.'

question: Why does the unit say GPS is fused with wheel odometry and IMU data rather than being used on its own for outdoor global position?
options: GPS messages are not compatible with ROS message types
GPS is noisy and updates slowly compared to the high-rate, locally-accurate odometry and IMU
GPS requires manual calibration before every mission run
GPS can only report altitude, not horizontal position
explanation: The unit explains GPS 'is noisy and updates slowly... so it's fused with the high-rate, locally-accurate sensors (wheel odometry, IMU) rather than used on its own.'

question: Per the unit, what is the practical failure mode if the IMU's yaw convention doesn't match what navsat_transform_node expects?
options: The EKF node fails to launch at all
The robot stops and refuses to move until reconfigured
The fused heading is rotated by a fixed offset, so the robot confidently drives in the wrong direction while believing it's on course
GPS fixes are silently dropped from the fused estimate
explanation: The unit describes exactly this failure mode: a fixed heading offset from a yaw convention mismatch causes the robot to 'confidently drive in the wrong direction while believing it's on course.'

question: According to the unit, what does navsat_transform_node do with raw GPS fixes, and what should you suspect if the robot 'thinks it's somewhere impossible'?
options: It converts lat/lon fixes into UTM coordinates aligned to the map frame using the starting fix as datum; an impossible position usually points to a datum/frame mismatch, not a bad GPS fix
It replaces wheel odometry entirely; an impossible position means the odometry has failed
It filters lidar scans for outdoor use; an impossible position means the lidar needs recalibration
It only functions with 3D lidar; an impossible position indicates a GPS antenna hardware fault
explanation: The unit states navsat_transform_node projects NavSatFix data into UTM aligned with the map frame using the starting fix as datum, and that an 'impossible' reported position 'is almost always a datum/frame mismatch rather than a bad GPS fix.'

question: What technique does the unit describe for finding a candidate grasp centroid on a flat surface without needing a trained model?
options: Run a CNN-based object detector on the RGB image alone
Segment out the dominant plane via RANSAC (SACSegmentation), cluster the remaining points, and take the centroid of the largest cluster
Use a built-in MoveIt object-recognition module
Manually click the object's location as an RViz marker
explanation: The unit describes exactly this pipeline: 'segment the point cloud to remove the dominant plane... cluster what's left, and take the centroid of the largest cluster as your candidate grasp target,' using PCL's SACSegmentation and Euclidean cluster extraction.

question: Per the unit, if executed grasps are consistently offset in one direction, what should you check first?
options: The gripper's closing force setting
The camera-to-arm static transform/calibration, since every downstream grasp is only as accurate as that transform
The RANSAC plane distance threshold
The MoveIt planner's timeout parameter
explanation: The unit says: 'Every downstream grasp is only as accurate as this transform — if grasps are consistently offset in one direction, recheck the calibration before suspecting the planner.'

question: Why does the unit recommend executing the pick sequence as four discrete MoveGroupInterface calls rather than one continuous Cartesian path?
options: MoveIt does not support Cartesian paths when a gripper is attached
The ROS 2 action API mandates discrete calls for grasping
It's far easier to debug, since you can check the return code of each step before proceeding to the next
It significantly reduces the total execution time of the grasp
explanation: The unit states that executing pre-grasp, descend, close-gripper, and retreat as four discrete calls, checking each return code, 'is far easier to debug than trying to plan the whole sequence as one Cartesian path.'

question: Why does the unit recommend architecting the fetch mission as an explicit state machine rather than one long sequential script?
options: State machines execute faster on the onboard PC
The MoveIt API requires a state machine to function
A short, explicit state machine is easier to debug and easier to extend than one long script with no error handling
It uses less CPU than a sequential script
explanation: The unit advises resisting 'the temptation to write one long script' that does everything sequentially with no error handling, noting a short explicit state machine 'is both easier to debug and easier to extend.'

question: Per the unit, what does a large jump between the AMCL pose estimate and the GPS-fused estimate at the indoor-to-outdoor transition usually mean?
options: The GPS receiver has a hardware fault
The indoor map's origin and the GPS datum aren't consistently aligned
The lidar sensor needs recalibration
The wheel encoders have drifted excessively
explanation: The unit's handoff discussion states: 'A large jump at this transition means your indoor map's origin and your GPS datum aren't consistently aligned — worth checking before the mission ever runs.'

question: According to the unit, what should a 'real mission' do if the DETECT_OBJECT state finds nothing?
options: Immediately transition to DONE and end the mission
Retry indefinitely in a tight loop until something is detected
Retry with a small repositioning move before giving up
Crash and require a manual restart of the mission
explanation: The unit specifies: 'A real mission retries with a small repositioning move before giving up, not an infinite loop or a silent crash.'

question: Why does Jackal, despite having four wheels, behave like a two-wheeled differential-drive robot from a control standpoint?
options: The left wheel pair and right wheel pair are mechanically coupled, so it behaves like a two-wheeled diff-drive base
It uses Ackermann (car-like) steering on its front axle
Only one of its four wheels is powered
Its rear wheels are unpowered caster wheels
explanation: The unit states Jackal is a skid-steer 4WD UGV that is 'a differential-drive robot even though it has four wheels' because the left pair and right pair are mechanically coupled, so it's commanded with linear x and angular z just like a two-wheeled diff-drive base.

question: According to the unit's sensor-to-question mapping, which sensor answers 'where am I in the world'?
options: The GPS receiver
The laser scanner
The wheel encoders
The IMU
explanation: The unit explicitly maps sensors to questions: the laser tells you 'what's near me right now', the camera 'what that near thing looks like', IMU/encoders 'how have I moved', and GPS 'where I am in the world'.

question: What safety habit does the unit recommend regarding `/cmd_vel`, and why?
options: Always cap `/cmd_vel` velocities in your own code, since a bug like a stray large linear.x value is far more dangerous on real hardware than in simulation
Never publish to `/cmd_vel` directly; only use RViz's teleop panel
Disable `/cmd_vel` entirely once localization is running
Always command the maximum possible velocity to stress-test the motor controller
explanation: The unit says to 'always cap /cmd_vel velocities in your own code rather than trusting downstream limits alone', giving the example of a stray linear.x = 5.0 bug being far more dangerous on a real chassis than in sim.

question: According to the unit, what is the functional difference between the global costmap and the local costmap?
options: The global costmap covers the whole known map for long-range planning, while the local costmap is a small rolling window around the robot used for reactive obstacle avoidance
The global costmap is rebuilt from scratch every control cycle, while the local costmap never updates
The global costmap only uses camera data, while the local costmap only uses laser data
The local costmap is used exclusively outdoors, while the global costmap is used exclusively indoors
explanation: The unit distinguishes them directly: 'The global costmap covers the whole known map and drives long-range path planning; the local costmap is a small rolling window around the robot used for reactive obstacle avoidance.'

question: Why does the unit recommend giving AMCL a rough starting pose before relying on it?
options: Without it, the particle cloud has to disambiguate a symmetric-looking room from scratch, which can take a while or converge to the wrong spot entirely
AMCL cannot start publishing `/amcl_pose` at all without an initial pose
A rough starting pose is required to build the static map itself
It reduces the resolution needed in the local costmap
explanation: The unit warns that without an initial pose estimate (via RViz's '2D Pose Estimate' or the `initialpose` topic), the particle filter may take a long time or converge to the wrong spot in a symmetric-looking room.

question: Which parameter does the unit call 'the single highest-leverage knob' for how cautiously Jackal hugs walls versus how narrow a gap it will squeeze through?
options: inflation_radius
resolution
global_frame
robot_base_frame
explanation: The unit states that tuning `inflation_radius` in the costmap configuration is 'the single highest-leverage knob' for wall-hugging caution versus gap-squeezing willingness.

question: Why does the unit say laser-based SLAM loses most of its value outdoors?
options: Open fields and roads give the scanner little structure to match against, so small map-frame drift that's invisible in a small room becomes meters of error across a large field
Laser scanners physically cannot operate outside of climate-controlled buildings
GPS signals interfere with and jam the laser scanner's wavelength
Outdoor Jackal units are not equipped with a laser scanner at all
explanation: The unit explains that open outdoor spaces lack the structure laser SLAM needs to match against, so drift that's negligible in a small room becomes meters of error over something like a 100 metre field.

question: What does the unit say happens if `magnetic_declination_radians` or `yaw_offset` are set incorrectly in `navsat_transform`?
options: The robot's fused heading ends up rotated relative to true north, so it confidently drives in the wrong direction while its GPS position still looks fine
The EKF immediately stops publishing any odometry output
The GPS receiver stops producing raw fixes entirely
The local costmap's inflation radius is automatically recalculated
explanation: The unit warns that these two parameters 'matter more than they look' — getting them wrong rotates the fused heading relative to true north, so the robot drives confidently in the wrong direction despite a seemingly correct GPS position.

question: According to the unit, what is the core trade-off between GPS and odometry/IMU-based estimation outdoors?
options: GPS gives drift-free global position but is noisy and updates slowly, while odometry and the IMU are smooth and high-rate but drift unbounded over time
GPS is high-rate and precise while odometry is low-rate and drift-free
Odometry provides global position while GPS only corrects local motion estimates
Both sources provide identical accuracy, making fusion unnecessary
explanation: The unit states the trade-off explicitly: GPS is drift-free but noisy and slow (1-10 Hz, meter-level accuracy), while odometry and IMU are smooth and high-rate but drift unbounded — which is why fusing both is better than picking one.

question: What pattern does the laser-based leg detector look for in the 2D scan to identify a person?
options: Two small, roughly circular clusters of similar range, spaced 10-50 cm apart, moving together
A single large continuous blob spanning at least a meter in width
A straight uninterrupted line of constant range across the whole scan
Three evenly spaced clusters forming a triangular pattern
explanation: The unit describes legs as producing 'two small, roughly circular clusters of similar range, spaced 10-50 cm apart, moving together' in the laser scan.

question: What limitation of the laser-only leg detector does the unit call out?
options: It's easy to confuse with table legs or bollards
It cannot operate at all in darkness
It requires a trained deep neural network to run
It cannot report a 2D position at all
explanation: The unit says the leg detector is 'cheap, fast, and works in the dark, but easy to confuse with table legs or bollards on its own.'

question: How does the unit describe deriving a 3D person position from the stereo camera pipeline?
options: Crop the point cloud to the pixels inside the HOG bounding box and take the centroid of the valid (non-NaN) points in that region
Average every point in the full-frame point cloud regardless of where the person is
Use only the 2D pixel coordinates of the bounding box center as the 3D position
Run AMCL directly against the raw RGB image
explanation: The unit states: 'crop the point cloud to the pixels inside the HOG bounding box and take the centroid of the valid (non-NaN) points in that region — that centroid... is your camera-based 3D person estimate.'

question: In the patrol loop's `while ... else` construct, what is the purpose of the `else` clause?
options: It runs only when the inner loop completes without hitting `break`, so the waypoint index advances only when the current leg finished normally rather than being interrupted
It executes on every iteration of the outer loop regardless of state
It is what triggers the transition into the REACTING state
It has no functional effect and exists only as required Python syntax
explanation: The unit explains that the `else` on the `while` 'only runs if the inner loop completed without break — a convenient way to advance to the next waypoint only when the current leg actually finished normally, not when it was interrupted by a detection.'

question: Why does the unit call the length of the REACTING hold time 'a real design choice, not a detail'?
options: Too short a hold causes the robot to flicker between states on a noisy detection, while too long stops it from actually functioning as a patrol
The hold time directly sets the robot's maximum linear velocity cap
The hold time determines which of the two detectors (laser or camera) is used
Longer hold times always reduce the robot's battery consumption
explanation: The unit states that deciding how long to hold in REACTING matters because 'too short and the robot flickers between states on a noisy detection, too long and it stops being a patrol.'

question: What build order does the unit recommend for assembling the patrol micro-project?
options: Get the patrol loop solid on its own first, then add detection as a pure logger, then finally wire in the interrupt-and-resume behavior
Implement the full interrupt-and-resume behavior first, then add navigation afterward
Build detection and navigation simultaneously from the very first commit
Start with the REACTING state's logic before the PATROLLING loop exists
explanation: The unit advises to 'build and test it incrementally rather than all at once — get the patrol loop solid on its own first, then add detection as a pure logger before you let it actually interrupt navigation, then finally wire in the interrupt-and-resume behavior.'

question: According to the unit, what is the single most useful habit for closing the sim-to-real gap?
options: Recording real sensor data with `ros2 bag record` for offline replay, so costmap, AMCL/EKF, and detector settings can be tuned against real noise before driving on hardware
Increasing the simulated GPS noise until it matches expectations
Turning off the EKF entirely during real-world tests
Restricting all real-world testing to indoor environments only
explanation: The unit calls recording real sensor data (`ros2 bag record /scan /odom /gps/filtered /cmd_vel ...`) for offline replay 'the single most useful habit' for closing the sim-to-real gap.

question: What does the unit suggest as a next step to replace the patrol project's simple two-state FSM so it can handle more edge cases?
options: Nav2's behavior trees
A single large if-else chain
MoveIt manipulation planning
Multi-robot coordination
explanation: The unit suggests digging into 'Nav2's behavior trees... to replace the simple two-state FSM from the patrol project with something that handles more edge cases without becoming spaghetti.' MoveIt and multi-robot coordination are mentioned as separate, unrelated next directions.

question: What test does the unit propose to check whether you learned transferable concepts rather than just Jackal-specific commands?
options: Port the patrol project to a different diff-drive robot and see if it takes an afternoon rather than a rewrite
Re-run the exact same Jackal simulation ten times and check for identical results
Rewrite the same project in a different programming language on the same robot
Deliberately increase sensor noise in simulation until the code crashes
explanation: The unit states: 'The fastest way to find out how much of this actually transferred is to port the patrol project to a different diff-drive robot... If it takes you an afternoon instead of a rewrite, you've learned the concepts and not just the Jackal-specific commands.'

question: According to Unit 0, what is the Summit XL's stock drive configuration, and which variant departs from it?
options: Ackermann (car-like) steering by default; the Steel/HL variant switches to skid-steer for tighter turns
Four-wheel skid-steer differential drive by default; some Steel/HL variants swap in Mecanum wheels for omnidirectional motion
Mecanum wheels for omnidirectional motion by default; the Steel/HL variant reverts to skid-steer for outdoor stability
Two-wheel differential drive with a single caster; all Summit XL variants share this identical geometry
explanation: The unit states the stock configuration is four-wheel skid-steer differential drive, with some variants like the Steel/HL line using Mecanum wheels instead — so drive geometry should be checked per variant.

question: Which sensor does Unit 0 describe as doubling as a low-cost person detector via leg-pair clustering, ahead of the dedicated detection work in Unit 3?
options: The GPS receiver
The wheel encoders
The Hokuyo 2D laser scanner
The PTZ RGB camera
explanation: The unit says the Hokuyo laser 'drives obstacle avoidance and indoor SLAM in Unit 1, and doubles as a cheap person detector (via leg-pair clustering) in Unit 3.'

question: How does Unit 0 describe the typical package layout for Robotnik-style Summit XL packages?
options: A single monolithic package containing description, simulation, and control code together
A robot description package (URDF/xacro), a Gazebo bring-up package, and a control/teleop package
One package per sensor (laser, camera, GPS, encoders) with no shared bring-up package
A navigation package and a perception package only, with teleop bundled into the navigation package
explanation: The unit describes the split as 'a robot description package (URDF/xacro model), a Gazebo bring-up package (worlds, spawn logic), and a control/teleop package,' while noting exact names vary by distro/variant.

question: When you save a map with map_saver_cli after SLAM mapping, what two files does Unit 1 say you get?
options: A .yaml describing the robot footprint and a .urdf of the sensor frames
A .bag file of the raw laser scans and a .yaml of odometry drift
A .pgm image and a .yaml file describing resolution and origin
A .pgm occupancy grid and a .launch file to relaunch SLAM automatically
explanation: The unit states: 'The saved map is a .pgm image plus a .yaml file describing resolution and origin; both are inputs to the next step.'

question: Why does Unit 1 say AMCL needs a rough initial pose estimate before it's useful?
options: Because AMCL cannot start without first re-running slam_toolbox on the saved map
Because the particle filter needs a seed near the true pose so its scattered particle cloud can collapse onto the robot's actual pose after a short drive
Because the global costmap cannot be built until an initial pose is published
Because /initialpose recalibrates the Hokuyo laser's angle_min and angle_increment values
explanation: The unit explains AMCL is a particle filter that 'needs a rough starting pose before it converges' — after publishing an estimate and driving a short distance, 'the scattered particle cloud should collapse onto the robot's real pose.'

question: Per Unit 1, what distinguishes the local costmap from the global costmap?
options: The local costmap is a small rolling window updated live from the laser to dodge obstacles absent from the static map, while the global costmap is the full inflated map used for whole-environment path planning
The global costmap updates live from the laser while the local costmap is a static, pre-inflated snapshot of the whole map
The local costmap is generated once by SLAM and never updates, while the global costmap refreshes every control cycle
Both costmaps cover the full mapped area, differing only in inflation radius, not in update frequency
explanation: The unit defines the global costmap as 'the full map, inflated...used by the global planner' and the local costmap as 'a small rolling window around the robot, updated live from the laser...to dodge obstacles the static map doesn't know about.'

question: According to Unit 2, why doesn't the indoor laser-SLAM approach from Unit 1 transfer to outdoor navigation?
options: Outdoor GPS signals interfere with the Hokuyo laser's rangefinder readings
Outdoor environments typically lack stable, nearby geometric features for the laser to match scans against, and are too large for a pre-built occupancy grid — while GPS is available outdoors instead
The Hokuyo laser's maximum range is too short to be used outdoors under any circumstances
Wheel encoders drift too fast outdoors for odometry to be usable at all
explanation: The unit says outdoor spaces give the laser 'almost nothing distinctive to lock onto' and are 'far larger than any pre-built occupancy grid you'd want to maintain,' while GPS fills that gap outdoors.

question: In the NavSatFix message discussed in Unit 2, what does a negative value in the status.status field mean?
options: The GPS covariance is unusually low, meaning high confidence in the fix
The robot has descended below sea level, affecting the altitude field
There is no valid GPS fix, so the position should not be trusted
The receiver is in high-precision RTK mode
explanation: The unit states: 'status.status (a negative value means no valid fix — don't trust the position when this happens).'

question: In the outdoor localization pipeline described in Unit 2, what is the respective role of navsat_transform_node and ekf_node?
options: navsat_transform_node fuses odometry with the IMU directly; ekf_node then converts the result into latitude/longitude
ekf_node converts GPS fixes into a local flat frame; navsat_transform_node then fuses that with wheel odometry
navsat_transform_node converts GPS fixes plus odometry/IMU into local-frame GPS-derived odometry; ekf_node fuses that with wheel odometry and IMU into /odometry/filtered
Both nodes perform the same fusion independently, and the system averages their two outputs
explanation: The unit describes navsat_transform_node converting GPS fixes into local-frame odometry, then ekf_node fusing 'that GPS-derived odometry with wheel odometry and the IMU through an extended Kalman filter' to produce /odometry/filtered.

question: Why does Unit 3 caution that the leg-cluster laser detector shouldn't be your only signal for detecting people?
options: It requires a large labeled training dataset that most teams don't have
It only works when the robot is stationary, never while patrolling
It will false-positive on things like chair legs and bins, so it's presented as a fast first-pass check rather than a standalone detector
It cannot run in real time on the Summit XL's onboard computer
explanation: The unit says the leg detector 'will false-positive on chair legs and bins — good enough as a fast, low-cost first-pass detector, not as your only signal,' which is why the camera detector exists as an independent check.

question: Without a depth sensor, how does Unit 3 say you can turn a camera bounding-box detection into a map-frame position?
options: Assume a fixed distance of one meter from the camera for every detection
Use a ground-plane assumption: project the base of the bounding box onto the floor plane using the camera's known height and tilt
Average the bounding box position with the nearest laser leg-cluster detection
Use the PTZ's zoom level to estimate distance via lens focal length alone
explanation: The unit states you need 'either a known ground-plane assumption (project the base of the bounding box onto the floor plane using the camera's known height and tilt) or a depth source.'

question: Unit 3 distinguishes 'unrecognized' from 'recognized but not authorized.' Why does it say these should be treated as different outcomes?
options: Because unrecognized faces should always trigger an immediate alarm, unlike unauthorized ones
Because an unrecognized face might just mean poor lighting or a new visitor, while recognized-but-unauthorized is a stronger signal
Because unrecognized faces indicate a hardware fault in the PTZ camera that needs servicing
Because only recognized-but-unauthorized detections are logged; unrecognized ones are discarded
explanation: The unit says: 'the first might just mean poor lighting or a new visitor, the second is a stronger signal' — so the patrol logic (Unit 4) should treat them differently.

question: How many states does the patrol state machine in the micro-project define, and what are they?
options: Two: MOVING and STOPPED
Four: IDLE, PATROLLING, INVESTIGATING, and ALERTING
Three: PATROLLING, INVESTIGATING, and PAUSED
Five states, one per patrol waypoint
explanation: The unit's PatrolState enum defines exactly three states: PATROLLING, INVESTIGATING, and PAUSED.

question: In the on_person_detected() function, why does it immediately return the current state unchanged if state != PatrolState.PATROLLING?
options: To reset the current_waypoint_index back to zero
To ignore re-triggers when the robot is already investigating or paused, rather than re-entering INVESTIGATING repeatedly
To switch control from the laser detector to the camera detector
To force the robot to log an alert every time a person is redetected
explanation: The code comment states 'already investigating or paused, ignore re-triggers' — the guard prevents re-triggering a transition that's already underway.

question: Why does the unit emphasize cancelling the in-flight navigation goal (rather than letting it keep running) when a person is detected?
options: Because Nav2 automatically cancels goals whenever a new detector callback fires
Because leaving a goal active would exceed the Summit XL's battery budget
Because cancelling immediately is what makes the reaction feel prompt — a robot that reacts meters too late undermines the purpose of the patrol
Because the local costmap cannot update while a goal is active
explanation: The unit states cancelling 'is what makes the reaction feel immediate' and warns that noticing a person 'three meters late undermines the whole point of the exercise.'

question: To improve outdoor localization robustness beyond what Unit 2 built, what does the closing unit suggest adding to the EKF?
options: A second localization source, such as visual odometry or a second GPS fix source, as a fallback
A second Hokuyo laser to run full indoor SLAM outdoors as a backup
A higher-resolution occupancy grid map covering the entire outdoor area
Removing the IMU from the fusion to reduce noise
explanation: The unit recommends 'adding a second localization source (visual odometry, or a second GPS fix source) so the EKF has something to fall back on' during multipath or dropout.

question: Per the safety section, what must the minimum standoff distance and speed limit near people NOT rely solely on?
options: The GPS covariance value reported by the receiver
The patrol route's waypoint ordering
The person-detection logic — it must be enforced independently, since a detector bug should never be the only thing preventing a collision
The face-recognition similarity threshold
explanation: The unit states the standoff distance and speed limit must be 'enforced independently of the detection logic (a detector bug should never be the only thing preventing a collision).'

question: What advice does the unit give about choosing a portfolio extension to the patrol project?
options: Implement as many small extensions as possible to show breadth
Pick one clear extension and develop it well, rather than several small ones
Focus only on writing documentation, without changing any code
Avoid extending the project and instead resubmit the original micro-project
explanation: The unit advises: 'pick one clear extension rather than several small ones... One well-documented extension demonstrates more than a pile of half-finished features.'

question: According to Unit 1, what is TIAGo?
options: A fixed-base industrial arm requiring no mobile base
A mobile manipulator from PAL Robotics built from four coordinated subsystems: base, torso, arm, and head
A quadruped robot designed for rough terrain
An aerial drone platform used for mapping
explanation: Unit 1 defines TIAGo as a mobile manipulator from PAL Robotics with four physical subsystems (base, torso, arm, head) treated as separate-but-coordinated ROS actors throughout the course.

question: Why does Unit 1 say it pays off to get comfortable with tf2 tools early in the course?
options: Because tf2 tools are required to launch the Gazebo simulation
Because TF frames control ROS network communication between nodes
Because every sensor reading and command is expressed relative to TF frames defined in the URDF, so understanding transforms matters for the whole course
Because tf2 replaces rostopic echo for all debugging tasks
explanation: The unit states that every sensor reading and command is expressed relative to a TF frame from the URDF, which is why getting comfortable with tf2 tools early pays off for the whole course.

question: What does Unit 1 recommend as your default reconnaissance step before commanding a newly launched TIAGo simulation?
options: Launch tiago_moveit_config and inspect the planning scene
Publish directly to cmd_vel without checking anything first
Run rqt_tf_tree only, since topics are self-explanatory
Use rostopic list, rostopic echo, and rosnode list to know what a robot is publishing and subscribing to before commanding it
explanation: Unit 1 explicitly says to treat rostopic list, rostopic echo, and rosnode list as default reconnaissance tools before writing any code against a robot.

question: Per Unit 2, why is TIAGo's base commanded with velocities while the torso, head, arm, and gripper are commanded with joint trajectories?
options: Because velocity commands are enough for the base's open continuous movement with no joint limits, while trajectories coordinate multiple joints within limits
Because the base has no entry in controller_manager
Because trajectories are computationally cheaper to send than velocity commands
Because Python cannot publish Twist messages in a loop
explanation: Unit 2 explains a velocity interface suffices for the base since there's no joint limit to violate, while joints like the arm need a JointTrajectory to arrive together within physical limits.

question: What does Unit 2 say happens to the linear.y field of a Twist command sent to TIAGo's mobile_base_controller?
options: It causes the robot to strafe sideways instantly
It raises the torso by that amount
It's accepted but ignored, since TIAGo's differential-drive base has no way to move sideways
It's rejected with an error by the controller
explanation: Unit 2 states that only linear.x and angular.z have any effect because the base is differential-drive; linear.y is accepted but ignored.

question: Why does Unit 2's base-control example publish Twist commands in a loop rather than once?
options: Because rostopic pub syntax requires looping by default
Because looping gradually increases the linear velocity over time
Because a Twist message can only be published once per node's lifetime
Because the controller expects a steady stream of commands and stops the robot if messages stop arriving, a deliberate safety behavior
explanation: Unit 2 notes the controller expects a steady stream of commands and will stop the robot if messages stop arriving — a safety behavior so a crashed script doesn't leave it rolling.

question: What does amcl do in TIAGo's navigation stack, according to Unit 3?
options: It builds the static map used for navigation from scratch during operation
It fuses laser scan data against a pre-built map to maintain a probabilistic pose estimate
It directly converts MoveBaseGoal poses into wheel velocity commands
It performs 3D segmentation of objects for grasping
explanation: Unit 3 describes amcl (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization) as fusing laser scan data against a pre-built map to maintain a probabilistic pose estimate.

question: How do the global and local costmaps differ, per Unit 3?
options: The global costmap is built from the static map for long-range planning, while the local costmap is built from live sensor data for short-range obstacle avoidance
The global costmap tracks joint positions while the local costmap tracks wheel velocities
They are identical, just displayed at different RViz zoom levels
The global costmap only exists in simulation; the local costmap only exists on real hardware
explanation: Unit 3 states move_base layers a global costmap (from the static map, long-range) and a local costmap (from live sensor data, short-range obstacle avoidance).

question: In Unit 3's Python navigation example, what does client.get_state() returning 3 mean?
options: The goal was rejected outright
An obstacle is currently blocking the path
SUCCEEDED, per actionlib's GoalStatus enum, as the code comment notes
The robot is still actively moving toward the goal
explanation: The code comment in Unit 3 explicitly states '3 == SUCCEEDED in actionlib's GoalStatus enum'.

question: According to Unit 4, what does MoveIt actually plan against?
options: The raw URDF file, unmodified
A kinematic model built from joint limits, planning group definitions, a self-collision matrix, and an IK solver — not the raw URDF
Only the Gazebo physics engine's collision meshes
A pre-recorded set of joint trajectories stored in the SRDF
explanation: Unit 4 states MoveIt plans against a kinematic model, not the raw URDF, needing joint limits, planning group membership, a self-collision matrix, and an IK solver.

question: How does the arm_torso planning group differ from the arm group in Unit 4?
options: arm and arm_torso are functionally identical, just named differently
arm_torso only controls the gripper, while arm controls the whole robot
arm_torso plans faster because it involves fewer joints than arm
arm_torso adds the torso lift joint for extra reach, at the cost of a harder IK problem, while arm keeps the torso fixed
explanation: Unit 4 explains arm_torso includes the torso lift joint for extra reach at the cost of a slightly harder IK problem, whereas arm is just the 7 arm joints with the torso fixed.

question: Why does Unit 4 emphasize always previewing a plan in RViz before hitting Execute?
options: Because a kinematically fine plan can still be a bad idea near people or fragile objects, so previewing catches problems like an elbow swinging through the torso
Because previewing is required to conserve the robot's battery
Because RViz can only display a trajectory after it has already executed
Because execution, once started, can never be cancelled
explanation: Unit 4 says this habit prevents the most real-world mistakes, since a plan that looks fine kinematically can still be dangerous near people or fragile objects.

question: Why does Unit 5 recommend printing get_planning_frame() and get_end_effector_link() at the start of every MoveIt script?
options: Because they change randomly between runs and must be logged for reproducibility
Because MoveIt refuses to accept any pose target unless they've been printed first
Because every pose built with set_pose_target is interpreted relative to the planning frame, and mixing up frames is a common source of unexpected arm motion
Because they report the arm's current battery charge
explanation: Unit 5 states these are worth printing because every pose is interpreted relative to the planning frame, and mixing up frames commonly causes plans that succeed but move the arm unexpectedly.

question: Why does the collision-object example in Unit 5 call rospy.sleep(1.0) right after scene.add_box()?
options: Because the scene update travels asynchronously over a topic to move_group, and planning immediately after could race ahead of it and plan against a stale, empty scene
Because add_box() is a blocking call that always takes exactly one second
Because Gazebo needs a second for the box to physically settle before it exists
Because MoveIt enforces a fixed one-second cooldown between any two service calls
explanation: Unit 5 explicitly says the sleep isn't cosmetic: the scene update travels asynchronously, and planning right after adding an object can race ahead of it.

question: Per Unit 5, what should you call after arm_group.go(wait=True) to avoid leftover targets affecting the next plan?
options: set_num_planning_attempts(10)
add_box() followed by remove_world_object()
wait_for_server()
