explanation: The unit states preventDefault() on a form's submit event is essential because 'without it, the browser tries to reload the page as if this were a classic server-rendered form.'

question: According to Unit 8, what do map, filter, and reduce have in common that isn't guaranteed by a hand-written for loop?
options: They always execute asynchronously in the background
They always return a new array (or value) without mutating the original
They automatically split work across multiple CPU cores
They can only operate on arrays containing numbers
explanation: The unit says these methods 'replace most hand-written loops and, unlike a for loop, always return a new array (or value) without mutating the original — which keeps your rendering code predictable.'

question: What gotcha does Unit 8 warn about regarding Array.prototype.sort()?
options: It cannot sort an array that contains any negative numbers
It sorts in place and, without a comparator, sorts numbers as strings (e.g. [10,2,1].sort() gives [1,10,2])
It only works correctly on arrays of strings, never arrays of numbers
It always sorts in descending order unless told otherwise
explanation: The unit states sort() 'sorts in place and, without a comparator, sorts numbers as strings ([10, 2, 1].sort() gives [1, 10, 2]),' recommending a numeric comparator and spreading the array first if order must be preserved.

question: In the roslibjs /joint_states example, why does the code zip message.name and message.position together with .map((n, i) => ...)?
options: Because ROS message definitions frequently split related data into separate parallel arrays rather than an array of objects
Because roslibjs requires every subscription callback to call map() before filter()
Because JSON.parse in the browser cannot handle nested object structures
Because /joint_states only publishes names, and positions must be requested separately via a service call
explanation: The unit explains this zipping pattern is 'a pattern you'll reuse often with ROS array messages, since ROS message definitions frequently split related data into separate arrays rather than an array of objects.'

question: According to Unit 9, what specific problem does a component framework like React address as a dashboard grows?
options: It removes the need to run any kind of local development server
Manually keeping each DOM node in sync with the right piece of state becomes hard to track once many things update independently
Plain JavaScript is described as incapable of ever opening a WebSocket connection
HTML forms are said to stop working once a page has more than one component
explanation: The unit says a real dashboard has many independently-updating pieces, and 'manually keeping each DOM node in sync with the right piece of state is exactly the class of bug component frameworks were built to eliminate.'

question: In the BatteryDisplay example, why does calling setBattery(b => b - 5) update what's shown, while directly mutating a plain variable would not?
options: setBattery encrypts the value before writing it back to memory
Calling the state setter is what triggers React to re-render; direct mutation doesn't notify React of the change
Plain JavaScript variables declared with const or let are always read-only
useState only works when called from inside an arrow function
explanation: The unit states 'Calling setBattery(...) is what triggers the re-render — directly mutating a variable would not,' calling this the core mental shift from Unit 7's manual DOM updates.

question: Why does the JSX example use className instead of class when setting an element's CSS class?
options: className applies styles with higher CSS specificity than the class attribute
class is a reserved word in JavaScript, so JSX uses className instead
className is required whenever a component uses conditional rendering with &&
class only works with Vue and Svelte components, not React
explanation: The unit notes the example uses 'className instead of class (a JS reserved word)' — since JSX is JavaScript, the reserved word class can't be used as an attribute name.

question: According to Unit 10, what is a "prop" in a React component?
options: A global variable automatically shared across all components in the app
Data passed from parent to child, read-only from the child's perspective
A CSS class that a parent component applies to itself automatically
A function a child component uses to directly overwrite its parent's state
explanation: The unit defines a prop as '{ connected } in StatusBadge's parameter list is a prop — data passed from parent to child, read-only from the child's perspective.'

question: Why does Unit 10 say the key prop is required when rendering a list of components with .map()?
options: It sets the initial state value for each rendered component
React uses it to track which item is which across re-renders, so it should be stable and unique
It controls the visual stacking order (z-index) of the rendered elements
It is only needed when a list contains fewer than five items
explanation: The unit states 'React uses it to track which item is which across re-renders, so pick something stable and unique, never the array index if the list can reorder.'

question: In the VelocityForm/App example, how does the child component report a submitted command back up to its parent?
options: By directly modifying a state variable owned by the parent component
By dispatching a custom DOM event that bubbles up through the page
By calling a callback function (onSend) that the parent passed down as a prop
By writing the command into a shared global JavaScript object
explanation: The unit explains this is 'how a form component reports a submitted command without needing to know what happens to it' — VelocityForm calls onSend(cmd), the callback prop App passed down.

question: According to Unit 1, why does the browser talk only to Rosbridge instead of connecting directly to the ROS 2 graph?
options: Because browsers cannot open TCP socket connections of any kind, only HTTP
Because ROS 2 requires an X11 display server that only Rosbridge provides
Rosbridge is the only component that speaks the ROS 2 graph, translating topics and services into WebSocket JSON messages the browser can use
Because JavaScript cannot parse binary ROS 2 message formats without a translation server
explanation: The unit states "the browser never talks to ROS 2 directly... Rosbridge is the only piece that actually speaks the ROS 2 graph," exposing it as WebSocket messages.

question: Per Unit 1, which topic/message-type pair is used as the common example robot throughout the course?
options: /odom (nav_msgs/msg/Odometry) and /image_raw (sensor_msgs/msg/Image)
/cmd_vel (geometry_msgs/msg/Twist) for driving and /scan (sensor_msgs/msg/LaserScan) for laser data
/joint_states (sensor_msgs/msg/JointState) and /battery_state (sensor_msgs/msg/BatteryState)
/cmd_vel (std_msgs/msg/Float32) and /scan (std_msgs/msg/String)
explanation: Unit 1 names a differential-drive robot publishing /cmd_vel (geometry_msgs/msg/Twist) and a laser scan on /scan (sensor_msgs/msg/LaserScan) as the common case used throughout the course.

question: Why does Unit 1 say you don't need physical hardware to follow this course?
options: Because Rosbridge includes a built-in simulator that replaces the need for Gazebo
Because the course only uses recorded rosbag data instead of live topics
Because JavaScript can generate synthetic sensor data without ROS 2 running at all
A simulated robot exposes the exact same topics over Rosbridge as a real one, since Rosbridge only cares about the ROS 2 graph, not what's driving it underneath
explanation: The unit explicitly states that simulation exposes identical topics over Rosbridge as real hardware because Rosbridge is indifferent to what drives the ROS 2 graph.

question: Why does Unit 2 say you can't just double-click an HTML file to develop the robot panel?
options: Because double-clicking doesn't render CSS properly in most browsers
file:// pages have browser restrictions on JavaScript modules, fetch calls, and WebSocket connections that a real server avoids
Because ROS 2 requires the page to be served over HTTPS specifically
Because file:// pages cannot display HTML tables
explanation: The unit explains that browsers enforce stricter security under file:// than http://, restricting JS modules, fetch, and WebSocket connections needed for Rosbridge.

question: According to Unit 2, what must you do after editing a static file served by the local HTTP server for the change to appear?
options: Simply refresh the browser — no server restart is needed for static files
Restart the server process with Ctrl+C and re-run it
Clear the browser cache manually every time
Re-run python3 -m http.server with a new port number
explanation: The unit states any edit you save is visible on a browser refresh, with no server restart needed for static files.

question: What does Unit 2 say to do if you see "address already in use" when starting the server?
options: Switch to Node's http-server package, which does not have this issue
Reboot the machine to release the port
Stop the previous server instance running in another terminal with Ctrl+C before starting a new one on the same port
Always use port 8000 since it is reserved specifically for this course
explanation: The conclusion notes that error means a previous server instance is still running, and to stop it (Ctrl+C) before starting a new one on that port.

question: Why does Unit 3 say to add id attributes to elements whose content will change dynamically?
options: They are required for HTML5 validation to pass
They control the visual styling applied to that element by default
They determine the tab order for keyboard navigation
They serve as hooks so JavaScript can find and update those exact elements later, not for CSS styling
explanation: The unit explicitly says id attributes "are not for styling, they're hooks so JavaScript can find and update these exact elements later."

question: How does Unit 3 distinguish <span> from <div>?
options: <span> is inline and sits within surrounding text, while <div> is block-level and takes its own line — making span natural for an inline value and div natural for a whole panel section
<span> is used only inside <table> elements while <div> can appear anywhere
<div> is inline while <span> is block-level
<span> can hold an id attribute but <div> cannot
explanation: The unit states span is inline (fits next to a label) while div is block-level (a whole panel section).

question: In Unit 3's practice example, what port does Rosbridge's WebSocket server default to?
explanation: The unit states Rosbridge runs a WebSocket server on default port 9090, connected to with ws://localhost:9090.

question: Why does Unit 4 recommend <input type="range"> for a value like linear speed, rather than a free-text field?
options: Range inputs automatically publish their value to Rosbridge without JavaScript
Range inputs are the only input type that supports decimal values
The min/max/step attributes constrain the input at the browser level, so you don't need to validate "is this a sane speed" in JavaScript later
Free-text fields cannot be labeled with <label for="...">
explanation: The unit states the min/max/step attributes constrain the value at the browser level, removing the need for separate JS validation.

question: Why does Unit 4 prefer <select> over free text for something like drive mode?
options: Because <select> elements load faster in the browser
<select> can't produce an invalid value, unlike free text where a user could type anything
Because <select> automatically submits the form when changed
Because free-text inputs cannot be styled with CSS
explanation: The unit says "<select> beats free-text for a fixed set of modes, since it can't produce an invalid value."

question: Per Unit 4, what benefit does pairing an <input> with a matching <label for="..."> provide beyond accessibility?
options: Clicking the label focuses or toggles the input, which matters a lot for small checkboxes and radio buttons on a touch display
It automatically validates the input's value against a schema
It causes the form to submit without a submit button
It hides the input's default browser styling
explanation: The unit notes clicking the label focuses/toggles the input, which is especially useful for small checkbox/radio targets on touch displays.

question: According to Unit 5, why is an external stylesheet the standard approach for real projects?
options: Only external stylesheets can use box-sizing: border-box
It keeps panel.html readable and lets every future page in the project share the same style.css
Inline and internal CSS are not supported by modern browsers
External stylesheets apply automatically to elements without needing selectors
explanation: The unit says an external stylesheet "keeps panel.html readable and lets every future page in your project share the same style.css."

question: What problem does setting box-sizing: border-box globally solve, per Unit 5?
options: It prevents margins from collapsing between adjacent elements
It makes text inside buttons wrap correctly on small screens
It is required for the class selector to apply padding
Without it, a width:200px element with padding renders wider than 200px on screen, causing layout confusion
explanation: The unit explains that without border-box, width plus padding makes an element wider than its declared width, causing layout confusion.

question: Per Unit 5's guidance on selectors, when should you prefer a class selector over an id selector for styling?
options: IDs should be preferred for styling since they're more specific and classes are just for grouping unrelated elements
Never — inline styles should be used instead of either
Almost always — classes should be used for styling since they can be reused across similar elements, while id is reserved for JavaScript hooks
Only when styling table elements
explanation: The unit advises to "prefer classes over IDs for styling (reserve id for JavaScript hooks and reuse class freely across similar elements)."

question: According to Unit 6, what is the key difference between Flexbox and Grid?
options: Flexbox only works on button elements while Grid works on any element
Flexbox is for one-dimensional layout (a single row or column), while Grid handles two-dimensional layout (rows and columns at once)
Grid is one-dimensional and Flexbox is two-dimensional
Flexbox requires media queries to be responsive, while Grid does not
explanation: The unit describes Flexbox as "the workhorse for one-dimensional layout" and Grid as "the workhorse for two-dimensional layout."

question: Why does Unit 6 recommend position: fixed for a persistent emergency-stop button?
options: It positions the element relative to the browser viewport, so it stays glued in place regardless of how far the page is scrolled
It automatically increases the element's z-index above all other content
It disables all other buttons on the page while active
It positions the element relative to its nearest positioned ancestor only
explanation: The unit states position: fixed positions an element relative to the viewport so it "stays glued in place regardless of scrolling."

question: What does the auto-fit/minmax grid pattern (repeat(auto-fit, minmax(150px, 1fr))) let you avoid, per Unit 6?
options: It removes the need for the gap property between tiles
It avoids needing a container with display: grid at all
It can remove the need for a dedicated mobile media query on the grid, since it fits as many tiles as the container allows and wraps automatically
It replaces the need for flexbox's justify-content property entirely
explanation: The unit says this pattern "can remove the need for a dedicated mobile media query on the grid entirely" for a variable number of sensor tiles.

question: Why does Unit 7 recommend placing the <script> tag at the end of <body> (or using defer)?
options: It makes the script load faster over the network
It avoids the classic bug of JavaScript trying to grab an element that hasn't been parsed yet
It is required for addEventListener to work at all
It prevents the script from running more than once
explanation: The unit states this placement "avoids the classic bug of JavaScript trying to grab an element that hasn't been parsed yet."

question: Per Unit 7, why should var be avoided in favor of const/let?
options: var cannot hold numeric values
var is not supported in modern browsers
var automatically converts strings to numbers unexpectedly
var has confusing function-level scoping rather than block-level scoping
explanation: The unit says to avoid var because "it has confusing function-level, rather than block-level, scoping."

question: Why are arrow functions preferred for callbacks in Unit 7?
options: They are concise and, unlike the function keyword, don't rebind this
They are the only function type that can be passed to addEventListener
They automatically return JSON-stringified values
They execute synchronously while regular functions execute asynchronously
explanation: The unit states "Prefer arrow functions for callbacks — they're concise and, unlike function, don't rebind this."

question: Per Unit 8, why is no additional parsing required for a LaserScan message's ranges field once JSON.parse has decoded the incoming Rosbridge message?
options: Because Rosbridge automatically converts it into a string before sending
Because the ranges field already arrives as a plain JavaScript array
Because JavaScript treats all numbers as arrays by default
Because the range values are pre-filtered by Rosbridge before transmission
explanation: The unit states the ranges field "lands in JavaScript as a plain object whose ranges field is already an array — no parsing required."

question: Why does Unit 8 emphasize that filter, map, and reduce return a new array or value rather than mutating the original?
options: Because mutating arrays in JavaScript throws a runtime error
Because Rosbridge requires arrays to be immutable to accept new messages
So you can freely derive values like valid or closest from ranges on every incoming message without corrupting the data you just received
Because it reduces the number of WebSocket messages needed
explanation: The unit explains this non-mutating behavior matters because you're re-rendering from ranges on every message and don't want to corrupt the received data.

question: Per Unit 8, when should you prefer a classic indexed for loop over for...of or forEach?
options: Always, since for...of does not work on arrays of numbers
Never — indexed for loops are deprecated in JavaScript
Only when processing arrays longer than 100 elements
Only when you specifically need the index arithmetic — otherwise for...of/forEach are preferred as less error-prone
explanation: The unit says to prefer for...of or forEach "unless you specifically need the index arithmetic."

question: How does Unit 9 describe the difference between React's declarative approach and the imperative approach used in earlier units?
options: Declarative: you describe what an element's content should be based on state, and React updates the DOM; imperative: you manually find elements and set their content when events occur
Declarative means writing HTML directly; imperative means writing only JavaScript
Declarative approach doesn't support event handlers at all
Imperative rendering is faster but declarative rendering is required for arrays
explanation: The unit contrasts imperative "find this element and update its text" (Unit 8) with declarative "this element's text is this piece of state" (React).

question: Per Unit 9, why does calling setConnected(true) update the screen while directly assigning connected = true would not?
options: Direct assignment throws a syntax error in JSX
React only watches variables declared with let, not const
Calling the setter function is what tells React to re-render the component with the new value; direct assignment doesn't trigger a re-render
The setter function automatically calls JSON.stringify on the value
explanation: The unit states calling the setter tells React to re-render with the new value, while direct assignment would not update the screen.

question: According to Unit 9, what does npm run dev provide that the plain HTTP server from Unit 2 did not?
options: Automatic deployment to a production web host
A development server with hot-reload, showing saved changes instantly without a manual browser refresh
A built-in Rosbridge server replacing the need for rosbridge_suite
Support for serving multiple HTML files simultaneously
explanation: The unit states npm run dev starts a dev server "with hot-reload: save a file, see the change instantly without a manual browser refresh."

question: Why does Unit 10 recommend using a Fragment (<>...</>) instead of wrapping multiple returned elements in an extra <div>?
options: Fragments render faster than div elements in all browsers
JSX cannot compile multiple sibling elements at all without a Fragment
Fragments allow a component to accept more than one prop
A Fragment groups elements without adding an extra wrapper node to the DOM, avoiding unnecessary nodes that could break CSS assuming a specific parent-child structure
explanation: The unit explains a Fragment groups elements without adding a wrapper node, avoiding extra DOM nodes that could break CSS assumptions.

question: Per Unit 10, in which direction does data flow between parent and child components, and how does a child trigger behavior in its parent?
options: Props flow down from parent to child; a child triggers parent behavior by calling a function passed down to it as a prop
Props flow both ways automatically; a child can directly modify the parent's state variables
Data flows up from child to parent via props; the parent calls functions defined in the child
Components communicate only through global variables, not props
explanation: The unit states props flow down, and "there's no other direction for data to travel" except a child calling a callback function passed down as a prop.

question: Why does Unit 10 say useEffect with an empty dependency array ([]) is the right place to open a WebSocket connection?
options: It runs every time any state variable changes, ensuring the connection stays fresh
It runs once when the component mounts and its returned cleanup function runs once when the component unmounts, matching connect/disconnect lifecycle
It prevents the component from re-rendering while the socket is open
It automatically retries the connection if it fails
explanation: The unit states an empty dependency array means the effect runs once on mount with cleanup running once on unmount, matching connect/disconnect.

question: According to Unit 1, running `docker run hello-world` demonstrates which complete sequence?
options: Docker built the image locally from a Dockerfile before running it
Docker pulled the image, started a container, ran it, and let it exit
Docker attached the container to a user-defined network before running it
Docker mounted a volume to persist the hello-world output
explanation: The unit states: "If hello-world prints its welcome message, Docker pulled a tiny image, started a container from it, ran it, and let it exit — the whole lifecycle you'll be working with for the rest of this course."

question: Per the course roadmap in Unit 1, what does Unit 6 specifically cover?
options: Building ROS images and running ROS nodes in containers
Using Docker as an everyday development environment
Docker Compose and networking, for coordinating multiple containers as one system
Shipping containers to a real robot and keeping them running reliably
explanation: The roadmap explicitly lists "Unit 6: Docker Compose and networking, for coordinating multiple containers as one system."

question: In the 'Try it yourself' exercise comparing /etc/os-release inside an ubuntu:22.04 container to the host, what point is the exercise meant to illustrate?
options: Ubuntu containers cannot run on a host with a different Linux distribution
The container automatically synchronizes its OS release info with the host
The container's OS identity changes each time it is restarted
The container has a completely separate filesystem and OS identity while still sharing the host's kernel
explanation: The unit says to "Note how the container has a completely separate filesystem and OS identity even though it's sharing your machine's kernel — this is the core idea you'll build on for the rest of the course."

question: Unit 2 compares the relationship between an image and a container to which analogy?
options: A recipe and a restaurant
A class and an object
A blueprint and a finished building that cannot be replicated
A server and a client
explanation: The unit states: "This is the same relationship as a class and an object — you can start many containers from one image, and each one is isolated from the others."

question: What does Unit 2 say happens if you omit the tag when pulling an image such as `ubuntu`?
options: The pull command fails with an error requiring an explicit tag
It defaults to the tag matching your host's OS version automatically
It defaults to the `latest` tag, which is convenient for experimentation but bad for reproducibility
Docker pulls every available tag for that repository
explanation: The unit says: "Omitting the tag defaults to latest, which is convenient for experimentation but a bad idea for anything you want to be reproducible — pin a specific tag."

question: According to the flags table in Unit 2, what does `--rm` do?
options: Remove the image after the container stops
Restart the container automatically on failure
Remove all stopped containers on the host
Automatically remove the container when it exits
explanation: The flags table defines `--rm` as "automatically remove the container when it exits."

question: Why does Unit 3 recommend ordering a Dockerfile with dependency installation (e.g., pip install) before copying application source code?
options: Because Docker requires all RUN instructions to precede COPY instructions
So editing source code doesn't invalidate the cached, expensive dependency-installation layers
So the final image only contains the dependencies, not the source code
Because COPY instructions cannot appear before RUN instructions in valid Dockerfile syntax
explanation: The unit says: "Order your Dockerfile from least-to-most frequently changing: dependency installation before source code copy, so editing your Python/C++ source doesn't invalidate the expensive apt-get/pip install/colcon build layers."

question: Why does Unit 3 recommend combining `apt-get install` and the cleanup `rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*` within the same `RUN` instruction rather than separate ones?
options: Because Docker only allows one RUN instruction per Dockerfile stage
Because separate RUN instructions run in parallel and could conflict
Because cleanup in a later, separate RUN layer doesn't shrink the earlier layer where the files were created
Because apt-get requires cleanup to run in the same shell session or it fails
explanation: The unit's 'Bad' vs 'Good' example illustrates that "Bad: two layers, second one doesn't shrink the first" — cleanup must happen within the same layer to actually reduce image size.

question: What benefit does Unit 3 attribute to multi-stage builds for compiled robotics code like C++ nodes or CUDA kernels?
options: They automatically parallelize compilation across multiple CPU cores
They allow a single image to target multiple CPU architectures simultaneously
They eliminate the need for a base image in the final stage
They let you compile with a full build toolchain in one stage, then copy only the resulting binaries into a slim final image
explanation: The unit states multi-stage builds let you "compile in a 'builder' stage with all your build tools, then copy only the resulting binaries into a slim final image — very relevant for compiled robotics code... where the build toolchain is much larger than the runtime footprint."

question: How does Unit 4 distinguish `docker attach` from `docker exec`?
options: `docker attach` works only on stopped containers while `docker exec` works only on running ones
`docker attach` connects to the container's main PID 1 process (risky, since exiting can stop the container), while `docker exec` starts a new, independent process inside the container
`docker attach` creates a new container while `docker exec` reuses an existing one
`docker exec` requires the container to be run with `--rm` while `docker attach` does not
explanation: The unit says `docker attach` "connects your terminal to the container's main process (PID 1) — risky, because exiting the shell can stop the container," while `docker exec` "starts a new, independent process inside an already-running container, leaving the main process untouched."

question: Per Unit 4's exit-code table, what does exit code 137 typically indicate?
options: The process finished normally with no errors
The process received SIGTERM as a result of `docker stop`
The process received SIGKILL, frequently from an out-of-memory kill
The application itself threw an unhandled exception
explanation: The table states 137 means "Process received SIGKILL (128 + 9); frequently an out-of-memory kill — check docker inspect for OOMKilled: true."

question: Which restart policy does Unit 4 describe as a good fit for 'long-lived robot services you sometimes pause on purpose'?
options: `always`
`on-failure:N`
`no` (the default)
`unless-stopped`
explanation: The restart policy table lists `unless-stopped` as good for "long-lived robot services you sometimes pause on purpose," since it restarts like `always` but remembers a manual `docker stop`.

question: According to Unit 5, why does a container's writable layer disappearing on removal count as a 'feature, not a bug'?
options: It forces every container to use a named volume by default
It prevents any data from ever being written inside a container
It is what makes containers disposable and reproducible, so you must deliberately decide which paths should persist
It ensures containers can never share data with the host
explanation: The unit states: "This is a feature, not a bug: it's what makes containers disposable and reproducible. The lesson isn't 'avoid this behavior,' it's 'decide deliberately which paths should escape it.'"

question: What does Unit 5 give as the fix when files written by a container into a bind-mounted host directory end up owned by root?
options: Switch the bind mount to a tmpfs mount instead
Run `docker volume prune` before every container start
Always run containers with `--rm` to avoid ownership issues
Run the container with `--user $(id -u):$(id -g)` to match your host UID/GID
explanation: The unit shows the fix: "run as your own host UID/GID instead" using `docker run ... --user $(id -u):$(id -g)`.

question: Why does Unit 5 say the more explicit `--mount` syntax is preferable to `-v` once you have several mounts on one command?
options: `--mount` is required for named volumes, while `-v` only works for bind mounts
`--mount` automatically backs up data to a tar.gz file
`--mount` fails loudly if you typo a path rather than silently creating a directory
`--mount` is faster at runtime because it skips Docker's volume driver
explanation: The unit says `--mount` "is easier to read once you have several mounts on one command and fails loudly (rather than silently creating a directory) if you typo a path."

question: What does Unit 6 say `depends_on` actually controls, and what's the catch?
options: It controls readiness checks automatically, so a dependent service always waits until the other is ready
It controls which network a service joins, not the order in which it starts
It controls resource limits shared between dependent services
It controls start order, not readiness — a dependency container can be 'started' long before it's actually accepting connections
explanation: The unit states: "depends_on controls start order, not readiness — a database container can be 'started' long before it's actually accepting connections."

question: Per Unit 6, what mechanism lets containers on a user-defined bridge network resolve each other by container name?
options: A manually maintained /etc/hosts file on each container
Docker's built-in DNS on user-defined bridge networks
The `--network host` mode shared across containers
The ROS_DOMAIN_ID environment variable
explanation: The unit states: "Containers on the same user-defined bridge network can resolve each other by container name via Docker's built-in DNS — this is the mechanism Compose relies on."

question: What trade-off does Unit 6 associate with using `--network host`?
options: The container can no longer reach the outside world at all
The container must publish every port explicitly with `-p`
The container gets the host's network stack directly with no isolation
The container loses the ability to be part of a Compose stack
explanation: The unit describes `--network host` as giving "the container the host's network stack directly with no isolation — occasionally used for ROS 1/2 workloads that need multicast discovery to 'just work,' at the cost of losing network isolation."

question: How does Unit 7 distinguish the `ros-core`, `ros-base`, and `desktop` image variants?
options: `ros-core` includes GUI tools while `desktop` is the minimal middleware-only variant
All three variants contain identical packages but differ only in image tag naming
`ros-base` is for ROS 1 while `ros-core` and `desktop` are for ROS 2 only
`ros-core` is minimal (middleware and client libraries only), `ros-base` adds common build/dev tools, and `desktop` adds GUI tools like rviz
explanation: The unit states "ros-core is minimal (just the middleware and client libraries), ros-base adds common build/dev tools, and desktop adds GUI tools like rviz."

question: In the entrypoint pattern from Unit 7, why does the script end with `exec "$@"` after sourcing the ROS setup files?
options: So the container automatically restarts if the command fails
So the command passed to `docker run` (the container's CMD) executes with the ROS environment already sourced
So the entrypoint script itself becomes the container's permanent PID 1 forever
So multiple ROS distros can be sourced simultaneously without conflict
explanation: The unit explains: with the entrypoint sourcing setup files and ending in `exec "$@"`, "docker run myimage ros2 topic list runs ros2 topic list with the full ROS environment sourced first."

question: Why does Unit 7 recommend running `rosdep install` inside the Dockerfile rather than relying on the host having the right packages installed?
options: rosdep only works when invoked at container build time, never at runtime
It reduces the final image size compared to installing packages at runtime
It keeps the image self-sufficient rather than depending on the host's package setup
It's required so `colcon build` can locate the ROS installation
explanation: The unit states: "running it inside the Dockerfile keeps the image self-sufficient rather than relying on the host having the right packages installed."

question: Why does Unit 8's multi-container ROS Compose example set `network_mode: host` on every service instead of a shared user-defined bridge network?
options: Because Compose does not support user-defined networks for more than two services
Because `network_mode: host` is required to set the ROS_DOMAIN_ID environment variable
Because bridge networks don't allow containers to be named
Because ROS 2's default DDS discovery relies on UDP multicast, which Docker's default bridge network isolates from the host
explanation: The unit states: "ROS 2's default middleware discovers other nodes via UDP multicast. Docker's default bridge network isolates containers from the host's multicast traffic, which is why containers on a bridge network often can't see each other's topics."

question: What alternative to host networking does Unit 8 mention for achieving DDS discovery while keeping containers network-isolated?
options: Setting `--network host` on only one of the containers instead of all of them
A DDS configured for unicast/discovery-server mode (e.g., via `ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER`)
Using `docker attach` instead of `docker exec` to connect nodes
Increasing the `--memory` limit on each container
explanation: The unit describes "A DDS configured for unicast/discovery-server mode: some DDS implementations support a discovery server or explicit peer lists instead of multicast, which works across a bridge network... Check your DDS vendor's documentation for ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER."

question: Per Unit 8, what two things are both required to make `--gpus all` work for a CUDA workload?
options: An X11 socket mount and the DISPLAY environment variable
A named volume for GPU memory and a bridge network for driver communication
Running the container with `--privileged` and `--network host`
The NVIDIA Container Toolkit installed on the host, and matching CUDA libraries inside the image
explanation: The unit says GPU passthrough needs "the NVIDIA Container Toolkit installed on the host" and notes "Your image still needs matching CUDA libraries, but the driver comes from the host."

question: Per Unit 9, why does a dev container mount the project source as a bind mount rather than `COPY`ing it in at build time?
options: Because devcontainer.json does not support the COPY instruction at all
Because bind mounts are required for VS Code extensions to install correctly
So file changes on the host appear instantly inside the container without a rebuild, unlike a deployment image where code is baked in
Because COPY only works with named volumes, not source directories
explanation: The unit states this is "the key difference between a dev image and a deployment image: in development, you want file changes on your host to appear instantly inside the container without a rebuild; in deployment, you want the code baked in."

question: What does Unit 9 say `postCreateCommand` is for, and why isn't that setup baked into the image instead?
options: It runs every time the container starts, replacing the need for an entrypoint script
It builds the final deployment image from the dev container automatically
It installs the base ROS distro itself before any other steps
It runs once after the container is created, a good place for one-time setup like `rosdep install` that shouldn't be baked into the image
explanation: The unit states: "postCreateCommand runs once after the container is created — a good place for one-time setup like rosdep install that shouldn't be baked into the image itself."

question: According to Unit 9, what problem specifically motivates using a dev container instead of installing ROS directly on the host?
options: ROS cannot be installed on a host machine at all without Docker
Installing ROS and dependencies directly on the host can conflict with other projects or the OS's own packages
Dev containers are required to compile any C++ ROS package
Editors like VS Code refuse to open ROS projects without a devcontainer.json
explanation: The unit states: "Without dev containers, you either install ROS and every dependency directly on your host (which can conflict with other projects or your OS's own packages), or you build inside a container but edit code outside it."

question: Per Unit 10, what's the division of labor between Docker's restart policies and the systemd unit in a robot deployment?
options: systemd handles individual container crashes while Docker restart policies handle full robot reboots
They perform the identical function, providing redundant recovery for the same failures
Docker restart policies only apply during development, while systemd is used exclusively in production
Docker restart policies handle process-level recovery (a crashed node restarting), while systemd handles machine-level recovery (the whole robot rebooting)
explanation: The unit states: "Docker's own restart policies handle process-level recovery (a node crashing and restarting), while a systemd unit handles machine-level recovery (the whole robot rebooting after a power cycle)."

question: Why does Unit 10 recommend pinning a specific version tag rather than tracking `latest` on a deployed robot?
options: The `latest` tag is not supported by `docker buildx` for ARM builds
Pinned tags are required for `systemd` to recognize the image
A bad update on a robot in the field is much harder to fix than one in a data center, so rolling back to a known-good tag is preferred
`latest` tags cannot be used with `docker compose up -d`
explanation: The unit states: "prefer pulling a new version explicitly and rolling back to a known-good tag over always tracking latest — a bad update on a robot in the field is much harder to fix than one in a data center."

question: Why does Unit 10 use `docker buildx --platform linux/arm64` when developing on an x86 laptop for a robot's onboard computer?
options: To automatically shrink the image size regardless of target architecture
To cross-build (or multi-arch build) an image for the robot's ARM-based architecture without needing the target hardware in hand
To enable GPU passthrough on ARM-based Jetson boards
To convert an existing x86 image into an ARM image after it has already been built
explanation: The unit states: "docker buildx lets you build multi-architecture images, or cross-build directly for the robot's architecture, without needing the target hardware in hand."

question: According to Unit 1, what does Git actually store each time you make a commit?
options: A snapshot of the entire project's content at that point in time
Only a diff (delta) of the lines that changed since the last commit
A compressed copy of just the staged files, discarding untouched files entirely
A pointer to the previous commit with no additional data
explanation: The unit states Git 'tracks content, not files-as-blobs: it stores snapshots of your whole project each time you commit,' distinguishing it from delta-based systems.

question: Unit 1 states that Git was created by Linus Torvalds in 2005. What was it originally created to manage?
options: The source code of the Linux kernel
The GitHub hosting platform
The ROS 2 middleware stack
Torvalds' personal robotics side projects
explanation: The unit describes Git as 'a distributed version control system created by Linus Torvalds in 2005 (originally to manage the Linux kernel source).'

question: Per Unit 1, why does Git work offline without a server needing to be reachable?
options: Because it is distributed — every clone carries the entire project history, not just a snapshot
Because GitHub automatically caches every repository locally
Because git init starts a lightweight local server that mimics GitHub
Because the staging area stores enough data to reconstruct history without commits
explanation: The unit explains: '"Distributed" means every clone of a repository carries the entire project history... there is no single server that must be reachable for you to commit, branch, or inspect history. This is why Git works offline.'

question: Per Unit 2, what is the difference between `git diff` and `git diff --staged`?
options: `git diff` compares the working directory to the staging area; `git diff --staged` compares the staging area to the last commit
`git diff` compares the staging area to the last commit; `git diff --staged` compares the working directory to the staging area
Both commands show identical output; `--staged` only changes the color of the output
`git diff` only works after a commit, while `git diff --staged` works only before staging
explanation: The unit shows `git diff` as 'unstaged changes vs. the index' and `git diff --staged` as 'staged changes vs. the last commit.'

question: Why does Unit 2 recommend only amending commits that haven't been pushed or shared yet?
options: Because amending rewrites the commit, which is disruptive for anyone who already has the old version
Because `git commit --amend` deletes the previous commit's file contents permanently
Because GitHub blocks pushes of amended commits entirely
Because amending automatically force-deletes the branch it's used on
explanation: The unit warns: 'Only amend commits that haven't been pushed/shared yet — amending rewrites the commit, which is disruptive for anyone who already has the old one.'

question: According to Unit 2, what happens if you `git add` a file and then edit it further before committing?
options: The staging area still holds the older version; you must `git add` again, and `git status` shows the file as both staged and modified
Git automatically re-stages the newest edits the next time you run `git commit`
The commit will fail with an error until the working directory and staging area match exactly
The file is removed from the staging area since it no longer matches the working directory
explanation: The unit states: 'the staging area still holds the old version — you need to git add again... Git status will show the same file as both staged and modified simultaneously to warn you of this.'

question: How does Unit 3 define a Git branch?
options: A movable, named pointer to a commit
A full, independent copy of the entire project's files and history
A special file inside `.git/` that lists every commit ever made
A permanent snapshot that cannot be deleted once created
explanation: The unit states plainly: 'A branch is simply a movable, named pointer to a commit,' also noting a branch is 'a few dozen bytes, not a copy of the project.'

question: Per Unit 3, what's the difference between `git branch -d` and `git branch -D`?
options: `-d` deletes only if the branch has already been merged; `-D` force-deletes even if unmerged
`-d` renames a branch while `-D` deletes it
`-d` deletes local branches only; `-D` deletes both local and remote branches
`-d` and `-D` behave identically, but `-D` also prints a confirmation prompt
explanation: The unit lists `-d` as 'delete, but only if already merged' and `-D` as 'force-delete, even if unmerged,' recommending `-d` as a safety net.

question: According to Unit 3, what determines whether merging a branch results in a fast-forward versus a merge conflict?
options: Whether `main` has changed the same lines that the feature branch changed since it branched off
Whether the branch was created with `git switch -c` versus `git checkout -b`
Whether the branch has been pushed to a remote before merging
Whether the commit messages on the branch follow the imperative style
explanation: The unit's diagram and text hinge the outcome on 'Did main change the same lines since branching?' — no means fast-forward, yes means a conflict to resolve by hand.

question: Per Unit 4, how does the unit distinguish Git from GitHub?
options: Git is the version control tool itself; GitHub is a hosted service built around Git that adds a web UI, access control, pull requests, issue tracking, and CI
Git and GitHub are two names for the same underlying software
GitHub is the tool that performs commits and merges, while Git only stores files for backup
Git is only usable through GitHub's servers; there is no way to use Git without a GitHub account
explanation: The unit states: 'Git is the version control tool; GitHub is a hosted service built around Git that adds a web UI, access control, pull requests, issue tracking, and CI integration.'

question: What does Unit 4 say `git pull` actually does under the hood?
options: It runs `git fetch` to download new commits, then `git merge` to integrate them into your current branch
It runs `git push` in reverse to undo your last upload
It only downloads commit metadata without changing any files
It performs a `git commit --amend` on the latest remote commit
explanation: The unit explains: '`git pull` is really `git fetch` (download new commits) followed by `git merge` (integrate them into your current branch).'

question: According to Unit 4, what is the effect of writing `Fixes #42` in a commit message or pull request description?
options: It auto-closes issue #42 when the commit/PR is merged, keeping the history traceable to the discussion that motivated the change
It automatically assigns issue #42 to the commit author
It creates a brand-new issue numbered #42 if one does not already exist
It blocks the PR from merging until issue #42 is manually closed
explanation: The unit states that referencing issues this way, e.g. 'Fixes #42... auto-closes that issue on merge — this is how a project's history stays traceable back to the discussion that motivated each change.'

question: Per Unit 5, what is the distinction between the `origin` and `upstream` remotes in the fork-based contribution workflow?
options: `origin` points to your own fork, which you can push to; `upstream` points to the original repository, which you cannot push to
`origin` points to the original repository; `upstream` points to your personal fork
`origin` and `upstream` are two different names for the same remote, used interchangeably
`origin` is used only for pull requests, while `upstream` is used only for issues
explanation: The unit's example labels them: 'origin -> your fork (you can push here); upstream -> the original (you cannot push here).'

question: Why does Unit 5 recommend regularly syncing your fork's `main` with `upstream` before and during a contribution?
options: Because it minimizes merge conflicts when your pull request is eventually reviewed
Because GitHub automatically deletes forks that fall out of sync after 30 days
Because it is required before GitHub will allow you to open a pull request at all
Because syncing automatically rewrites your commit history to match upstream
explanation: The unit states: 'Doing this regularly means your feature branches are based on recent code, which minimizes merge conflicts when your PR is eventually reviewed.'

question: According to Unit 5, what kind of change is recommended for a first contribution to a project?
options: A small, genuinely useful, self-contained improvement, such as fixing a typo or a broken link
A large architectural redesign that demonstrates strong technical ability
A change that touches as many files as possible to show broad familiarity with the codebase
A change that avoids using pull requests, merging directly into upstream's main branch
explanation: The unit advises: 'Pick a small, genuinely useful, self-contained improvement... Avoid large or opinionated changes for a first PR.'

question: What does the unit say Jenkins does "at its core"?
options: It watches for a trigger and runs a defined sequence of steps on a machine you control
It compiles C++ code faster than a local machine can
It replaces the need for a version control system
It provides real-time teleoperation of robots
explanation: The unit defines Jenkins as an automation server that "watches for a trigger (a git push, a timer, a manual click) and then runs a defined sequence of steps... on a machine you control, independent of anyone's laptop."

question: Why does the unit say Jenkins remains popular despite predating modern hosted CI products like GitHub Actions and CircleCI?
options: It requires no plugins to function
It is self-hosted and plugin-extensible, so it can wire into non-cloud-native infrastructure
It was purpose-built for ROS workspaces
It is the fastest CI tool for compiling C++
explanation: The unit explains Jenkins "remains popular specifically because it is self-hosted and plugin-extensible", letting it connect to non-cloud-native infrastructure like GPU build farms and licensed simulators.

question: Per the unit's list of reasons robotics projects need CI, why does scarce hardware time matter?
options: Robots cannot legally run more than one test per day
Catching a broken launch file or bad topic remap in simulation before touching the real robot saves real time and hardware
Hardware licenses expire faster than software licenses
Jenkins agents can only run on real robot hardware
explanation: The unit states: "If Jenkins can catch a broken launch file or a bad topic remap in simulation before anyone touches the real robot, that's real time and real hardware saved."

question: According to the unit, when is a native package (apt) install of Jenkins preferable to Docker?
options: When Jenkins needs direct access to hardware/USB devices or a pre-installed toolchain on the host
When you want the fastest possible teardown and recreation
When you want to isolate Jenkins from the host's ROS toolchain versions
When you have no administrative access to the machine
explanation: The unit says native package install is "better if Jenkins needs direct access to hardware, USB devices, or a pre-installed robotics toolchain on the host itself", while Docker is the fast/isolated/reproducible default.

question: In the docker run command shown, what is port 50000 used for?
options: Serving the Jenkins web UI
SSH access into the container
The port inbound Jenkins agents connect on
Connecting to the initialAdminPassword file
explanation: The unit's notes say "-p 8080:8080 exposes the web UI, -p 50000:50000 is the port inbound Jenkins agents connect on".

question: What warning does the unit attach to mounting /var/run/docker.sock into the Jenkins container?
options: It disables the plugin installer
It gives the Jenkins process root-equivalent access to the host, risky on shared/production machines
It is required before the setup wizard will run
It prevents Jenkins from creating build agents
explanation: The unit notes mounting the Docker socket "gives the Jenkins process root-equivalent access to the host, so don't do this on a shared or production machine without understanding the tradeoff."

question: Why does this unit choose a Freestyle project for the first job instead of a Pipeline?
options: Freestyle jobs run faster on the same hardware
Pipeline jobs require plugins that aren't installed yet
It makes every concept (source, triggers, steps, post-build) visible as a distinct UI section before meeting them as code
Freestyle is required for any job that uses Docker
explanation: The unit states the Freestyle project "makes every concept... visible as a distinct UI section before you meet the same concepts as code in a Jenkinsfile later."

question: What ultimately determines whether a Freestyle build shows Success or Failed?
options: Whether the console output contains the word "error"
The exit code of the build step (e.g., the shell command)
The number of files changed in the workspace
Whether the job has a description filled in
explanation: The unit says "the build's status is driven by exit codes: a shell step that exits non-zero marks the build Failed; a zero exit code marks it Success."

question: What does the "Wipe out workspace" option do, and why would you use it?
options: It deletes the job's entire build history
It resets all Jenkins plugins to their default state
It gives a clean workspace slate, useful when persisted state might be hiding a build that only works because of leftovers
It removes the job from the Jenkins dashboard
explanation: The unit explains the workspace persists between builds, which "can also hide 'works only because of leftover state' bugs — the Wipe out workspace option gives you a clean slate when you need to be sure a build succeeds from nothing."

question: In the unit's example, what is a Choice Parameter used for?
options: A checkbox for whether to run integration tests
A free-text field for a Git branch name
A dropdown of fixed values, e.g. which ROS distro (humble/jazzy/rolling) to build against
Passing the Git commit SHA to a downstream job
explanation: The unit lists "Choice Parameter — a fixed dropdown, e.g. [humble, jazzy, rolling] for which ROS distro to build against," distinct from the String and Boolean parameter examples.

question: According to the unit, what is one benefit of chaining separate jobs (build-job, test-job, deploy-job) instead of using one long job?
options: It removes the need for source control entirely
It gives independently re-runnable stages and a clear place to look when something breaks
It automatically parallelizes every stage
It eliminates the need for parameters
explanation: The unit states chaining jobs "gives you independently re-runnable stages... It also gives you a clear place to look when something breaks, and lets different stages run on different agents."

question: What two approaches does the unit describe for passing data (like a Git SHA) between chained jobs?
options: Environment variables set manually on the agent, or a shared database
Git tags and Git branches
Archive/Copy Artifacts, or the Parameterized Trigger plugin
Editing the downstream job's Jenkinsfile at runtime
explanation: The unit lists two common approaches: "Archive the Artifacts... then Copy Artifacts from another project" or "Pass parameters to the downstream build using the... Parameterized Trigger plugin."

question: Per the unit, what question does "Authorization" answer, as distinct from "Authentication"?
options: Who are you?
What are you allowed to do once logged in?
Which plugins are installed?
How long does your session last?
explanation: The unit defines "Authorization — what are you allowed to do?" as separate from Authentication ("who are you?").

question: Which authorization strategy does the unit say you'd actually use once more than one person touches the Jenkins instance?
options: Anyone can do anything
Logged-in users can do anything
Matrix-based / Role-based Strategy
Disabling authorization checks
explanation: The unit says Matrix-based security (Role-Based Strategy) "is what you'd actually use once more than one person touches the instance," unlike the other two options which are only fine solo or as a throwaway test.

question: What does the unit say should be used instead of a user's real password for CLI/scripted access?
options: A shared team password stored in Credentials
The user's personal API Token, generated from their account's Configure page
The initialAdminPassword
An SSH key pasted directly into the Jenkinsfile
explanation: The unit says each user manages their own API Token "used for CLI and scripted access... instead of their real password."

question: Which pipeline syntax does this course use throughout, and why is it recommended as a starting point?
options: Scripted, because it is more flexible
Declarative, because it's a structured, opinionated format that's easier to read and lint
XML-based, because it integrates with job configuration files
YAML, because it's Jenkins' native format
explanation: The unit describes Declarative as "easier to read and lint, and is the recommended starting point," and states "this course uses Declarative syntax throughout."

question: In the example Jenkinsfile, what does `agent any` specify?
options: The pipeline runs on every registered agent simultaneously
The pipeline runs only inside a Docker container
The pipeline can run on any available executor
The pipeline runs only on the controller, never an agent
explanation: The unit explains "agent says where the pipeline runs (any = any available executor; you can pin to a labeled agent)."

question: Why does the unit place the Lint and Unit Tests stages inside a `parallel` block?
options: Because Jenkins requires at least two stages to run in parallel
Because they are independent stages, so running them in parallel saves wall-clock time
Because parallel stages automatically retry on failure
Because lint must complete before the build stage starts
explanation: The unit says "independent stages can run in parallel to save wall-clock time — useful when, say, linting and unit tests don't depend on each other."

question: According to the unit, what is the tradeoff of Poll SCM compared to a webhook?
options: Polling is near-instant but requires Jenkins to be publicly reachable
Polling adds latency and load but needs no network path from the Git host to Jenkins
Polling requires a GitHub-specific plugin while webhooks don't
Polling only works with Pipeline jobs, not Freestyle
explanation: The unit describes Poll SCM as "simple, needs no network path from the Git host to Jenkins, but adds latency and load," versus webhooks which are near-instant but require reachability.

question: For a personal/learning setup where Jenkins isn't publicly reachable, what does the unit recommend?
options: Disabling triggers and building manually every time
Poll SCM, as the pragmatic default
Setting up a webhook through a third-party relay
Using the Jenkins CLI to poll every second
explanation: The unit states: "For a personal/learning setup where Jenkins isn't publicly reachable, polling is the pragmatic default; in a team setting with a reachable Jenkins, webhooks are strictly better."

question: What does the unit say the GitHub Branch Source plugin (used with a Multibranch Pipeline) enables?
options: Storing SSH credentials in plaintext for convenience
Automatically discovering branches and pull requests and reporting pass/fail status checks back onto the PR
Replacing the need for a Jenkinsfile entirely
Running builds without a Git repository connected
explanation: The unit states this plugin "automatically discovers branches and open pull requests... and builds each one independently, reporting a pass/fail status check back onto the PR itself."

question: Per the unit, what makes a build "Unstable" rather than "Failed"?
options: The pipeline errored with an unhandled non-zero exit code
The build completed, but the `junit` step recorded one or more failing tests
The build was manually cancelled by a user
The workspace could not be wiped before the build started
explanation: The unit distinguishes "a Failed build (something in the pipeline itself errored...) from an Unstable build (the build completed, but the junit step recorded one or more failing tests)."

question: Why does the example stage run `sh 'pytest --junitxml=results/pytest.xml || true'` instead of letting pytest's exit code stand?
options: To always force the build to report Success regardless of test outcome
To skip running the tests entirely when pytest is slow
So a non-zero pytest exit doesn't immediately kill the pipeline, letting the junit step decide unstable vs. failed based on actual results
Because pytest cannot produce a JUnit XML file otherwise
explanation: The inline comment in the unit explains this: "don't let a nonzero pytest exit code kill the whole pipeline immediately — let the junit step below determine unstable/failed based on actual results."

question: What capability does the `junit` step give Jenkins that a raw shell exit code alone does not?
options: Automatic retry of failing tests
A searchable table of individual test cases with trend graphs across builds
Deployment of passing builds to production
Encryption of test result files
explanation: The unit says the `junit` step "turns a wall of console text into a searchable table of individual test cases, with trend graphs across builds over time."

question: What does the `-s` flag do when running `jenkins-cli.jar build`?
options: Silences all console output from the build
Skips the SCM checkout step
Blocks until the build finishes and returns the build's own result as the CLI's exit code
Runs the build only on the controller, never an agent
explanation: The unit states "the -s flag on build makes the CLI block until the build finishes and returns the build's exit code as its own exit code."

question: What does appending `/api/json` to a Jenkins URL return, per the unit?
options: A downloadable copy of jenkins-cli.jar
A structured JSON view of that page's data
A trigger to rebuild the last failed job
The plaintext contents of stored credentials
explanation: The unit notes "Appending /api/json (or /api/xml) to almost any Jenkins URL returns a structured view of that page's data."

question: Why does the unit recommend running the ROS build inside a Docker container (`agent { docker { image 'osrf/ros:jazzy-desktop' } }`) rather than relying on packages installed on the agent host?
options: It's the only way rosdep can be installed
Containers execute compiled code faster than bare metal
It gives every build the exact same OS/ROS install regardless of the underlying agent machine
It removes the need for a Test stage
explanation: The unit states this approach "gives every build the exact same OS and ROS install, regardless of what's on the underlying Jenkins agent machine — the same reproducibility argument."

question: In the launch smoke test stage, what is the purpose of combining `timeout 15s` with `sleep 5`?
options: To force the test to run for exactly 15 seconds regardless of outcome
To bound the launch and give nodes time to come up before checking the expected node is present
To replace the need for `colcon test`
To prevent `ros2 launch` from starting at all during CI
explanation: The unit says "The timeout bounds the launch, and sleep 5 gives nodes time to come up before checking that the expected node is present."

question: What does the unit suggest to speed up rebuilds with respect to `rosdep install`?
options: Always rerun rosdep install fresh on every build for safety
Disable rosdep entirely and manage dependencies manually
Skip rosdep install when package.xml manifests haven't changed since the last successful build
Cache only the Docker image, never the rosdep step
explanation: The unit suggests you can "skip rosdep install when the package manifests (package.xml) haven't changed since the last successful build (a conditional stage checking git diff...)."

question: Per the concept checklist, what should you be able to explain about a Jenkins controller vs. an agent?
options: The difference between Freestyle and Pipeline jobs
Why you might run builds on a separate agent rather than on the controller itself
What the -s flag does on jenkins-cli.jar build
What a workspace is and when to wipe it
explanation: The checklist item reads: "The difference between a Jenkins controller and an agent, and why you might run builds on a separate agent rather than the controller itself." The other options are separate checklist bullets.

question: Per the capstone requirements, where must the Jenkinsfile for the pipeline job be defined?
options: Entered directly into the Pipeline job's "Pipeline script" text box
Checked into the Git repository alongside the package it builds
Uploaded as an XML file via Manage Jenkins
Stored only in the Jenkins CLI's local cache
explanation: Capstone requirement 2 specifies "a Jenkins Pipeline job, defined via a Jenkinsfile checked into that repository."

question: According to the self-grading rubric, how do you confirm "CLI works"?
options: jenkins-cli.jar lists all jobs without throwing an error
The API token was successfully generated
`jenkins-cli.jar build <job> -s` returns a non-zero exit code when the underlying build fails
The CLI jar downloads without a checksum mismatch
explanation: The rubric table lists the pass condition for "CLI works" as: "jenkins-cli.jar build <job> -s returns a non-zero exit code when the underlying build fails."

question: According to the unit's description of the Blender/Gazebo workflow, what is the division of labor between the two tools?
options: Blender authors geometry and visuals (modeling, texturing, rigging, animation); Gazebo handles physics, sensors, and robot behavior
Blender replaces URDF/SDF entirely for defining robot behavior
Gazebo authors the meshes and Blender handles the physics simulation
Blender and Gazebo both independently simulate physics so results can be cross-validated
explanation: The unit explicitly states Blender is where you author geometry and visuals, while Gazebo is where physics, sensors, and robot behavior live -- you feed Blender-authored meshes into SDF/URDF links, not replace them.

question: Per the course roadmap in this unit, which unit introduces animation support that Gazebo Classic never had?
options: Unit 3
Unit 4
Unit 5
Unit 6
explanation: The roadmap states: "Unit 5 introduces Gazebo Sim's animation support, which Gazebo Classic never had."

question: What Blender version does the unit say the course's exporters and UI assume?
options: 2.8 or newer
3.6 or newer
4.1 or newer
5.0 or newer
explanation: The unit instructs readers to verify Blender's version explicitly, "since the exporters and UI referenced in this course assume 4.1 or newer."

question: Why does the unit emphasize placing the 3D cursor before adding new geometry?
options: It automatically defines the UV unwrap seams for the new object
It sets the position of the render camera for preview renders
It determines where new objects spawn and their default origin, which becomes the link frame origin in Gazebo
It controls which collection/layer newly added objects are placed into
explanation: The unit says the 3D cursor determines where new objects spawn and where an object's origin sits by default, and notes "a mesh's origin becomes its link frame origin in Gazebo," so it should be set deliberately before adding geometry.

question: What does the unit identify as the single most common reason a "correctly built" robot behaves strangely once dropped into a physics simulation?
options: An incorrectly assigned material
A poor UV mapping layout
The model being authored at the wrong scale (unit scale not set to Metric/1.0)
Missing bevels on hard edges
explanation: The unit states that a model authored at the wrong scale "is the single most common reason a 'correctly built' robot behaves strangely once dropped into a physics simulation (falls through the floor, explodes on contact, etc.)."

question: How does the unit contrast the Collada (.dae) and glTF export options for Gazebo?
options: OBJ is preferred by Gazebo Sim while STL is widely supported and keeps materials
glTF 2.0 is preferred by Gazebo Sim while Collada (.dae) is widely supported and keeps material references
FBX is preferred by Gazebo Sim while glTF is only used for collision geometry
STL is preferred by Gazebo Sim while OBJ keeps material references
explanation: The unit lists Collada (.dae) as "widely supported, keeps material references" and glTF 2.0 as "modern, compact, preferred by Gazebo Sim."

question: In the Principled BSDF's Roughness parameter as described in this unit, what does a value of 0 represent?
options: A fully matte surface
A mirror-like (perfectly reflective) surface
A fully metallic surface with no color
No effect on the shader output
explanation: The unit states Roughness ranges from "0 = mirror, 1 = fully matte," so 0 corresponds to a mirror-like surface.

question: Per the troubleshooting section, what does the unit say is the usual cause of textures appearing pink/magenta in Gazebo?
options: Backfacing or inverted normals on the mesh
Uneven UV island scaling during unwrap
The texture file path failing to resolve, e.g. an absolute path instead of a relative one
The Roughness value being set too high
explanation: The unit says pink/magenta textures mean "the texture file path didn't resolve -- check that image files are copied alongside the mesh and referenced with relative paths, not absolute paths."

question: What fix does the unit recommend for a mesh showing dark or missing faces due to backfacing/inverted normals?
options: Re-unwrap the UVs using Cylinder Project
Select the mesh and use Shift+N (Recalculate Normals) in Edit Mode
Lower the Roughness value to 0 in the material
Switch the Base Color input from an image texture to a constant value
explanation: The unit states that backfacing/inverted normals show up as dark or missing faces and that "Shift+N (Recalculate Normals) in Edit Mode fixes most cases."

question: According to the unit, where must a link's origin be placed before exporting it, and why?
options: At the mesh's geometric center, to keep scaling calculations simple
Exactly at the joint axis connecting the link to its parent, so the joint rotates around the correct point
At the world origin (0,0,0) regardless of the link's actual position
At the link's center of mass, to simplify inertia tensor calculations
explanation: The unit calls this "the critical modeling discipline": each link's origin must be placed exactly at the joint axis connecting it to its parent, not the mesh's geometric center -- "Get this wrong and every joint will rotate around the wrong point."

question: Why does the unit recommend using a simplified primitive (like a cylinder) rather than the detailed visual mesh for a link's collision geometry?
options: Detailed meshes slow down Blender's own viewport rendering
It's far cheaper for the physics engine and avoids concave-mesh collision instability
Simplified shapes look better under Gazebo's default lighting
SDF does not support mesh files inside a <collision> element
explanation: The unit notes that using simplified primitives for collision "is standard practice; it's far cheaper for the physics engine and avoids concave-mesh collision instability," even though the example still uses a mesh for the visual element.

question: What joint type does the unit use for the mobile base's wheel joints, and what reason does it give?
options: Revolute, because it limits rotation to a safe range for the wheel
Prismatic, because the wheels slide along a track rather than spin
Continuous, because the wheels need unlimited rotation
Fixed, because the wheels are rigidly bolted to the chassis
explanation: The unit describes the differential-drive base as needing "wheel links connected by continuous (unlimited rotation) joints," shown in the left_wheel_joint/right_wheel_joint example.

question: How does the unit describe the key difference between Gazebo Classic and Gazebo Sim regarding animation?
options: Classic supports skeletal mesh animation natively, while Sim requires external plugins
Classic has no native mesh-animation feature and fakes motion via plugins repositioning objects each timestep, while Sim added a native <actor> element for skeletal playback
Both versions support identical native <actor>-based animation
Sim only accepts animations authored in Maya, never from Blender
explanation: The unit states that in Classic "you fake motion with plugins that reposition objects each timestep," while "Gazebo Sim added an <actor> element to SDF that references an animated mesh... and plays it back."

question: When parenting a mesh to an armature with "Automatic Weights" in Blender, what does the unit say about the result?
options: It is a guaranteed final weighting that needs no further checking
It's a starting point for weight painting, and the result should still be checked
It automatically generates all the keyframes for the animation
It bakes procedural textures directly onto the mesh
explanation: The unit says: "Check the weight paint result -- automatic weighting is a starting point, not a guarantee."

question: What common symptom does the unit describe when verifying an animated actor's playback in Gazebo Sim?
options: The actor's mesh disappears entirely from the scene
The whole simulation crashes on load
The animation sits frozen at frame zero, often due to an unbaked export or a mismatched animation filename
The actor's textures render as pink/magenta
explanation: The unit warns to confirm the animation is "actually driving the actor rather than sitting frozen at frame zero -- a common symptom of an export that didn't bake the action, or an SDF <animation> filename that doesn't match the skin."

question: Why does the unit recommend adding the animated actor last in the final project's build order?
options: Because it requires the robot's joint controllers to already be fully tuned
Because it's independent of the physics/robot pipeline and is easiest to debug in isolation
Because Gazebo Sim only allows adding an <actor> after the world file is saved once
Because texturing the actor must wait until the whole environment is finalized
explanation: The build order says to add the animated actor last "since it's independent of the physics/robot pipeline and easiest to debug in isolation."

question: Per the integration checklist, what does it likely mean if the robot visibly jitters or sinks into the ground on contact?
options: A texture file path failed to resolve
The UV unwrap has overlapping islands
A collision-geometry or mass/inertia problem
The animated actor's export wasn't baked
explanation: The checklist states the robot "doesn't visibly jitter or sink on contact with the ground (a collision-geometry or mass/inertia problem if it does)."

question: What does the "clean-room test" described in this unit's Try It Yourself section ask you to verify?
options: That every joint responds correctly to a velocity command
That the world launches correctly from a fresh Gazebo instance using only the files in the project folder, with no leftover state from earlier experiments
That textures still look correct under different lighting angles
That gz stats reports a real-time factor above 1.0
explanation: The exercise instructs: "close Gazebo entirely, reopen it, and launch your world from scratch using only the files in that folder -- no leftover state from earlier experiments. Fix anything that only worked because of an artifact left over from development."

question: According to the unit, when migrating a URDF from a Gazebo Sim + ROS 2 project to Gazebo Classic + ROS 1, what happens to the core `<robot>` block (links, joints, visuals, collisions)?
options: It must be entirely rewritten because SDF versions differ between the two simulators
It is largely portable and stays untouched; only the `<gazebo>` extension tags need rewriting because those configure simulator-specific plugins
It must first be converted from URDF into native SDF before Gazebo Classic will accept it
Only the joint names need to be updated to match Gazebo Classic's naming convention
explanation: The unit states the URDF `<robot>` block itself is 'largely portable' and that links, joints, visuals, and collisions don't change between simulators — what changes is everything inside `<gazebo>` extension tags, since those configure simulator-specific plugins and sensors.

question: The unit warns that a wheel friction value (e.g. `<mu1>`, `<mu2>`) that 'worked' in Gazebo Sim may behave differently after porting to Gazebo Classic. What reason does it give for this?
options: Gazebo Classic uses the Bullet physics engine by default instead of ODE, changing how friction is computed
Friction and contact parameter names have shifted across SDF versions, so a value can silently default to something else in Classic
Gazebo Classic ignores `<mu1>` and `<mu2>` tags entirely and requires a separate friction plugin
PBR materials from Gazebo Sim override any friction coefficients defined in the `<gazebo>` block
explanation: The unit explicitly says to check the SDF version the Gazebo Sim world was authored against versus what Classic ships with, because 'friction and contact parameter names have shifted across SDF versions, so a value that worked in Sim may silently default to something else in Classic.'

question: Per the ROS plugin mapping table in the unit, what is the Gazebo Classic (`gazebo_ros_pkgs`) equivalent of the Gazebo Sim differential-drive plugin `gz-sim-diff-drive-system`?
options: libgazebo_ros_joint_state_publisher.so
libgazebo_ros_camera.so
libgazebo_ros_diff_drive.so
libgazebo_ros_imu_sensor.so
